  **About the Author** 作者简介

Richard G. A. Buxton is a classical scholar specialising in the study of ancient Greek mythology and literature. His many books include *Imaginary Greece: the Contexts of Mythology*.

> 理查德·G·A·布克顿是一位专长于研究古代希腊神话与文学的古典学者。他的著作包括《想象中的希腊：神话的语境》。

# 1 <span id="page-9-0"></span>*Introduction: Myths in Context 
>引言：神话的语境

<span id="page-9-1"></span>![](_page_9_Picture_1.jpeg)

*Few monsters overcome by Herakles are more horrifying than Kerberos, the hound of the Underworld. The serpents growing from the beast's heads and paws intensify the terror. In this vase-painting (6th century BC) Eurystheus, who had commanded Herakles to perform his Labours, is nervously hiding in a big jar.*

> 赫拉克勒斯所战胜的怪物中，没有什么比刻耳柏洛斯——冥界的猎犬——更令人恐惧了。这头野兽的头和爪子上生长出的蛇使恐怖更加加剧。在这幅陶器彩绘（公元前六世纪）中，曾命令赫拉克勒斯完成十二项苦役的欧律斯透斯正紧张地躲藏在一只大陶罐中。

No group of stories exhibits greater richness and depth, and certainly none has been more influential, than the set of tales which we know as 'the Greek myths'. Narrative texts and visual images recording the exploits of the Greek gods and goddesses, and of the heroines and heroes, are already found early in the 1st millennium BC, and they have continued to be produced, in an apparently inexhaustible series of variations and reinterpretations, until the present day. The meanings which people have read into these stories have varied enormously over time, according to the contrasting perspectives from which different cultures have viewed the legacy of ancient Greece; indeed this chameleon-like capacity for adaptation has been a decisive factor in the myths' survival. Nor does the impact of the Greek myths show any sign of diminishing: the tireless efforts of Herakles to rid the world of monsters, as depicted on countless thousands of ancient vasepaintings, still find their echo in the latest films and software for interactive computer games.

> 没有哪一组故事比希腊神话展现出了更丰富的内涵和更深的深度，也没有哪一组故事比它更具影响力。我们所知的“希腊神话”这一系列故事，早在公元前第一个千年早期就已经出现了叙述性文本和视觉图像，记录着希腊诸神与女神、英雄与女英雄的事迹。这些故事此后一直不断被以各种形式和重新诠释的方式创作出来，直到今天。人们从这些故事中读出的含义随着时间推移而发生了巨大的变化，这取决于不同文化从不同视角对古希腊遗产的解读；事实上，这种类似变色龙般的适应能力，正是神话得以流传的关键因素。而希腊神话的影响也丝毫没有减弱的迹象：赫拉克勒斯那永不疲倦消灭怪物的传说
> 正如在无数古代陶器绘画中所描绘的那样，至今仍回响在最新的电影和互动电脑游戏软件中。

The final chapter of this book explores some of the ways in which the Greek myths have been retold since antiquity. But before that the main focus will be on providing a detailed account of the myths as they were narrated within the contexts of ancient Greek culture. Quotations from Greek literary texts and reproductions of Greek artistic images therefore play a major part in the analysis. So also do illustrations of the Greek landscape, which was another crucial aspect of the world of Greek mythology. Many of the locations within which mythological episodes were said to have taken place – Mycenae, Delphi, Tiryns, Ithaca, the Athenian Acropolis – may still be visited today. Moreover, several types of landscape – mountain, river, cave, sea – played their own part in shaping the myths. In presenting the world of Greek mythology to the reader we shall frequently evoke the topographical contexts within which that mythology developed.

> 本书的最后一章探讨了自古至今希腊神话被重新讲述的各种方式。但在那之前，重点将放在对神话的详细叙述上，这些叙述源于古代希腊文化的具体背景。因此，引用希腊文学文本和再现希腊艺术图像在分析中起着重要作用。同样，希腊地理景观的图示也扮演了关键角色，因为这是希腊神话世界的重要组成部分。许多神话故事被传说是发生在此地——如迈锡尼、德尔斐、提尔辛、伊萨卡以及雅典卫城——这些地方今天仍然可以参观。此外，多种自然景观——如山地、河流、洞穴和海洋——也在塑造神话方面发挥了各自的作用。在向读者呈现希腊神话世界时，我们将频繁地唤起那些塑造该神话的地理环境背景。

It is important to stress this objective of setting out the Greek myths in their Greek contexts. There is no shortage of dictionaries and handbooks of 'Classical mythology' – by which is meant the myths of Greece *and Rome*. It is true that there were significant continuities between the stories told in these two ancient cultures, but there were also major differences. Roman myth-telling is a fascinating topic in its own right, but it is not the main topic which will be addressed in the present volume (though see [pp. 216](#page-469-1)[–24](#page-272-1), 'How Rome reimagined Greece'): it is myth-telling by Greeks, and predominantly in the Greek language, which will be our central concern.

> 需要强调的是，将希腊神话置于其希腊文化背景中进行阐述这一目标的重要性。关于“古典神话”的词典和手册并不少见——这里的“古典神话”指的是希腊和罗马的神话。确实，这两个古代文化所讲述的故事之间存在显著的连续性，但也存在重大的差异。罗马的神话叙述本身就是一个引人入胜的话题（尽管参见第216–24页，“罗马如何重新想象希腊”），但本书所要探讨的主要话题并非罗马神话，而是希腊人的神话叙述，尤其是以希腊语为主要语言的叙述，这将是本书的核心内容。

This Hellenic emphasis has relevance also for the form in which the names of mythological characters and places appear in this book. Until quite recently, scholars followed the convention of automatically Latinizing mythological names, even when everything about the context was Greek: not only would Kadmos (which is a literal transliteration from the Greek) appear as Cadmus, and Daidalos as Daedalus, but Zeus might even be referred to under the guise of his (partial) Roman 'equivalent' Jupiter, and Athene as Minerva. Unfortunately, there is no right answer to the problem of transliteration. It would seem over-fussy to call Athens by its ancient Greek name of 'Athenai', or Mycenae 'Mukenai'; so, where a commonly understood version of a place or a character is involved, that is the form which is adopted (thus the Latin form 'Oedipus' is preferred to the more puristically correct Greek form 'Oidipous'). In the case of relatively less well-known names, however, a more 'Hellenic' form is preferred (so 'Kreon', not 'Creon').

> 这种希腊文化强调也适用于本书中神话人物和地名的呈现形式。直到最近，学者们通常遵循将神话名称拉丁化的惯例，即使整个语境都是希腊的：不仅像卡德摩斯（Kadmos，这是从希腊语直接音译的）会写成卡德摩斯（Cadmus），达伊达罗斯（Daidalos）会写成达伊达罗斯（Daedalus），甚至连宙斯（Zeus）有时也会以他的（部分）罗马“对应者”朱庇特（Jupiter）之名来称呼，雅典娜（Athene）则会以智慧女神密涅瓦（Minerva）之名出现。不幸的是，音译问题并没有绝对正确的答案。似乎将雅典称为其古希腊名称“雅典娜”（Athenai）或迈锡尼称为“穆肯娜”（Mycenae）显得过于讲究；因此，当涉及到一个地点或人物普遍被理解的版本时，就会采用这种形式（因此，拉丁形式“俄狄普斯”（Oedipus）被优先采用，而不是更纯粹正确的希腊形式“奥伊狄普斯”（Oidipous））。在相对较少广为人知的名字，然而更倾向于采用“希腊化”的形式（因此，应使用“Kreon”而非“Creon”）。

<span id="page-11-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_11_Picture_1.jpeg)
*Delphi: the location of Apollo's oracle, which plays a role in many Greek myths.*
> 德尔斐是阿波罗神谕的所在地，在许多希腊神话中起重要作用。

The plan of the book is straightforward. The [first chapter,](#page-18-1) after posing the initial question of 'What is a myth?', investigates the general characteristics of Greek mythology, looking in particular at the kind of evidence we can draw on and – in keeping with one of our main emphases – the contexts within which Greeks told each other myths. The [second chapter](#page-85-1) describes one particular

> 本书的结构非常清晰。第一章在提出“什么是神话？”这一初始问题后，探讨了希腊神话的一般特征，特别关注我们可以借鉴的证据类型——同时遵循我们主要强调的一个重点——希腊人讲述神话的具体语境。第二章则描述了一个特定的案例。

category of tale:  故事类别：

'How things began'. The discussion deals with myths about the beginning of the universe, about the birth of the gods, and about the origins of humanity and its various communities. 
 >“万物起源”。本节讨论关于宇宙起源、诸神诞生以及人类及其各族群起源的神话。

[Chapter III](#page-133-1) analyses accounts of the powers and attributes of the Olympian gods. 
> 第三章分析了关于奥林匹斯诸神权能与特质的叙

[Chapter IV](#page-221-1) is devoted to some of the best-known tales of Greek heroism, including the legendary expeditions of the Greeks to Troy and of the Argonauts to capture the Golden Fleece. 
> 第四章则探讨了一些广为人知的希腊英雄故事，包括希腊人远征特洛伊以及阿尔戈船英雄远征获取金羊毛的传奇事迹。

[Chapter V](#page-328-1) once more concentrates on hero(in)es, exploring the strange and often terrifying events which befall these characters in their family relationships.
> 第五章再次聚焦于英雄人物，探讨这些人物在家庭关系中所遭遇的奇特而常常令人恐惧的事件。

[Chapter VI](#page-399-1) examines a topic which will have been implicit throughout: the landscape within which Greek mythical narratives were imagined as having occurred. 
> 第六章审视了一个自始至终隐含其中的主题：希腊神话叙事被想象为发生的背景环境。最后，

Finally, [Chapter VII](#page-467-1) considers the fascination which Greek myths have exercised since antiquity, for, amongst others, artists, musicians, poets and scholars, not forgetting the impact of Greek mythology on other cultural media such as film and television. This fascination is as intense as it has ever been.
> 第七章考虑了自古以来希腊神话对艺术家、音乐家、诗人和学者等群体所产生的吸引力，以及希腊神话对诸如电影和电视等其他文化媒介所产生的影响。这种吸引力至今依然强烈。

#  <span id="page-13-0"></span>*[Chronology of Greek Myth-telling](#page-6-1)*
>  希腊神话叙述的时间线

The following dating conventions are widely observed:
> 以下的纪年惯例普遍采用：

The Archaic period: 700–500 BC
> 古风时期：公元前700年–公元前500年

The Classical period: 500–323 BC
> 古典时期：公元前500年–公元前323年

The Hellenistic period: 323–31 BC
> 希腊化时期：公元前323年—前31年

| date             | author/artist | key moments in verbal and artistic myth-telling            |
|------------------|---------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| 900–700<br>BC    |               | 'Geometric' pottery                                        |
| ?8/7th           | Homer         | many epic poems were ascribed to him, incl.                |
| cent.            |               | the Iliad and Odyssey; also Homeric Hymns                  |
| ?8/7th           | Hesiod        | poems incl. Theogony and Works and Days                    |
| cent.            |               |                                                            |
| 625–475          |               | Athenian black-figure vase-painting                        |
| c. 570           |               | 'François Vase' (see p. 25)                                |
| c. 555–c.<br>465 | Simonides     | lyric poems                                                |
| 530–300          |               | Athenian red-figure vase-painting                          |
| c. 518–          | Pindar        | lyric poems, esp. choral odes celebrating athletic         |
| after 446        |               | victories                                                  |
| c. 525–c.<br>456 | Aischylos     | tragedies, incl. Oresteia trilogy                          |
| 490s–c.<br>406   | Sophokles     | tragedies, incl. Oedipus Tyrannos, Antigone and<br>Elektra |
| 480s–406         | Euripides     | tragedies, incl. Bacchae and Medea                         |
| c. 450–<br>380s  | Aristophanes  | comedies, incl. some involving mythological<br>themes      |
| c. 470           |               | Temple of Zeus at Olympia                                  |
| 450              |               | beginnings of S. Italian red-figure vase-painting          |
| mid-5th<br>cent. |               | white-ground vases known as lekythoi                       |
| c. 447–c.<br>430 |               | building of Parthenon at Athens                            |
| c. 428–c.<br>348 | Plato         | philosophical works, incl. Phaedo and Republic             |
| 380              |               | Temple of Asklepios at Epidauros                           |

|日期             | 作者/艺术家 | 口述与艺术叙事中的关键时刻                             |
|------------------|------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| 公元前900–700年  |            | 几何纹陶器                                             |

<span id="page-15-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_15_Picture_0.jpeg)
*Mycenae was said to have been the home of Agamemnon, conqueror of Troy. The city walls give an idea of the military and political might exerted by this ancient centre of power.*
> 迈锡尼据说曾是阿伽门农的故乡，特洛伊的征服者。这座城市城墙展示了这一古代权力中心所展现出的军事和政治力量。

<span id="page-16-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_16_Picture_0.jpeg)
*The Athenian Acropolis is dominated today, as it was in antiquity, by Athene's great temple, the Parthenon.*
> 雅典卫城现在仍由雅典娜的巨神庙——帕特农神庙主导，正如古代所见。

A final word may be added about the scope and title of the book. One of the themes upon which we shall insist is the diversity of Greek myths. There are innumerable variations: between authors, between genres, between historical periods, between localities. Does this mean that the notion of 'completeness', which the title implies is the book's aspiration, must remain an unattainable ideal? If to be 'complete' means to chronicle every single variant of every single myth, then the answer has to be 'yes' – though this may be no great loss, since such an enterprise, if carried out to the letter, would drown the reader in a flood of detail. But what we can do is to offer a comprehensive picture of the *world* of Greek mythology – the imaginative contours and horizons, the motifs and recurrent concerns, which lent meaning to the stories. That attainable and worthwhile objective will be the goal of the pages which follow.

> 关于本书的范围和书名，或许还可以补充一句。我们将重点强调的主题之一是希腊神话的多样性。存在无数的变体：在不同作者之间、不同体裁之间、不同历史时期之间以及不同地区之间。这是否意味着，书名所暗示的本书追求的“完整性”概念，必须成为一个难以实现的理想？如果“完整性”意味着要记录每一个神话的每一个变体，那么答案只能是“是”——尽管这可能并非什么大损失，因为如果严格按照这个标准执行，将会让读者陷入大量细节的洪流中。但我们可以做到的是提供一个关于希腊神话世界的全面图景——包括其想象的轮廓和视野，以及赋予故事意义的主题和反复出现的关注点。这一可实现且值得追求的目标，将是接下来的篇章所追求的目标。

![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_17_Figure_0.jpeg)
#  <span id="page-18-0"></span>[I Contexts, Sources, Meanings](#page-6-2)
>  I 语境、来源与含义

<span id="page-18-2"></span><span id="page-18-1"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_18_Picture_1.jpeg)

> 赫拉克勒斯，奥林匹斯山，泰坦

*Priam comes to Achilles' tent to ransom the body of his son Hektor, whom Achilles has slain in revenge for the death of his friend Patroklos. Achilles is eating a meal, with a knife in one hand and a piece of meat in the other; he has not yet noticed Priam. The focal point of the scene – Hektor's corpse – lies disregarded beneath the couch. Attic cup,* c. *490 BC*.

> 帕里斯来到阿喀琉斯的帐篷中，试图用赎金换回他儿子赫克托耳的遗体，赫克托耳正是被阿喀琉斯为报复好友帕特罗克洛斯之死而杀死的。阿喀琉斯正在用餐，一只手握着刀，另一只手拿着一块肉；他尚未察觉帕里斯的到来。场景的焦点——赫克托耳的尸体——被忽视地躺在沙发下方。雅典陶杯，约公元前490年。

## 1 *The Remaking of Tradition* 传统重塑

We often speak, by a kind of shorthand, of 'the myth of…' – 'the myth of Oedipus', for example, or 'the myth of the Trojan War'. But in fact one of the central characteristics of the stories told by the Greeks was plurality. There was no single, canonical, orthodox version of a given tale which all its tellers had to repeat. Rather, each teller remade tradition according to the requirements of the particular social and artistic context. Of course there were overlaps between stories: many of the extant versions of, say, the story of Jason and the Golden Fleece have much in common with each other. But there was always the possibility for invention and – within the intangible limits of tradition – originality.

> 我们经常以一种简略的方式提及“……的神话”——例如“俄狄浦斯的神话”或“特洛伊战争的神话”。但实际上希腊人讲述的故事的一个中心特征是其多样性。对于每一个故事而言，并不存在一个单一的、权威的、正统的版本，所有讲述者都必须重复这个版本。相反，每位讲述者都会根据特定的社会和艺术背景的要求，重构传统。当然，故事之间也存在重叠：例如，关于伊阿宋和金羊毛的故事的现存版本之间有许多共同之处。但始终存在着创新的可能——在传统无形的界限之内，也存在着原创性。

In this first chapter we shall describe some of the features of this narrative plurality. We shall set out the various and extremely diverse sources which have preserved the Greek myths, and we shall examine the different contexts, both domestic and public, in which myths were retold, whether orally, or in writing, or in visual representation.

> 在本章的第一部分，我们将描述这一叙述多样性的若干特征。我们将阐述保存希腊神话的各种极其多样化的来源，并考察神话在不同背景下被重新讲述的情况，包括家庭和公共领域，无论这些讲述是口头进行的，还是以文字形式或视觉形式呈现的。

## 2 <span id="page-20-0"></span>*[General Characteristics of Greek Myths](#page-6-3)*
> 3.2 希腊神话的一般特征

### 2.1 *What is a Myth?* 什么是神话？

One widespread usage of the word 'myth' is exemplified by a sentence such as this: 'It's a myth that all English people drink tea at four o'clock in the afternoon.' A myth, on this definition, is just 'a widely held misconception'. However, such a simplistic definition, which defines a myth in terms of its falsity, does nothing to alert us to the imaginative richness and social significance of stories and story-telling. A different and more useful kind of definition – the kind with which we shall be operating here – goes like this: 'A myth is a socially powerful traditional story.'

> “神话”一词的普遍用法可由这样的句子来体现：“所有英国人都在下午四点喝茶”是一种神话。按照这种定义，神话仅仅是“一种普遍存在的误解”。然而，这种仅以虚假性来定义神话的简单化定义，并未能提醒我们注意故事和讲故事所蕴含的丰富的想象力及其社会意义。另一种更为有用且更准确的定义——我们在此将采用的定义——是这样表述的：“神话是一种具有社会影响力的传统故事。”

There are three elements in this definition. The least problematic is the notion of *story*: a 'myth' is a narrative, a set of events structured into a sequence. The second element is *tradition*: the stories we shall be concentrating on were transmitted from teller to teller, and often from generation to generation; indeed, one way of characterizing a myth might be that it is a story whose origin has been forgotten. Thirdly, there is *social power*: our focus will be on narratives which occupy a highly significant position within the societies that retell them, in that they embody and explore the values, not just of individuals, but of social groups, and even whole communities.

> 这个定义包含三个要素。最不具争议性的是故事的概念：一个“神话”是一种叙述，是一组被组织成序列的事件。第二个要素是传统：我们将重点研究的故事是通过讲述者一代代传递下来的；事实上，可以将神话定义为一种其起源已被遗忘的故事。第三，还有社会权力：我们的关注点将放在那些在重新讲述它们的社会中占据重要地位的叙事上，因为它们不仅体现了个人的价值观，还体现了社会群体乃至整个社区的价值观。

In the light of this definition, we can see that myths – socially powerful traditional stories – have played and continue to play a focal part in the imaginative lives of very many societies. In modern Ireland, the legendary hero Cuchulain has been appropriated as an exemplar by adherents of both of the main, opposed, religious traditions, Catholic and Protestant. In the Balkans, the Battle of Kosovo – at which, in 1389, a Serbian force was said to have suffered a catastrophic defeat at the hands of an army of Ottoman Turks –has achieved mythical status for those who long to rediscover a Serbian nationhood. In a quite different register, but still exercising its own fascination, is the tale of Frankenstein's monster: thanks especially to diverse re-inventions in the cinema, this story-turned-myth explores one of the central anxieties of modern consciousness: how to deal with the capacity of technology to redefine life itself. The story of Frankenstein invites its readers or viewers to ask: what would the implications be if we could really create human life? Such a story is no longer just a story. Having become 'traditional', it belongs alongside such older tales as those of Robin Hood (what would it be like if the social order were inverted?) or, differently again, alongside the latest but already 'traditional' narratives about Superman. Myths are alive and well.

> 根据这一定义，我们可以看出，神话——这些社会上有影响力的传统故事——一直在许多社会的想象生活中扮演着核心角色，并且至今仍然如此。在现代爱尔兰，传奇英雄库丘林（Cuchulain）被天主教和新教两大对立宗教传统追随者分别用作典范。在巴尔干地区，1389年塞尔维亚军队据说在与奥斯曼土耳其军队的战斗中遭遇了毁灭性失败的“科索沃战役”，对于那些渴望重新发现塞尔维亚民族认同的人来说，这场战役已获得神话般的地位。在完全不同的语境中，但同样充满魅力的是弗兰肯斯坦怪物的故事：特别是由于电影中多样化的再创作，这个被转化为神话的故事探讨了现代意识中一个核心焦虑：如何应对技术重新定义生命本身的潜力。弗兰肯斯坦的故事邀请读者或观众思考：如果我们真的能够创造人类生命，其后果会是什么？这样的故事已不再仅仅是一个故事。它已成为“传统”的一部分，与罗宾汉（如果社会秩序被倒置会是什么样子？）或超人（以另一种方式）等更古老的传说并列。神话依然鲜活。

<span id="page-22-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_22_Picture_0.jpeg)

*Frankenstein's monster contemplates his features in a pool: an engraving by Lynd Ward, from a 1934 edition of Mary Shelley's tale of terror*, Frankenstein, or The Modern Prometheus, *1818. Thanks to its exploration of such themes as the creation of life and the dangers of excessive human ingenuity, this story has earned the right to be called a 'modern myth'.*

> 弗兰肯斯坦怪物在池塘中凝视自己的面容：林德·沃德的版画，出自1934年版的玛丽·雪莱的恐怖小说《弗兰肯斯坦，或现代普罗米修斯》（1818年首版）。由于其对诸如创造生命和人类过度智慧的危险等主题的探讨，这部作品理所应当地被称为“现代神话”。

<span id="page-23-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_23_Picture_0.jpeg)

*The legend of the ancient Celtic warrior Cuchulain is evoked in Oliver Sheppard's statue of the dying hero, located in Dublin at the site of the 1916 Easter Rising. Cuchulain's superhuman exploits, culminating in his early death, have been likened to those of Achilles.*

> 奥利弗·谢普德创作的雕像描绘了垂死的英雄库丘利安，这座雕像位于都柏林1916年复活节起义的发生地，唤起了这位古代凯尔特战士的传说。库丘利安超凡的战绩，最终导致他早逝，被比作阿喀琉斯的英勇事迹。

### 2.2 **Origins** 起源

No civilization has generated a richer or more abundant crop of myths than that of ancient Greece. The first myths for which we have direct evidence are found in texts of the poets Homer and Hesiod, usually dated to the 8th/7th centuries BC. However, traditions of myth-telling in Greece undoubtedly go back far beyond that date. Many aspects of the style of Homeric and Hesiodic poetry, especially the use of repeated 'formulaic' phrases (e.g. 'the wine-dark sea', 'Zeus the gatherer of clouds'), suggest that a long tradition of orally composed verse lies behind the poetry which we actually possess; moreover, the poems often allude briefly to mythical episodes with which the audience was evidently assumed to be already familiar. How far back we should push 'the origins' of Greek mythology is a question which is unanswerable in practice and, anyway, fatally flawed in principle – it is quite uncertain what such an alleged 'origin' is supposed to look like, and how we should know if we had found it. What we can assert with confidence, though, is that the myths which have survived from later Greece will have been shaped in a variety of earlier cultural contexts.

> 没有哪个文明产生过比古希腊更丰富、更繁多的神话。我们目前可以直接证明的最早神话见于荷马与赫西俄德诗人的作品文本，通常被定为公元前8至7世纪。然而，希腊的神话讲述传统无疑可以追溯到更遥远的年代。荷马与赫西俄德诗歌风格的许多方面，尤其是反复使用的“程式化”短语（例如“酒色的海”、“云之聚集者宙斯”），表明我们实际拥有的诗歌背后存在着一个长期的口头创作传统；此外，这些诗作经常简要地提及一些神话事件，而这些事件显然被观众所熟知。关于希腊神话的“起源”应追溯到多远，实际上无法回答，而且在原则上也存在致命缺陷——我们完全无法确定所谓的“起源”应该是什么样子，以及如何判断我们是否已经找到了它。不过，我们可以确信的是，后来希腊流传下来的神话是在多种更早的文化背景下形成的。

One such context is the late 2nd millennium BC civilization which modern archaeologists have labelled 'Mycenaean', adopting the name of one of that civilization's most remarkable sites. Many Mycenaean population-centres whose political importance subsequently declined – Orchomenos in Boiotia, and Tiryns and Mycenae itself in the Argolid – figure prominently in Greek myths; this strongly suggests a formative period of Mycenaean influence on the stories, even if – because of the absence of relevant written Mycenaean evidence – we cannot demonstrate the point conclusively. Another formative influence on the development of Greek myths may have been another 2nd millennium culture, the so-called 'Minoan' civilization of Crete (the name 'Minoan', now automatically applied by modern archaeologists, has its sole basis in mythology: Minos was a mythical king of Crete). Once more, lack of direct evidence prevents us from reaching definite conclusions about the possible role played by Minoan Crete in the development of Greek myths, but we cannot fail to register the island's prominence in a number of mythological episodes: the Minotaur in the labyrinth [\(p. 128](#page-280-0)), the tale of Daidalos and Ikaros ([pp. 92–93](#page-193-0)), the almost invulnerable bronze giant Talos ([pp. 112–13\)](#page-243-0), and many others.

> 其中一个背景是公元前第二个千年末期的文明，现代考古学家将其称为“迈锡尼文明”，这个名称源自该文明最著名的遗址之一。许多迈锡尼文明的中心城市，其政治重要性后来逐渐下降，如位于荷马地区的奥尔霍迈诺斯，以及位于阿尔戈斯地区的特里尼和迈锡尼本身，在希腊神话中占据重要地位；这强烈表明迈锡尼文明对这些故事的形成产生了影响，尽管由于缺乏相关的迈锡尼文字证据，我们无法确凿地证明这一点。另一个可能影响希腊神话形成发展的因素，可能是另一个公元前第二个千年的文化——所谓的“米诺斯”文明，即克里特岛的文明（“米诺斯”这一名称如今被现代考古学家自动使用，其唯一依据来自神话：米诺斯是传说中克里特岛的一位国王）。同样，缺乏直接证据使我们无法确定米诺斯克里特岛在希腊神话形成过程中可能扮演的角色，但我们无法忽视该岛在许多神话情节中的突出地位：如迷宫中的米诺陶洛斯（第128页），达伊达罗斯与伊卡洛斯的故事（第92–93页），几乎不可摧毁的青铜巨人塔洛斯（第112–13页），以及其他许多例子。

<span id="page-25-1"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_25_Picture_0.jpeg)
*The ruins of Mycenae in the northeastern Peloponnese are an inspiring reminder of the power and influence the great city once wielded. Mycenae features prominently in Greek myths: traditionally, it was the home of Agamemnon, conqueror of Troy.*

> 伯罗奔尼撒半岛东北部的迈锡尼遗址是昔日这座伟大城市所拥有的权力和影响的令人振奋的见证。迈锡尼在希腊神话中占有重要地位：传统上，它被认为是阿伽门农的故乡，而阿伽门农则是特洛伊的征服者。

<span id="page-25-0"></span>Locations in which scholars have sought to find the well-springs of Greek mythology are by no means confined to what we regard today as 'Greece'. When, in the late 18th century, the British colonial presence in India resulted in the dissemination of knowledge of the Sanskrit language to Western linguists, comparisons between Greek, Latin and Sanskrit led to the hypothesis of an original Indo-European language. Moreover a common language might, it was felt, point to shared features of social life also. So it was that, when Friedrich Max Müller (1823–1900), a prodigiously learned German philologist, was appointed to a Chair in Oxford, he developed an approach which related the mythology of Greece to its assumed Indo-European origins; indeed, Müller argued that some myths which seemed to him not to make sense in their Greek form became lucidity itself when 'translated' back into Indo-European. The fact that so many of these translations involved reducing the rich complexity of Greek myths to rather banal allegorical utterances about elementary natural phenomena meant that Müller's comparativist theory failed to win lasting assent – although even at the beginning of the 21st century a few scholars continue to approach Greek mythology from an explicitly Indo-European perspective.

> 学者们寻找希腊神话源头的地点并不仅限于我们今天所认为的“希腊”。18世纪末，英国在印度的殖民统治使西方语言学家接触到梵语知识，由此比较希腊语、拉丁语和梵语的成果促成了关于原始印欧语系语言的假说。此外，人们认为一种共同的语言可能意味着社会生活中的共同特征。因此，当学识渊博的德国语言学家弗里德里希·马克斯·缪勒（Friedrich Max Müller，1823–1900）被任命为牛津大学教授时，他发展出一种将希腊神话与假定的印欧语系起源联系起来的研究方法；事实上，缪勒认为，一些在希腊形式下似乎没有意义的神话，当被“翻译”回印欧语系时却变得清晰明了。然而，由于这些翻译往往将希腊神话中丰富的复杂性简化为关于基本自然现象的平庸寓意表达，因此缪勒的比较理论未能获得持久的认可——尽管即使在 21世纪初，一些学者继续从明确的印欧视角探讨希腊神话。

More broadly accepted than the Indo-European angle of approach is that which locates Greek myths against a background of Near Eastern – or, shifting the viewpoint, West Asiatic – civilizations, such as those of Assyria, Babylonia, the Hittites, ancient Israel, and so on. The two most influential contemporary voices which have articulated this case are those of the German scholar Walter Burkert and the British scholar Martin West. Their work and that of others has persuasively demonstrated major continuities between Greek storytelling and that of its eastern precursors, especially during the late Bronze Age (14th/13th centuries BC) and the so-called 'orientalizing period' (8th/7th centuries BC). Amongst the numerous significant parallels found are those involving cosmological accounts of the overthrow, by castration, of one supreme god by another (there are close similarities here between Hittite and Greek stories), and the monster-slaying exploits of the Greek hero Herakles (similar Mesopotamian myths exist). In some quarters, particularly in Greece itself, there has been opposition to this stress on West Asiatic parallels, on the grounds that such an emphasis might be felt to dilute the 'Hellenic' quality of Greek myths. As often, it is a question of striking a judicious balance: there should be room both for noting Near Eastern parallels and for asserting the undeniable and distinctive contribution made by Greeks themselves.

> 比印欧语系视角更广为接受的是这样一种观点，即将希腊神话置于近东——或者说，换一种视角来看，西亚——文明的背景下进行考察，比如亚述、巴比伦、赫梯、古代以色列等文明。当代最有力地阐述这一观点的两位学者是德国学者沃尔特· Burkert 和英国学者马丁·韦斯特。他们的研究以及其他人所做的工作，有力地证明了希腊叙事与东方先驱叙事之间存在重大连续性，尤其是在青铜时代晚期（公元前14至13世纪）和所谓的“东方化时期”（公元前8至7世纪）。在众多发现的显著相似之处中，包括关于一位至高神因被阉割而被另一位神推翻的宇宙学叙述（赫梯和希腊故事在此方面有密切相似之处），以及希腊英雄赫拉克勒斯屠龙事迹（类似美索不达米亚的神话也存在）。在某些地区，尤其是希腊本身，人们对此强调西亚平行现象持反对态度，理由是这种强调可能会被认为削弱希腊神话的“希腊性”。如常所见，这往往是一个需要恰当平衡的问题：一方面应注意到近东的相似之处，另一方面也应肯定希腊人自己所做出的不可否认且独特的贡献。

The controversy about West Asiatic issues pales into insignificance beside another contemporary debate about Greek culture, including its mythology. Martin Bernal, a British scholar now teaching in the United States, has maintained that the key to understanding ancient Greece lies not in West Asia but in Africa, and specifically in Egypt, whose civilization, in his view, enormously influenced that of Greece in ways which only the racially motivated misapprehensions of generations of Western academics have masked. Many scholars would agree that Egypt's role as an 'origin' for aspects of Greek culture has been, for whatever reason, downplayed; nevertheless, the pan-Egyptian explanation advocated by Bernal has found complete favour only with the ideologically committed. Whatever the final verdict on Bernal's thesis, this controversy illustrates that Greek mythology is far from being a matter of merely 'academic' interest. As we noted in relation to contemporary Ireland and Serbia, myths may constitute some of the most dynamic and 'charged' aspects of any society. Correspondingly, those who interpret myths, whether ancient or modern, may expose raw nerves, and thus provoke hostility or even fury.

> 关于西亚问题的争议，相比之下，在关于希腊文化（包括其神话）的当代讨论面前显得微不足道。目前在美国家教的英国学者马丁·贝尔纳（Martin Bernal）认为，理解古代希腊的关键并不在西亚，而是在非洲，尤其是埃及。在他看来，埃及文明对希腊文明产生了巨大的影响，而这种影响仅因几代西方学者基于种族偏见的误解而被掩盖。许多学者同意，埃及作为希腊文化某些方面“起源”的作用，无论出于何种原因，一直被低估；然而，贝尔纳所倡导的泛埃及解释仅在意识形态上坚定的人群中获得完全认可。无论最终对贝尔纳理论的评判如何，这场争议表明，希腊神话远非仅仅是“学术”兴趣的问题。正如我们之前提到的当代爱尔兰和塞尔维亚的情况一样，神话可能构成任何社会中最动态且“充满张力”的方面之一。相应地，无论是古代还是现代的神话诠释者，可能会触及某些敏感点，从而引发敌意甚至愤怒。

<span id="page-27-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_27_Picture_0.jpeg)
*The Mycenaean citadel at Tiryns, in the Argive plain, is surrounded by massive defensive walls, whose 'Cyclopean' masonry is named for the one-eyed giants who were said to have carried out the building work. It was from the top of these mighty walls that Herakles allegedly hurled Eurytos' son Iphitos, when he slew him in a fit of fury.*

> 位于阿尔戈斯平原上的迈锡尼城邦提尔尼亚的城堡被巨大的防御墙环绕，这些墙的“独眼巨人式”石工技艺是以传说中能建造此墙的独眼巨人为名。据称，赫拉克勒斯是在这些雄伟城墙之上将厄律托斯之子伊菲托斯扔下，当时他因愤怒而杀死了伊菲托斯。

### 2.3 **Where was Greece?** 希腊在哪里？

The word 'Greece' derives from the Latin 'Graecia'. It is probable that this name was first used of the Graikoi, a people living in Epeiros (in the northwest of present-day Greece), and that it was then taken over by the Italic peoples of the opposite, Adriatic coast, and used to refer, initially, to the Graikoi themselves, and later to a wider group of culturally and linguistically related peoples. Members of this wider group called themselves 'Hellenes', and called the territory in which they dwelt 'Hellas'. Accordingly, some modern scholars have adopted the terms 'Hellene/Hellas' in preference to 'Greek/Greece'. In the present book we shall retain 'Greece', while using 'Hellenic' and 'Greek' interchangeably. The word 'Hellenic' must, incidentally, be distinguished from 'Hellenistic', a term used conventionally (and in this book too) to designate the period from the death of Alexander the Great (323 BC) to the Battle of Actium (31 BC).

> “希腊”这个词源自拉丁语“Graecia”。有学者认为，这个名字最初是用于居住在埃庇鲁斯（今希腊西北部）的格拉伊奥斯人（Graikoi），随后被亚得里亚海沿岸的伊特鲁里亚人所采用，最初用来指代格拉伊奥斯人本身，后来则用来指代一个更广泛的、在文化和语言上相关的民族群体。 这个更广泛的群体自称“希腊人”（Hellenes），并将他们居住的地区称为“希腊”（Hellas）。因此，一些现代学者更倾向于使用“Hellene/Hellas”而非“希腊/Greece”这些术语。在本书中，我们将保留“希腊”一词，同时交替使用“Hellenic”和“Greek”。顺便指出，“Hellenic”一词必须与“Hellenistic”加以区分，后者是一个通常使用的术语（在本书中也是如此），用来指代从亚历山大大帝去世（公元前323年）到亚克兴战役（公元前31年）之间的时期。

The stories that we know as 'Greek myths' were told over a wide and historically fluctuating area, by no means coincident with the territory labelled 'Greece' on modern maps. Already by the Archaic period (conventionally 700– 500 BC), in addition to occupying the mainland north as far as Macedonia and east through Thrace to the Hellespont, Greek speakers had settled the Aegean islands and the western seaboard of Asia Minor (in present-day Turkey). In addition, the founding of overseas settlements further afield expanded the reach of all aspects of Hellenic culture, including its stories. During the Archaic and Classical periods (the Classical period is conventionally dated 500–323 BC), the most significant destination for this type of expansion was 'Magna Graecia', a Latin term designating the parts of southern Italy and Sicily which came under Greek influence. From Apulia (mod. Puglia) and Campania as far north as Etruria (mod. Tuscany), we find ample evidence of the presence of Greek stories, not least thanks to the enormous quantities of painted pottery which have survived in south and central Italian tombs.

> 我们所知的“希腊神话”故事是在一个广阔且历史变迁频繁的区域流传的，远非现代地图上标记为“希腊”的领土。早在古风时期（通常认为是公元前700至500年），除了占据巴尔干半岛北部直至马其顿，以及向东经色雷斯至赫勒斯滂一带外，希腊语使用者还已在爱琴海诸岛和小亚细亚西部沿海（今土耳其境内）定居。此外，海外殖民地的建立进一步扩展了希腊文化各个方面的影响力，包括其故事。在古风和古典时期（古典时期通常定为公元前500至323年），这种扩张最显著的地区是“大希腊”（Magna Graecia），这是拉丁语对受希腊影响的南意大利和西西里部分地区所作的指称。从普利亚（今普利亚大区）和坎帕尼亚地区，一直到以特拉西梅诺湖（今托斯卡纳）为界的北方，我们都能发现大量希腊故事存在的证据，尤其是由于大量彩陶在南意大利和中部意大利墓葬中得以保存下来。

<span id="page-28-0"></span>The export of Hellenic culture, myths included, extended further still. As early as the Archaic period, the north African coastal settlement of Kyrene (mod. Shahat) was a Greek foundation whose links with the homeland were expressed through mythology. As the poet Pindar (5th century BC) would put it later, celebrating the victory of the king of Kyrene in a chariot race in the Pythian Games at Delphi: 'Today, Muse, you must stand by a dear man, the king of Kyrene, city of fine horses – city of chariots on the silver-shining breast of the earth' (see also [pp. 35–](#page-65-0)[36\)](#page-66-0).

> 希腊文化（包括神话）的传播范围更进一步。早在古风时期，北非沿海的基提翁（今沙哈特）就是一个希腊殖民地，其与故土的联系通过神话得以体现。正如诗人品达（公元前5世纪）后来所言，庆祝基提翁国王在德尔斐皮媞亚竞技会上的战车赛胜利：“今天，缪斯，你必须站在一位亲爱的人身边，基提翁国王，骏马之城——银光闪耀大地的战车之城”（另见第35–36页）。

<span id="page-29-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_29_Picture_0.jpeg)
*This Etruscan vase (late 6th century BC) illustrates a myth in which Dionysos turns a group of pirates into dolphins (see [p. 82\)](#page-171-0). The power of the god – discreetly evoked by the presence of vine-leaves at the far left – effects a miraculous transformation, depicted in the sequence of images in the lower section of the vase.*

> 这具伊特鲁里亚陶罐（公元前6世纪晚期）描绘了一个神话故事：狄奥尼索斯将一群海盗变成了海豚（参见第82页）。神祇的力量——通过画面左侧的葡萄叶微妙地暗示——引发了一种奇迹般的转变，这一转变在陶罐底部区域的图像序列中得到了表现。

The invasion of Greece by Persian forces in the early 5th century BC was the single most decisive factor in generating a sense of Hellenic identity. With the
> 公元前5世纪初波斯军队入侵希腊是促成希腊认同感产生的最关键因素。

Persians repulsed, Greeks turned in upon themselves, tearing themselves apart in the protracted Athens–Sparta conflict ('the Peloponnesian War') in the last third of the 5th century. Then, in the 4th century, into the power-vacuum left by the weakened city-states of the southern mainland, there stepped a new and powerful force based in the northern Greek region of Macedonia. Hellenic expansion was to receive a totally unparalleled impetus thanks to the astonishing, albeit murderous energies of Alexander the Great, whose armies carried Greek culture as far as Egypt and Libya in the south and Afghanistan in the east. In the wake of military victory there followed civic consolidation, embodied in the buildings, customs and institutions which constituted the fabric of civic life: gymnasia, theatres, a common Greek language (the *koine*, 'common', 'shared'), as well as the stories which that language enabled to be retold. In the post-Alexander world of the Hellenistic kingdoms – ruled by dynasties founded by Alexander's generals, and handed on to their successors – many Greeks found themselves living at a greater distance than ever before from their ancestral hearth. In such a world, myths provided a vital means of retaining contact with cultural roots, while at the same time exploring new situations through a medium whose traditionality had always been open to constant adaptation, yet which was well on the way to becoming nostalgically regarded as 'classical'.

> 波斯人被击退后，希腊人陷入内斗，在公元前5世纪末期的雅典与斯巴达之间旷日持久的战争（即伯罗奔尼撒战争）中自相残杀。到了公元前4世纪，随着南部城邦势力的衰弱，形成权力真空，北方希腊马其顿地区崛起了一股新的强大势力。由于亚历山大大帝所展现出的惊人且毁灭性的能量，希腊的扩张获得了前所未有的动力，其军队将希腊文化传播至南部的埃及和利比亚，以及东部的阿富汗。军事胜利之后，随之而来的是城邦的巩固，这体现在诸如体育馆、剧院、通用希腊语（koine，意为“共同”或“共享”）以及由此语言得以重述的种种故事等构成市民生活的制度、习俗和建筑之中。在亚历山大大帝之后的希腊化王国时代，由其将领们建立的王朝及其继承者统治着世界，许多希腊人发现自己比以往任何时候都更远离祖先的故土。在这样的时代，神话成为维系文化根源的重要手段，同时又通过一个传统始终开放适应的新媒介，探索新的处境；然而，这种媒介正逐渐被怀旧地视为“古典”的。

<span id="page-31-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_31_Picture_0.jpeg)

*In this limestone relief (1st century BC) from Commagene in southeastern Turkey, King Antiochos I of Commagene clasps the hand of Herakles, who wields his trademark club.*
> 这幅石灰岩浮雕（公元前1世纪）出土于土耳其东南部的科马根，科马根国王安条克一世正与赫拉克勒斯握手，后者持着他的标志性木棒。

Region by region, the various parts of what had been Alexander's empire fell under the sway of Roman military and administrative power. The result was not the effacing of Greek myth-telling; on the contrary, Roman domination provided a framework for the survival of the Greeks' cultural heritage, including their mythology. One way in which this happened was that the Romans paid the Greeks the ultimate compliment of taking their literary and artistic works as models; the myths imperceptibly became not so much Greek as Greco-Roman.

> 各个地区逐渐落入罗马军事和行政权力的控制之下，曾经属于亚历山大帝国的各个部分都受到了罗马的影响。结果并非希腊神话叙述的消逝；相反，罗马的统治为希腊文化传统的延续提供了框架，包括他们的神话。这种情况发生的一种方式是，罗马人给予了希腊人最高的赞美，即将他们的文学和艺术作品作为典范；神话在不知不觉中不再是纯粹的希腊神话，而是演变为希腊-罗马神话。

What of the Greeks themselves under Roman power? Even when their cities had long since ceased to enjoy political independence, myth-tellers continued to narrate the ancient stories. Such harking back was not simply a product of inertia: it could make a strong ideological point. For example, the 2nd century AD Greek writer Pausanias incorporated into his description of a tour of Greece's ancient monuments countless references to the myths and rituals which he had come across, yet he virtually ignored the external political domination by Rome which provided the ever-present context for the life of this society. Pausanias came from Asia Minor – that is, from within the geographical area associated with 'Classical Greece'. But the world was now a bigger place. The satirist Lucian (2nd century AD), who exploited the old mythological themes with glee and panache, hailed from Samosata (mod. Samsât) on the Euphrates. As for Nonnos (5th century AD), author of a monumental 48-book epic (*Dionysiaca*) about the multifarious exploits of the god Dionysos, his homeland was Panopolis in Egypt. The geographical reach of the Greek myths was becoming ever more extensive. When the Romans' domination eventually ebbed away, the myths which they had inherited from the Greeks outlasted that domination, taking on ever newer guises, with an undiminished capacity for adaptation.

> 希腊人自己在罗马统治下如何呢？即使他们的城市早已失去了政治独立，神话讲述者们仍然继续讲述古代的故事。这种追溯并非仅仅出于惰性：它能够表达一种强烈的意识形态立场。例如，公元2世纪的希腊作家帕乌萨尼亚斯在他的希腊古迹巡游描述中，融入了无数关于神话和仪式的参考，但他几乎忽略了罗马的外部政治统治，而这种统治正是当时社会生活所处的永恒背景。帕乌萨尼亚斯来自小亚细亚——也就是与“古典希腊”地理区域相关的地区。但世界此时已经变得更大了。讽刺作家卢西安（公元2世纪），他兴致勃勃地运用古老的神话主题，充满风格地加以发挥，来自幼发拉底河畔的萨莫萨塔（今萨姆萨特）。至于诺诺斯（公元5世纪），他创作了一部宏大的48卷史诗《狄奥尼索斯纪》（Dionysiaca），讲述酒神狄奥尼索斯丰富多彩的冒险事迹，他的故乡是埃及的帕诺波利斯。希腊神话的地理范围正变得越来越广泛。当罗马的统治最终衰落时，他们从希腊继承下来的神话却超越了这种统治，不断以新的形式展现出来，其适应能力丝毫未减。

<span id="page-33-0"></span>![](_page_33_Picture_0.jpeg)
*Part of the frieze of the Great Altar of Zeus from Pergamon portrays the victory of the Olympian gods over their monstrous adversaries, the Giants.*
> 佩加蒙宙斯大祭坛浮雕带的一部分描绘了奥林匹斯神战胜巨人对手的场景。

## 3 <span id="page-34-0"></span>*[Sources of Evidence](#page-6-4)* 证据来源

### 3.1 **Material Culture** 物质文化

Why have Greek myths continued to grip the imagination of later centuries? Part of the explanation lies in the richness and power of the visual images through which ancient artists depicted the stories. These images, which constitute one of our two principal sources of information for Greek mythology, may be divided into several main types: sculptures, wall-paintings, mosaics, vase-paintings and coins, not forgetting the evidence of such media as engraved gemstones and decorated mirrors.

> 为什么希腊神话一直持续吸引后世的想象力？部分原因在于古代艺术家通过视觉图像所展现的丰富性和力量。这些图像构成了我们了解希腊神话的两大主要信息来源之一，可以分为几种主要类型：雕塑、壁画、马赛克、陶罐绘画和硬币，也不应忽视诸如雕刻宝石和装饰镜等媒介所提供的证据。

Of these types it is sculpture, in both its freestanding and its architectural forms, which enjoys the highest status today. Most of what has survived is in marble; with a few spectacular exceptions – notably the 'Riace bronzes' recovered from the bottom of the sea off Calabria – works in bronze, or in even more precious metals, tended to be only too vulnerable to melting-down and reuse. Relatively few examples of ancient Greek sculpture remain *in situ*; most are now conserved and admired in museums: the magnificent Parthenon (or 'Elgin') Marbles in the British Museum, London, for instance, or the statue of the Winged Victory of Samothrace, in the Louvre, Paris. In antiquity, too, sculptures representing the divinities, heroes and heroines of mythology were often regarded with awe by contemporaries. A classic case was Pheidias' legendary statue of Zeus at Olympia, now lost, but re-animated in lovingly detailed description by the ancient traveller Pausanias. 'When the statue was completed,' he observes, 'Pheidias prayed to the god to make a sign if the work was to his liking, and immediately a flash of lightning struck the pavement in the place where a bronze urn was still standing in my day.'

> 在这类艺术形式中，雕塑无论以独立形式还是建筑形式出现，都享有最高的地位。现存的大部分作品是以大理石制成的；仅有少数例外——尤其是从卡拉布里亚海中打捞出的“里亚切青铜像”——青铜或更珍贵金属制成的作品往往极易被熔化并重新利用。相对而言，现存古希腊雕塑原址的实例并不多；大多数如今被保存并陈列在博物馆中，例如大英博物馆伦敦所藏的宏伟帕特农（或称“埃尔金”）大理石雕像，或巴黎卢浮宫所藏的萨莫色雷斯胜利女神像。在古代，那些描绘神话中诸神、英雄和女英雄的雕塑，也常常令同时代人敬畏。一个典型的例子是菲迪亚斯所创作的奥林匹亚宙斯雕像，如今已失传，但古希腊旅行家帕乌萨尼亚斯以详尽而生动的描述将其重现。他写道：“当雕像完成时，菲迪亚斯祈求神明若觉得这件作品合心意，便给予一个迹象。随即，一道闪电击中了地面上我当年见过的青铜花瓶所在的位置。”

<span id="page-35-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_35_Picture_0.jpeg)

*The Winged Victory of Samothrace (perhaps* c*. 190 BC, though many scholars date it earlier). The figure of Nike ('Victory') is shown alighting on the prow of a *ship; in its original location in the sanctuary of the Great Gods on the island of Samothrace, the prow was set in the basin of a fountain. The statue, now in the Louvre, may commemorate a naval victory by the Hellenistic ruler who commissioned the work. Thanks to the sculptor's genius, Nike's posture and drapery express a miraculous sense of dynamism and grace.*

> 萨莫色雷斯的胜利女神（可能约公元前190年，尽管许多学者认为它更早）。胜利女神（“胜利”）的雕像正降落在一艘船的船首。该船原址位于萨莫色雷斯岛上的大神神殿，船首被安置在喷泉池中。如今这尊雕像陈列于卢浮宫，可能纪念了委托创作此作品的希腊化时代君主取得的一次海战胜利。得益于雕塑家的天才，尼克的姿态与衣褶表现出一种奇迹般地动感与优雅。

<span id="page-37-0"></span>![](_page_37_Picture_0.jpeg)
*The abduction of Persephone by Hades, depicted in a wall-painting from the 'Tomb of Persephone', a royal Macedonian tomb at Vergina, west of Thessaloniki (mid-4th century BC). Persephone struggles in vain when Hades seizes her for his bride and rides away with her in his chariot to the Underworld.*
> 壁画中描绘了冥王哈迪斯劫走珀耳塞福涅的情景，出自位于希腊萨洛尼卡以西的维尔吉纳的“珀耳塞福涅墓”（王室马其顿墓穴，公元前4世纪中叶）。珀耳塞福涅徒劳地挣扎着，当哈迪斯抓住她作为新娘，乘着战车带她前往地下世界。

In contrast to sculpture, Greek wall-painting on mythological themes is represented by pitifully few extant examples. We know that many major works in this medium once existed: the invaluable Pausanias records a lengthy description of what must have been particularly sumptuous compositions, namely murals by the artist Polygnotos in the *lesche* ('conversation house'; see [p. 28](#page-50-0)) of the Knidians at Delphi, depicting the Fall of Troy, and Odysseus in the Underworld. However, by comparison with what has perished – and indeed by comparison with the remains of the wonderful frescoes of Minoan Crete and the splendid wall-paintings preserved from the Roman period – Classical Greece has left us relatively little. But the few exceptions are the more to be cherished, as with the sensational portrayal of Hades' abduction of Persephone, on the walls of a Macedonian royal tomb near Vergina.

> 与雕塑不同，现存的以神话主题为题材的希腊壁画作品寥寥无几。我们知道，这种艺术形式曾经存在过许多重要作品：宝贵的帕乌萨尼亚斯记录了对一幅必定尤为豪华的壁画作品的详细描述，即由艺术家波吕戈诺斯创作的壁画，该壁画位于德尔斐的克尼迪亚人所建的莱斯奇（“谈话屋”；参见第28页），描绘的是特洛伊的陷落以及奥德修斯在冥界的情景。然而，与已消逝的壁画相比，甚至与米诺斯克里特岛那些辉煌的壁画残迹以及壮丽的壁画相比，这些作品的存留实在微乎其微。罗马时期的壁画——古典希腊留给我们的遗存相对较少。但这些少数例外却更值得珍惜，例如在韦尔吉纳附近的一座马其顿皇家墓穴墙壁上，那幅引人注目的哈迪斯劫夺珀耳塞福涅的描绘。

![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_38_Picture_1.jpeg)

*Dionysos riding on the back of a panther-like creature: mosaic floor, Pella, late 4th century BC. Dionysos is often associated with 'exotic' wild beasts such as this one, signifying the god's ferocity and danger as well as his physical grace and beauty.*
> 狄俄尼索斯骑在一只豹形生物背上：马赛克地板，佩拉，公元前4世纪晚期。狄俄尼索斯常与这种异域的野兽相联系，象征着这位神祇的凶猛与危险，以及他那身姿优雅与俊美。

Decorated mosaic flooring is another medium for which Roman civilization offers us far more surviving material than does Greek. But from the end of the 5th century BC onwards, and increasingly in the Hellenistic period, the use of this means of decorating aristocratic or royal dwellings became more widespread; and some notable examples can still be appreciated. One of the main centres of Macedonian culture, the city of Pella, is a case in point; particularly fine is the image of the god Dionysos seated on a panther. To get a sense of what a Greek mosaic floor might have looked like in its ancient setting, a good place to go is the site of Olynthos, just north of the three-pronged peninsula of northern Greece known as Chalkidike. There, open to the elements in its original location, is a floor-mosaic of Bellerophon, mounted on the winged horse Pegasos and ready to slay the monstrous Chimaira.

> 装饰性马赛克地板是另一种媒介，罗马文明为我们提供了远多于希腊的幸存材料。但自5世纪末以来，特别是在希腊化时期，这种装饰手法在贵族或皇家居所中的使用日益广泛；一些著名的例子至今仍可欣赏。马其顿文化的主要中心之一佩拉城便是典型案例；其中尤以狄俄尼索斯神端坐于豹子上的图像尤为精美。若想了解古希腊马赛克地板在古代环境中的样子，可以前往位于希腊北部哈尔基迪克半岛北侧的奥林索斯遗址。在那里，这幅描绘贝尔费罗斯骑乘飞马佩加索斯、准备击杀怪兽奇美拉的马赛克地板仍保留在其原始位置，暴露于自然元素之中。

<span id="page-39-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_39_Picture_1.jpeg)

*Bellerophon, riding on his winged steed Pegasos, vanquishes the fearsome firebreathing monster, Chimaira. Centrepiece of mosaic floor from Olynthos in the Chalkidike peninsula, 4th century BC*.
> 贝尔洛丰骑着他翼马彭格索斯，击败了可怕的喷火怪兽奇美拉。这是来自卡利地亚半岛奥林索斯的马赛克地板中央图案，创作于公元前4世纪。

### 3.2 **Myth in vase-painting** 陶器彩绘中的神话

With vase-paintings, our evidence increases a thousandfold. The term 'vase' is conventionally used by archaeologists to denote a wide range of ceramic containers, from oil flasks to perfume boxes, water jars and mixing bowls. Such containers figured in a variety of social contexts, including funerals, weddings, drinking parties (*symposia*) and sacrificial rituals, as well as all the humdrum needs of domestic life, such as storage and cooking. Most containers were undecorated, but many displayed painted embellishment, which was fired along with the clay and hence acquired amazing resilience. Sometimes a scene represented on a pot can be securely identified as mythological; sometimes it may be drawn from 'real life'; sometimes it is impossible to say whether life is being represented like myth, or myth like life – a fact which in itself illustrates the central role which Greek mythological narratives played in the imaginative lives of those who told them. At all events, the evidence of painted pottery provides us with a rich harvest of mythological narrative. It would, indeed, have been possible to find a vase-painting to illustrate most of the Greek myths discussed in this book.

> 通过陶绘，我们的证据增加了千倍。考古学家通常将“陶器”一词用于指代各种陶瓷容器，从油瓶、香水盒、水罐到混合碗等。这些容器出现在多种社会场合中，包括葬礼、婚礼、饮酒聚会（酒会）和祭祀仪式，以及日常生活中的各种平凡需求，如储藏和烹饪。大多数容器都是无装饰的，但许多容器上绘有彩绘装饰，这些装饰与陶土一起烧制，因而具有惊人的耐久性。有时，陶器上的场景可以明确辨认为神话故事；有时则可能取材于“现实生活”；有时则难以分辨是现实被神话化，还是神话被现实化——这一事实本身便说明了希腊神话叙事在讲述者想象力生活中的核心地位。无论如何，彩绘陶器所提供的证据为我们提供了丰富的神话叙事。事实上，确实有可能找到陶绘来诠释本书中讨论的大多数希腊神话。

<span id="page-41-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_41_Picture_0.jpeg)

*One of the most famous Greek artefacts depicting mythological themes, the 'François Vase' (*c. *570 BC; named after its discoverer) was found in an Etruscan tomb near Chiusi in 1844. Among the numerous episodes which it represents are the slaying of the Kalydonian boar, the funeral games of Patroklos, and the wedding of Peleus and Thetis.*

> 著名的描绘神话主题的希腊艺术品之一——“弗朗索瓦花瓶”（约公元前570年；以其发现者命名），于1844年在奇乌西附近的一座伊特鲁里亚墓穴中被发现。其上描绘的众多事件包括卡吕冬野猪的猎杀、帕特罗克洛斯的葬礼竞技赛，以及佩琉斯与忒提斯的婚礼。

### 3.3 **Coins, gems and mirrors** 硬币、宝石和镜子

Whereas vases often depict a scene that is, in effect, the synchronic distillation of several episodes of a mythical narrative, an ancient Greek coin typically portrays a politico-religious image which in some way encapsulates the identity of the community who struck the coin. Often the image will evoke the community's foundation myth, or a god or goddess particularly associated with the community. The best-known examples are probably the 'owls of Athene', the birds which symbolize the identity of the patron goddess of Athens. But throughout the Greek

> 与陶器常常描绘的场景不同，陶器实际上是以同时性提炼的方式展现神话叙事中的多个情节相比，古希腊硬币通常描绘的是一种政治与宗教相关的图像，这种图像在某种程度上体现了铸造硬币的社区的身份。通常，这种图像会唤起该社区的起源神话，或与该社区特别相关的神或女神。最著名的例子可能是“雅典娜的猫头鹰”，这些猫头鹰象征着雅典的守护神雅典娜的身份。但整个希腊世界...

world we find comparable examples. For instance, the city of Mende (in the Chalkidike peninsula) celebrated the fame of its local wine by issuing a type of coinage representing the god Dionysos seated on the back of a donkey.

> 在我们的世界中，可以找到类似的例子。例如，位于卡利德基半岛的梅嫩德城，为了颂扬其本地葡萄酒的声誉，发行了一种硬币，上面描绘了狄奥尼索斯神坐在驴背上的形象。

Rarer than coins, but no less instructive in terms of their bearing of mythological images, are engraved gems and finger rings; some wonderful examples of this form of micro-artistry have survived. Another of the smallerscale arts of antiquity was concerned with the engraving of the lids or covers of mirrors (typically made of copper alloy). Often the imagery borne by these fine objects had a mythological flavour, perhaps designed to conjure up an ideal or heroized world to complement the role of the mirror in the beautifying of an individual. Especially intricate examples were made by the Etruscans, whose representations of mythology frequently have the added interest of filtering Greek stories through their own, distinctive imaginative structures.

> 比硬币更为稀有的是雕刻宝石和指环；它们在表现神话图像方面同样具有启发性，一些这种微观艺术形式的杰出作品至今仍得以保存。古代另一项小型艺术则专注于镜子盖子（通常由铜合金制成）的雕刻。这些精美的物品往往带有神话色彩，也许旨在唤起一种理想化或英雄化的世界，以补充镜子在美化个人形象中的作用。伊特鲁里亚人创作了一些尤为精巧的实例，他们对神话的描绘常常具有额外的趣味性，即通过他们自己独特的想象结构来重新诠释希腊故事。

<span id="page-43-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_43_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Athenian coin, 5th century BC, showing Athene's owl. The bird symbolized the protection exercised by the goddess over the people of Athens. The owl also kept a watchful eye on Athenian trade and commerce from its vantage point on the reverse side of coins, which bore Athene's own image on the obverse.*

> 雅典硬币，公元前5世纪，展示了雅典娜的猫头鹰。这只鸟象征着女神对雅典人民的保护。猫头鹰也从硬币背面的高处警惕地注视着雅典的贸易和商业，而硬币的正面则刻有雅典娜本人的形象。

<span id="page-44-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_44_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Dionysos perched on a donkey: coin from the city of Mende in Chalkidike (late 5th century BC). In artistic representations Dionysos is often associated with the donkey. Here the god's demeanour is particularly relaxed: the wine vessel in his hand alludes to the liquid which ideally suits him to be the emblem of a city famous for its wine.*

> 狄俄尼索斯骑在驴背上：来自哈尔基迪克门德城的硬币（公元前5世纪末）。在艺术表现中，狄俄尼索斯常与驴子联系在一起。此处神祇的神态尤为放松：他手中的酒器暗示了最适合他的饮品，即作为以葡萄酒闻名的城市象征。

<span id="page-45-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_45_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*A decorated Etruscan mirror, late 4th century BC, depicting the Judgment of Paris. Turan (the Etruscan Aphrodite) is seated; facing her are Uni (Hera; cf. the Roman Juno) and Menrva (Athene; cf. the Roman Minerva). At the far right is Elcsntre (Alexandros, an alternative name for Paris); at the far left is Althaia.*

> 一件装饰精美的伊特鲁里亚镜，公元前4世纪晚期，描绘了帕里斯的审判。特兰（伊特鲁里亚的阿佛洛狄忒）端坐其中；面对她的有优尼（赫拉；参见罗马的朱诺）和门努瓦（雅典娜；参见罗马的弥涅尔瓦）。在最右侧的是埃尔森特雷（亚历山德罗斯，帕里斯的另一个名字）；在最左侧的是阿尔泰亚。

<span id="page-46-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_46_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The provided text is missing. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Gold ring from a chamber tomb at Mycenae. This might represent a cult of the death-and-rebirth of vegetation. The central figure may be a divinity or a priestess; the figure on the right appears to be in mourning; the one on the left holds a tree ecstatically.*

> 迈锡尼墓室墓中出土的金戒。这可能代表对植物生死轮回的崇拜。中心人物可能是神或女祭司；右侧人物似乎在哀悼；左侧人物则狂喜地手握一棵树。

#### 3.3.1 **Texts**

> 3.4.3.1 文本

Images can tell us a great deal. But they are often silent at the very moments where we most want them to speak. One has only to consider the countless, frustrating ambiguities generated by attempts to interpret the religious symbolism of Minoan and Mycenaean images without the aid of accompanying texts. From the Archaic period of Greek civilization onwards, by contrast, we do have texts, in marvellous abundance. These texts provide the second of our two main sources of evidence for Greek myths.

> 图像可以向我们传达大量信息。但往往在我们最希望它们发声的时候，它们却保持沉默。只需考虑一下，试图在没有伴随文字的情况下解读米诺斯和迈锡尼文明的宗教象征时所产生的无数令人沮丧的歧义就足以说明这一点。相比之下，从古希腊文明的古风时期开始，我们便拥有了数量惊人丰富的文字资料。这些文字为我们了解希腊神话提供了第二个主要证据来源。

Sometimes we are lucky enough to have direct access to texts written in antiquity. In such cases, the place where the texts were deposited has been the decisive factor in their preservation, since only in very special circumstances have perishable materials been able to survive the ravages of the two millennia and more which separate us from the ancient Greeks. The prime example of such circumstances is provided by papyri, i.e. sheets made from strips of the papyrus

> 有时我们幸运地能够直接接触到古代的文献。在这种情况下，文献存放的地点往往是其得以保存的决定性因素，因为只有在非常特殊的情况下，易腐材料才能经受住我们与古代希腊人之间两千年以上的时光侵蚀。这类特殊情况的典型例子就是纸莎草纸，即由纸莎草纸条制成的薄片。

plant superimposed on one another to make a durable writing material. Although very occasionally papyri have been preserved in Greece, the bulk of them owe their survival to the bone-dry Egyptian sands: we have numerous, although often fragmentary, Greek writings from the Hellenistic period, when Greek civilization had expanded to include Egypt within its ambit. Given the importance of myths in Greek culture, it is no surprise to find that many papyrus texts relate or refer to mythological narratives. New finds of papyrus are still being made, and by no means all the papyri in the basements of our museums have been edited. The corpus of Greek mythology is not 'closed'.

> 将纸莎草纸层层叠加以制成耐久的书写材料。尽管在希腊偶尔也会发现保存下来的纸莎草纸，但大部分纸莎草纸之所以得以保存，多亏了埃及干燥的沙土。我们拥有大量（尽管常常是残缺的）希腊化时期的希腊文献，此时希腊文明已扩展至包括埃及在内的广阔范围。鉴于神话在希腊文化中的重要地位，发现许多纸莎草纸文献涉及或提及神话叙事也就不足为奇了。至今仍在不断发现新的纸莎草纸文献，而且我们博物馆地下室中保存的纸莎草纸并非全部都已整理出版。希腊神话体系并非封闭的。

<span id="page-47-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_47_Picture_1.jpeg)

> 该上下文涉及希腊神话，将以下英文文本翻译成中文：

*Extract from Book 2 of Homer's* Iliad*, from the 2nd–3rd century AD 'Hawara Papyrus', discovered in Egypt.*

> 《荷马史诗·伊利亚特》第二卷摘录，出自公元2至3世纪的“哈瓦拉纸莎草纸”（Hawara Papyrus），在埃及发现。

Important though texts on papyrus are, they do not compare in quantity with a less direct source: the manuscripts that have survived complete, mainly from much later times. The advent and growing influence of Christianity within the Roman Empire meant that preservation of the great classics of the pagan world could by no means be guaranteed. That any writing at all has survived from Greek antiquity is due in large part to the civilization of Byzantium, where texts continued to be copied and recopied, enabling study of selected works from the great Hellenic past to form part of the Byzantine educational programme. When Byzantium at last fell to the Turks in 1453, many of these texts were brought westwards, to attain at last a less fragile existence thanks to printing. This complex process of transmission, which depended on a mixture of diligent commitment and pure chance, has resulted in the present availability of excellent and relatively intact texts of incomparable mythological documents.

> 尽管纸莎草纸上的文本非常重要，但它们在数量上无法与一种更为间接的来源相比：那些在后来时代主要留存下来的完整手稿。基督教在罗马帝国的兴起及其日益增长的影响意味着，保证保存古希腊世界的伟大经典已不可能。能够从古希腊时代保留下来任何文字，很大程度上要归功于拜占庭文明，因为在拜占庭，文本继续被抄写和重抄，使得从伟大的希腊传统中选取部分作品进行研究，成为拜占庭教育体系的一部分。当拜占庭最终在1453年陷落于土耳其人之手时，许多这些文本被带往西方，最终因印刷术而获得了更稳固的存在。这一复杂的传播过程依赖于勤奋的投入与纯粹偶然的结合，从而使得如今我们能够拥有质量优秀、相对完整且不可比拟的神话文献。

<span id="page-48-0"></span>![](_page_48_Picture_0.jpeg)

*10th-century AD codex of Book 3 of Homer's* Iliad *in the library of San Marco, Venice.*

> 10世纪的《伊利亚特》第三卷手抄本，位于威尼斯圣马可图书馆。

## 4 <span id="page-49-0"></span>*[Contexts for Myth-telling](#page-6-5)*

> 3.5 神话讲述的语境

#### 4.1.1 **Myth-telling in everyday life**

> 3.5.1.1 日常生活中的神话讲述

Greeks first experienced mythology as children, through the stories told to them by their elders; mostly our sources identify the tellers as the children's nurses, mothers or grandmothers. The point of some of these tales clearly lay in social control: threatening bogey-figures such as Lamia and Gorgo were invoked in the hope of getting the recalcitrant toddler to toe the line. But the exploits of the heroes and the Olympian deities figured also – indeed, Plato strenuously objected to the (as he saw it) anti-social results of letting children hear about violent dissension between Gods and Giants, and about bitter disputes among the Olympians themselves. Outside the home, once schools became a normal feature of Greek life (at least for boys), children's familiarity with the world of mythology was reinforced and extended in this context also: memorizing and reciting poetry formed a central part of the curriculum.

> 希腊人最初是在童年时期通过长辈讲述的故事接触到神话的；大多数史料表明，这些故事的讲述者通常是孩子的保姆、母亲或祖母。一些故事显然具有社会控制的目的：例如，通过提及像拉米亚和戈戈这样的吓人角色，希望能使不听话的幼儿遵守规矩。然而，英雄们的壮举以及奥林匹斯众神的事迹同样受到重视——事实上，柏拉图曾强烈反对让孩子听闻诸神与巨人的暴力冲突以及奥林匹斯众神之间的激烈争执所可能带来的（在他看来）反社会后果。在学校成为希腊日常生活（至少对男孩而言）常态之后，孩子们在家庭之外也通过这种环境进一步加深并拓展了对神话世界的熟悉：背诵和朗诵诗歌成为课程的核心内容之一。

<span id="page-50-1"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_50_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本。

*The Gorgons were winged, female figures whose nightmarish expression provoked terror. One of the Gorgons was Medusa, mother of the winged horse Pegasos. In this terracotta relief (late 7th century BC) – perhaps part of an altar at Syracuse – Pegasos can be seen under the arm of his grotesque parent.*

> 戈耳工是拥有翅膀的女性怪物，她们令人恐惧的面容会引发恐怖。其中一位戈耳工是美杜莎，她是翼马佩加索斯的母亲。在这件陶土浮雕（公元前七世纪晚期）——可能属于锡拉库扎的一座祭坛的一部分——中，可以看到佩加索斯在它那狰狞的父母臂下。

<span id="page-50-0"></span>In later life, several different social and topographical contexts provided a framework for myth-narration. At the *symposion* – a kind of formalized drinking party for (principally) aristocratic, adult males – the participants sometimes exchanged songs and stories with a mythological slant, just as the vessels used by the drinkers often bore imagery with mythological resonance. Other typical contexts for story-telling were the cobbler's, the barber's, and the *lesche* (plural *leschai*), a communal gathering-place in which groups of (usually old) men would

> 在后期，几种不同的社会和地理环境为神话叙述提供了框架。在酒宴（symposion）——一种主要由贵族成年男性参加的正式饮酒聚会中，参与者有时会交换带有神话色彩的歌曲和故事，就如同饮酒者所使用的器皿上常常绘有富有神话寓意的图案。其他常见的讲故事场合还包括修鞋匠的作坊、理发师的工作间，以及“lesche”（复数形式为leschai），这是一种公共集会场所，通常年长的男子们会在那里聚会。

meet informally to talk and gossip; an anecdotal life of the poet Homer relates that he sang in, among other venues, a cobbler's shop and the '*leschai* of the old men'.

> 随意聚会聊天和八卦；关于诗人的轶事记载说，他曾在一个鞋匠铺和其他场所，如老人的集会中演唱。

<span id="page-51-0"></span>![](_page_51_Picture_1.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*By defeating the rebellious Giants, the Olympians decisively consolidated their own power. On this Attic vase (mid-5th century BC) three Olympians take vigorous action: Zeus with his thunderbolt; at his side, Hera with her spear; finally Athene,*

> 通过击败反叛的巨人，奥林匹斯诸神果断地巩固了自己的权力。在这件雅典陶罐（公元前5世纪中期）上，三位奥林匹斯神祇正展开激烈的行动：宙斯手持雷霆；在他身旁，赫拉持长矛；最后是雅典娜，

#### 4.1.2 *protected by her awesome 'aegis', a snake-fringed cape bearing a Gorgon emblem.*

> 3.5.1.2 被她强大的“埃癸斯”所保护，饰有蛇纹的披风，带有戈耳戈徽记。

Temples and other sites of ritual observance also provided contexts in which myths were narrated. In the case of many rituals, we can point to myths which in some sense 'went with' the action dramatized in the ceremony. For instance, the ritual complex known as the 'Eleusinian Mysteries' (Eleusis is west of Athens, in the direction of the Corinthian Gulf) finds its counterpart in the myth of the abduction of the goddess Persephone, best known to us from the version narrated in the *Homeric Hymn to Demeter*. As the myth related the seizing of Persephone by the god Hades and her eventual, albeit non-permanent, restoration to her mother Demeter, so the ritual at Eleusis, performed in honour of the 'two goddesses', i.e. Demeter and Persephone, enacted the contrast between the anxious gloom of the initiand and the joyful revelation experienced by the initiate. Unfortunately, in this case as in many comparable ones, it is not possible to say with certainty how or indeed whether the narration of the myth actually formed part of the rite. That said, analysis of myth-and-ritual complexes has been a productive growth area in the recent study of Greek myths. To take just one example, the Dutch scholar H. S. Versnel has thrown new light on the myth of Kronos – Zeus' primordial predecessor as ruler of the cosmos – by relating it to the Kronia festival, an in-between time of licence and topsy-turvy role-reversal during which slaves were waited on by their masters. As long as the festival lasted, 'old times' returned, with merry-making combined with chaos – a combination of positive and negative qualities which corresponded to a similar ambiguity in the character of Kronos himself. After the festival, the established order was reinstated, just as according to the myth Zeus had taken the place of Kronos. (On the myth of Kronos, see [pp. 46–](#page-89-0)[48.](#page-95-0))

> 庙宇和其他仪式性场所也为神话的叙述提供了背景。在许多仪式中，我们可以指出一些神话，这些神话在某种意义上与仪式中戏剧化的行为相伴随。例如，被称为“埃莱夫西斯秘仪”的仪式综合体（埃莱夫西斯位于雅典西边，靠近科林斯湾）在神话中找到了对应——即珀耳塞福涅女神被劫持的神话，我们最熟悉的版本来自《德米特尔颂》。正如神话叙述了珀耳塞福涅被冥界之神哈迪斯劫持，最终（尽管不是永久）被她的母亲德米特尔恢复原状一样，埃莱夫西斯的仪式则是以对“两位女神”（即德米特尔和珀耳塞福涅）的敬奉为形式，展现了入门者焦虑的阴郁与受礼者获得启示的喜悦之间的对比。不幸的是，在这种情况下，如同许多类似的情况一样，我们无法确切地说清楚或确定神话叙述是否实际构成了仪式的一部分。尽管如此，对神话与仪式综合体的分析近年来已成为研究希腊神话的富有成效的研究领域。以一个例子来说，荷兰学者H. S. Versnel通过将该神话与“克罗尼亚节”相联系，为克洛诺斯的神话带来了新的见解。克罗尼亚节是介于常态与混乱之间的时期，此时奴隶由他们的主人服侍，角色发生颠倒。在节庆期间，“旧日时光”重现，欢乐与混乱结合——这种正负并存的特质，与克罗诺斯本人性格中的矛盾性相呼应。节庆结束后，既定的秩序得以恢复，正如神话中宙斯取代克罗诺斯一样。 （关于克罗诺斯的神话，参见第46–48页。）

<span id="page-53-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_53_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本。

*Eleusis was one of the holiest places in Greece: it was the site of the 'Mysteries' in honour of Demeter and Persephone. These sacred rites drew participants from all over the Greek world. What the modern visitor sees here is the remains of the Roman rebuilding of the Telesterion ('Hall of Initiation').*

> 埃琉西斯是希腊最神圣的地方之一：它是纪念得墨忒尔和珀耳塞福涅的“秘仪”仪式的所在地。这些神圣的仪式吸引了来自整个希腊世界的参与者。现代游客在这里看到的是罗马人重建的泰莱斯特里翁（“启蒙大厅”）的遗迹。

<span id="page-54-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_54_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the specific English text you would like translated into Chinese, and I will assist you accordingly.

*Worship at Eleusis was mainly devoted to 'The Two Goddesses', Demeter and her daughter Persephone. On this relief (mid-5th century BC) Demeter is seated and holds three ears of corn. Standing before her is a younger figure, probably Persephone, holding torches. The contrast between darkness and illumination formed an important part of the initiatory experience at the Mysteries.*

> 埃莱乌西斯的祭祀主要献给两位女神——得墨忒尔和她的女儿珀耳塞福涅。在这块浮雕（公元前五世纪中期）中，得墨忒尔端坐并手持三粒麦穗。她面前站着一位年轻的身影，可能是珀耳塞福涅，正举着火炬。黑暗与光明之间的对比在密仪的启蒙仪式中扮演了重要角色。

Narration of myths may be visual as well as verbal, and we can point to countless instances where the fixed imagery of temples, and the mobile imagery of cult vessels, evoked for the worshipper stories from the mythical past. So far as temple imagery is concerned, we need look no further than the marble metopes from the Athenian Parthenon: through their combination of dynamic energy and effortless permanence, these supreme sculptures illustrate the opposition between chaos and order, by juxtaposing the subversive aggression of the monstrous

> 神话的叙述可以是视觉的，也可以是口头的，我们可以指出无数实例，其中庙宇固定的意象和祭祀器皿流动的意象，唤起了崇拜者对神话过往的回忆。至于庙宇意象，我们无需远眺，雅典帕特农神庙的大理石浮雕饰带便足以说明问题：这些至高无上的雕塑通过动态能量与毫不费力的永恒性的结合，通过将怪异的颠覆性攻击性与秩序相对立，展示了混沌与秩序之间的对立。

Centaurs with the controlled force of their heroic adversaries, the young Lapith men. As for the more mobile (but still amazingly durable) ceramic vessels used in various ceremonies, a typical example is offered by the small oil containing jars known as *lekythoi*, whose role in funerary ritual is complemented by the imagery which they commonly bear, imagery which, as often as not, evokes some aspect of death, for instance through the portrayal of the infernal ferryman Charon, or Hermes the Conveyor of Souls.

> 半人半马的肯陶罗斯在与英勇的拉庇斯青年战士的对抗中展现出受控的力量。至于那些更为灵活（但仍令人惊叹地坚固）的用于各种仪式的陶制器皿，典型的例子便是被称为利基托伊的小型油罐。这些利基托伊在葬礼仪式中所扮演的角色，由它们通常承载的图像所补充，这些图像经常描绘与死亡相关的场景，例如冥河摆渡人卡戎或引导灵魂的信使神墨丘利。

<span id="page-55-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_55_Picture_1.jpeg)

> <answer>特洛伊战争是希腊人和特洛伊人之间的一场传奇战役。</answer>

*A series of combats between Centaurs and Lapiths provides the theme of the marble metopes (decorative panels) on the Parthenon in Athens (mid-5th century BC). The astonishing combination of violence with calm is one of the high points of ancient mythological representation.*

> 雅典帕特农神庙（公元前5世纪中叶）上的大理石雕饰板（装饰板）以半人半马兽与卢皮斯人之间的战斗系列为主题。暴力与宁静的震撼结合是古代神话艺术表现中的杰出代表之一。

*Charon, ferryman of the Underworld, poles his boat towards a funeral stele (on the other side of the vase) beyond which is the figure of a woman; he will be offering her a passage to Hades. Charon's grim expression perfectly matches his occupation. The vase (5th century BC) is known as a* lekythos*, a type which often depicts Charon.*

> 卡戎，冥界摆渡人，正用木桨划着船，朝陶罐另一侧的葬礼石碑驶去，石碑彼端是一位女性的图象；他将为她提供通往冥界的渡船。卡戎严峻的神情完美地契合了他的职责。这个陶罐（公元前5世纪）被称为莱克西斯陶罐，这种类型的陶罐常常描绘卡戎。

### 4.2 **Myths in performance: Epic**

> 3.5.2 表演中的神话：史诗

If there is one thing which demonstrates conclusively that myths could be experienced as actively present in the lives of Greeks, it is the regularity with which mythical narratives were performed. Foremost among the mythological texts which survive from antiquity are the two towering epics ascribed to Homer: the *Iliad* and the *Odyssey.* The *Iliad* narrates a particular episode from near the

> 如果有一件事能够确凿地表明神话故事在古希腊人的生活中积极地存在，那就是神话叙事被频繁表演的规律性。在古代流传下来的神话文本中，最突出的是被归于荷马名下的两部宏伟史诗：《伊利亚特》和《奥德赛》。《伊利亚特》叙述了一个发生在接近……的特定事件。

end of the Trojan War when Achilles withdraws from battle, furious – the first word of the epic is *menin*, 'wrath' – because the Greek commander Agamemnon has deprived him of his prize, the slave-concubine Briseis; only when his dear companion Patroklos has been killed by the Trojans does Achilles return to the fray. At last Achilles' anger abates – not completely, but just enough to allow him to restore the corpse of Hektor (Patroklos' slayer, slain in his turn by Achilles) to his inconsolable father Priam. Even such a brief summary indicates that the *Iliad* is about far more than one small episode in a war: it is a story of the precarious balance between pity and rage, and between heroism and all-consuming violence. And all this is played out under the eyes of the gods; although capable of feeling passionate involvement with the fates of mortals, they are nevertheless ultimately remote, operating on a scale of time and power beyond human comprehension.

> 特洛伊战争结束时，阿基琉斯因愤怒而退出战斗——史诗的第一词是“menin”（意为“愤怒”）——因为希腊统帅阿伽门农夺走了他的战利品，奴隶情妇布里塞伊斯；只有当他的挚友帕特罗克洛斯被特洛伊人杀害时，阿基琉斯才重返战场。最终，阿基琉斯的怒火平息了——并非完全平息，但足以让他将赫克托耳（帕特罗克洛斯的杀害者，后又被阿基琉斯所杀）的尸体归还给悲痛欲绝的父亲帕里斯。即使如此简短的概述也表明，《伊利亚特》所讲述的远不止战争中的一个小片段：它讲述的是怜悯与愤怒之间、英雄主义与吞噬一切的暴力之间脆弱的平衡。所有这一切都在众神的注视下展开；尽管他们能够对凡人的命运产生强烈的关切，但最终他们仍是遥不可及的，他们的时空与力量尺度远超人类的理解。

<span id="page-58-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_58_Picture_0.jpeg)

*An idealized marble bust of Homer –* the *poet. What relationship this sculpture (probably harking back to a Hellenistic original) bears to any real blind bard who ever sang in pre-Archaic Greece, it is impossible to say. But the quiet authority of this face suits the tremendous power of Homeric poetry.*

> 理想化的大理石半身像，这位诗人。这尊雕塑（很可能源自一个希腊化时期的原作）与任何曾在古希腊前古典时期吟唱过的真正盲眼诗人之间有何关联，我们无法确定。但这张面容所展现出的沉静权威，正与荷马史诗的巨大影响力相得益彰。

*Homer's supremacy amongst poets is expressed in a remarkable sculptured relief (2nd century BC) known as 'The Apotheosis of Homer'. Below a mountain-top dominated by Zeus, the Muses and Apollo, Homer himself is majestically seated on a throne (bottom left). Personified figures surround him, including Oikoumene*

> 荷马在诗人中的至高地位通过一件非凡的浮雕作品表现出来，这件作品创作于公元前2世纪，名为《荷马的神化》。在宙斯统治的山巅之上，缪斯女神和阿波罗环绕其间，荷马本人则庄严地端坐在左下角的宝座上。围绕他的还有拟人化的形象，其中包括欧亚大陆。

*('The Inhabited World'), who places a wreath on his head; Mythos ('Myth'), the boy immediately in front of him; and Historia ('History'), to the right of Mythos, on the other side of a sacrificial altar.*

> 《 inhabited world 》头上戴着花环；神话（Myth），他面前的男孩；以及历史（History），在神话的右侧，位于祭坛的另一侧。

No less profound in its resonance is the other great Homeric epic, the *Odyssey*. Once more there is a strong and quite specific narrative core, namely the events surrounding the return home to Ithaca, after the Greeks' victory at Troy, of the endlessly resourceful hero Odysseus. But this is not just a tale of monsters vanquished and foes at home – that is, the suitors of his wife Penelope – duly despatched. It is also an exploration of what it means to be civilized, as expressed in the treatment of beggars, suppliants and strangers; and it probes emotions which are anything but straightforward, especially the ambivalence of Odysseus, drawn towards many different women yet simultaneously to one only. If the *Iliad* gives us only occasional glimpses of the peace that forms the framework for war, the *Odyssey* evokes a firmer sense of that world of solid and enduring normality – embedded above all in the practices of agriculture and in relationships within the family – with which even the greatest hero must come to terms.

> 同样深刻的是另一部伟大的荷马史诗《奥德赛》。再次出现了一个强烈而具体的故事核心，即足智多谋的英雄奥德修斯在特洛伊战争后返回家乡伊萨卡的种种经历。但这不仅仅是一个关于打败怪物、惩治家中仇敌——也就是他妻子佩涅洛佩的追求者们——的故事。它还探讨了文明的含义，体现在对乞丐、求援者和陌生人的对待方式中；同时，它还深入挖掘了那些远非简单直接的情感，尤其是奥德修斯内心的矛盾心理：他被众多女性所吸引，却又同时只钟情于一人。如果说《伊利亚特》只是偶尔展现战争背后和平的背景，那么《奥德赛》则唤起了一种更为稳固的正常世界的感觉——这种世界根植于农业实践和家庭关系之中，即便是最伟大的英雄也必须与之和解。

How do we envisage these poems within their contexts? They were, of course, at some stage written down, when the need to preserve such acknowledged 'classics' was felt to be pressing. But epic poetry had a variety of performative contexts too; indeed, as we noted earlier, many features of Greek epic verse (for example its use of repeated 'formulae'), together with comparative evidence from modern societies where epic poetry is a living medium, have led scholars to conclude that the poems were, in all probability, originally composed orally. The Homeric poems themselves mention bards who sang heroic songs, to lyre accompaniment, at banquets in the residences of aristocrats and kings; it is by no means implausible that such contexts might have provided the setting for real-life performances of epic too. Performances at religious festivals are another likely context. In addition, we know that the Classical period saw professional reciters or 'rhapsodes' who performed excerpts from Homer in public competition. But smaller-scale settings also witnessed the performance of epic: Homer was said to have sung in a cobbler's shop and in the *lesche*. Add to all this the fact that ancient Greek schools placed considerable emphasis on pupils' learning to recite Homer aloud, and we can begin to get a picture of just how pervasive within society was the influence of these works of the poetic imagination.

> 我们应如何将这些诗作置于其具体语境中加以理解？这些诗作当然在某个时期被记录下来，当时人们感到迫切需要保存这些公认的“经典”作品。但史诗诗歌也有多种表演性语境；事实上，正如我们之前所提到的，许多希腊史诗诗歌的特点（例如其重复使用的固定句式），结合现代仍有史诗诗歌传统的社会的比较证据，使学者们得出结论：这些诗作很可能最初是口头创作的。荷马史诗本身提到了吟游诗人，他们在贵族和国王宅邸的宴会上，伴着里拉琴演唱英雄颂歌；显然，这样的场合很可能就是史诗真实表演的实际背景。宗教节日庆典的表演也是另一种可能的语境。此外，我们还知道古典时期出现了专业的吟诵者或“吟游诗人”，他们在公开比赛中朗诵荷马的片段。更小型的场合也见证了史诗的表演：据说荷马曾在鞋匠铺和莱舍中吟唱。再加上古代希腊学校非常重视学生大声背诵荷马史诗，我们便可以开始理解这些诗歌作品在社会中产生的影响是何等深远。

<span id="page-61-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_61_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Thanks to the authority conveyed by their staff and by their own eloquence, performers known as 'rhapsodes' held their audiences with recitations of epic verse. The painter of this vase (*c. *480 BC) has included an inscription spelling out what the reciter's words were: 'So once in Tiryns…', part of a hexameter line from a mythological narrative as yet unknown to us.*

> 凭借其权杖所传达的权威以及自身口才的魅力，被称为“吟诵者”的表演者通过吟诵史诗诗句而使观众着迷。这只陶罐（约公元前480年）的画家在陶罐上刻有一行铭文，详细说明了朗诵者的话语：“所以曾经在提尔尼亚……”，这是尚未被我们所知的神话叙事中的一行六音步诗。

<span id="page-62-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_62_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*One of the contexts in which knowledge of the poetic mythological tradition was reinforced was the school. Here (Attic cup,* c. *490 BC) a young man recites in front of his teacher, who follows the text on a papyrus roll.*

> 知识诗性神话传统的巩固之一发生在学校中。此处（雅典陶杯，约公元前490年）描绘了一位青年在老师面前诵读，老师则根据卷轴上的文字进行诵读。

Controversy continues about numerous aspects of Homeric poetry, not least whether the *Iliad* and the *Odyssey* were composed by one and the same poet, two poets, or many. But for the Greeks there was only one Homer – indeed he was referred to simply as 'the poet'. However, Homer was not the only great figure among the Archaic composers of epic verse (in the six-beat metre known as 'hexa-meter'). Regularly coupled with him was Hesiod, whose importance for the student of Greek mythology rivals that of Homer himself. Hesiod was said to have lived in the small village of Askra, near Mount Helikon in Boiotia. The two principal works of his to survive are the *Theogony* and the *Works and Days*; usually dated to the 8th/7th centuries BC, these compositions are regarded by some scholars as even earlier than the epics of Homer.

> 关于荷马史诗的诸多方面仍存在争议，尤其是关于《伊利亚特》和《奥德赛》是否由一位诗人创作、两位诗人创作还是多位诗人创作的问题。然而，对于古希腊人来说，只有一位荷马——事实上，人们简单地称他为“诗人”。但荷马并不是古希腊史诗诗人中唯一的杰出人物（这些诗人创作的是六音步诗体）。经常与荷马并列的是赫西俄德，他对于希腊神话研究的重要性可与荷马本人相媲美。据说赫西俄德生活在博伊奥蒂亚地区希利孔山附近的小镇奥斯克拉。他流传下来的两部主要作品是《神谱》和《工作与时辰》；通常被定为公元前8至7世纪的作品，但有些学者认为这些作品甚至比荷马史诗还要早。

<span id="page-63-0"></span>![](_page_63_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 忽略 <span> 标签，将以下英文文本翻译成中文：

*The interior of an Attic cup (*c. *460 BC) depicting a ritual celebration, with music, dancing and a burnt sacrifice. Poetry was often performed by a group of singers, who used mythology as a backdrop against which to explore personal experience.*

> 一只雅典陶杯（约公元前460年）的内壁描绘了一场仪式庆典，有音乐、舞蹈和焚烧祭品的情景。诗歌常常由一群吟游诗人演唱，他们以神话为背景，借此探讨个人经历。

As its name implies, the *Theogony* narrates the birth of the gods, tracing their origins back to the very beginnings of the universe; eventually, after a sequence of vicious struggles for succession, the Olympian divinities establish their power, under the at last unchallenged dispensation of Zeus. The *Works and Days* is a more varied and, if anything, an even more fascinating composition. The 'Works' referred to in the title are the labours which the peasant farmer must undertake if his crops are to survive and prosper; the 'Days' constitute a kind of informal calendar of the times within the year and the month when various practices – from ploughing to cutting one's nails – ought to be carried out. Weaving in and

> 正如其名称所暗示的，《神谱》叙述了诸神的诞生，追溯其起源至宇宙最初形成之时；最终，在一系列残酷的继位斗争之后，奥林匹斯神祇在宙斯无可挑战的统治下确立了自己的权威。《农事与农时》则是一篇更加丰富多样的作品，甚至可以说更加引人入胜。标题中所指的“农事”指的是农民必须从事的劳动，以确保作物的生长与繁荣；而“农时”则构成了一种非正式的年度及月份日程表，指示着各种活动——从耕种到剪指甲——应当在何时进行。这些内容穿插着……

out of these themes is a recurring series of references to the sacred, including mythology. It is in the *Works and Days* that two enormously influential myths receive their earliest and most authoritative narration: the Five Races of Mankind (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Heroes, Iron), and the tale of Pandora, the gods' lovely but baneful gift to humanity.

> 这些主题中包含着一系列反复出现的对神圣的提及，包括神话。在《工作与日子》中，两个极具影响力的神话首次且最具权威地被叙述：人类的五大种族（金、银、青铜、英雄、铁），以及潘多拉的故事——众神赐予人类的美丽却有害的礼物。

Was Hesiod's poetry, like that ascribed to Homer, designed for 'performance'? It seems virtually certain that it was. There will surely have been opportunities to sing in local contexts such as the *lesche*; but real fame could only come from performance to a larger audience. According to one unverifiable but plausible tradition, Hesiod won a prize for one of his sung compositions at a contest in Chalkis in the isle of Euboia (mod. Evia), just over the water from his Boiotian homeland. The contest took place at the funeral of a local nobleman, the type of occasion at which a community could celebrate and explore its relationship with the past, thanks to the privileged voice of poetry.

> 荷马的诗歌是否像归于荷马的诗作一样，是为“表演”而创作的？似乎可以肯定的是，它确实是这样。在诸如莱斯赫这样的地方性场合中，必定会有机会进行歌唱表演；但真正的名声只能来自向更广泛的观众群体进行表演。根据一种虽无法证实但合情合理的传统说法，赫西俄德曾在埃维亚岛（现代称为埃维亚岛）的哈利斯城举行的一次比赛中，因其一首歌唱作品而获得奖赏，而该城仅在赫西俄德的波奥提亚故乡的水域对岸。比赛是在一位当地贵族的葬礼上举行的，这种场合使社区能够通过诗歌这一特权声音，庆祝并探索自身与过去的关系。

### 4.3 **Choral song**

> 3.5.3 合唱歌曲

Homeric and Hesiodic poetry was performed by individuals. But another distinctive form of ancient Greek poetry was sung and danced by choirs. Such choral performances, to the accompaniment of the lyre, often had a competitive edge: we have particularly good evidence for competitions between choirs of young girls or young men. What differentiated this poetry from epic was the way in which, in contrast to the apparently more 'objective' voice of the epic narrator, the composer of choral lyric used mythology as a rich and resonant backdrop against which to explore personal experiences.

> 荷马与赫西俄德的诗歌是由个人吟诵的。但另一种独具特色的古希腊诗歌形式则是由合唱团吟唱并伴随着舞蹈表演。这类合唱表演通常伴有里拉琴的伴奏，往往带有竞争性质：我们尤其有充分的证据表明，少女合唱团和青年男子合唱团之间曾进行过此类比赛。这种合唱抒情诗与史诗的区别在于，与史诗叙述者似乎更加“客观”的声音不同，合唱抒情诗的作者将神话作为丰富的、富有共鸣的背景，借此探索个人的经历。

The context for such performances had a sacred aspect to it. We see this most clearly if we look at the type of choral lyric about which we know most, namely praise-poetry in honour of victors in the four great Games: at Nemea (northeastern Peloponnese), Isthmia (near Corinth), Delphi and Olympia. Each of these Games fell under the patronage of one of the gods: Poseidon at Isthmia, Apollo at Delphi, and Zeus at both Nemea and Olympia. Victorious competitors had their exploits celebrated in songs performed by a choir, whether at the site of the victory itself or in the victor's home community on his return.

> 此类表演的背景具有神圣的性质。如果我们最清楚地观察我们所知最多的合唱诗类型，即对四大竞技赛中优胜者的颂诗，就能看出这一点。这四大竞技赛分别是：奈梅亚（位于伯罗奔尼撒半岛东北部）、伊斯米亚（靠近科林斯）、德尔斐和奥林匹亚。每项竞技赛都由一位神祇庇护：伊斯米亚由波塞冬庇护，德尔斐由阿波罗庇护，而奈梅亚和奥林匹亚则由宙斯庇护。获胜的竞技者在战胜地或返回家乡社区后，其事迹都会被合唱团以歌曲的形式加以颂扬。

![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_65_Picture_0.jpeg)

*Delphi: the ancient stadium, rebuilt in the 2nd century AD, is set high on the hillside. Victors in the 'Pythian' Games, held in honour of Apollo, were praised in choral song; mythology provided poets with a repertoire of themes for such celebrations.*

> 德尔斐：这座古代体育场于公元2世纪重建，建在山丘高处。在为阿波罗举行的“皮提亚运动会”中获胜的选手会受到合唱赞歌的颂扬；神话为诗人提供了此类庆典的丰富主题。

<span id="page-65-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_65_Picture_2.jpeg)
