用户提供的英文文本是关于希腊神话的，请提供需要翻译的具体英文内容，我将为您翻译成中文。

*Olympia, home of the great Games in honour of Zeus; illustrated is the temple of Hera, the consort of Zeus. Athletes competed at the Games under the eyes of the gods. The achievements of the victors were immortalized in choral 'praisepoetry'.*

> 奥林匹亚，古代奥林匹克运动会的发源地，为宙斯的荣耀而举办；图中展示了赫拉神庙，赫拉是宙斯的妻子。运动员们在众神的注视下参加比赛。胜利者的成就被铭刻于合唱赞美诗中，永载史册。

Some of Greece's most illustrious poets were commissioned to immortalize such victories. The most famous of all was Pindar, who came from a village near Thebes in Boiotia. Pindar's rich and elaborate language bathed the physical prowess of contemporary athletes in the glow of their mythical antecedents. A typical example can be found at the beginning of his ode (*Pythian* 9) praising a victory in the race-in-armour at Delphi. The victor, whose name was Telesikrates, came from the north African city of Kyrene (see [p. 19\)](#page-28-0), founded by colonists from the island of Thera (mod. Santorini), *c*. 630 BC:

> 一些最杰出的希腊诗人受命将这些胜利永载史册。其中最著名的是品达，他来自波奥提亚地区底比斯附近的一个村庄。品达的丰富而华丽的语言，将当时运动员的体能置于其神话祖先的光辉之中。一个典型的例子可见于他颂扬德尔斐武装赛跑胜利的颂歌（《皮提亚颂》第9首）的开头。这位获胜者名叫泰莱西克勒特斯，来自北非城市基提翁（参见第19页），该城由来自圣托里尼岛（今称桑托里尼岛）的殖民者于约公元前630年建立。

I wish, with the aid of the deep-girdled Graces, to proclaim the bronze-shielded Pythian victor: Telesikrates, a fortunate man and a garland upon Kyrene, mistress of horses: Kyrene whom once, from the windswept folds of Pelion, Leto's son Apollo, god of the flowing hair, seized and carried away, a wild maiden, in his golden chariot. He made her dwell as queen in a country rich in flocks, and all fruits,

> 我希望借助深腰带的美惠女神们，宣告青铜盾牌的皮提亚冠军：特莱西克剌特斯，一位幸运之人，为凯吕瑞奈的桂冠所加冕，凯吕瑞奈是马的主宰。从前，从风化的佩洛尼亚山峦，阿波罗，赫利俄斯之子，流美发的神，曾掳走并带走这位野性的少女，乘着金色战车。他使她成为富饶牧畜与丰盛果品之国的女王。

blossoming and lovely, third root of the boundless earth.

> 盛开而美丽，无边大地的第三根。

Kyrene is at one and the same time a city and the homonym of a nymph loved by Apollo; the poet can thus borrow from the world of mythology images of beauty, wealth and fertility in order to enhance, by implication, the standing of the ordinary mortal Telesikrates.

> 基伦既是城市，也是阿波罗钟爱的宁芙的同名者；诗人因此可以从神话世界中借用美丽、财富和丰饶的意象，通过暗示来提升平凡凡人特莱西克勒特斯的地位。

<span id="page-66-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_66_Picture_3.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本。

*The magnificent ancient theatre at Epidauros, an exhilarating setting for staging drama. Thanks to its fine state of preservation and splendid location, the theatre is still regularly used for performances.*

> 位于埃皮达鲁斯的宏伟古代剧场，是上演戏剧的理想场所。由于其良好的保存状态和绝佳的地理位置，这座剧场至今仍被定期用于演出。

#### 4.3.1 **Drama**

> 3.5.3.1 戏剧

Of all the Greek media for the performative narration of myths, none was more distinctive or influential than drama, an art-form which has bequeathed to us some of ancient Greece's most magnificent architectural constructions. Although the origins of Greek dramatic representation are obscure, what is not in doubt is that it was towards the end of the 6th century BC, in the city of Athens, that the theatre developed its characteristic features. Thespis, the dramatist who was linked with this formative era, is hardly more than a name to us (though he has given us the word 'thespian'), but his successors Aischylos, Sophokles and Euripides (in tragedy) and Aristophanes (in comedy) span the next hundred years like mighty colossi of the imagination.

> 在所有希腊用于戏剧性叙述神话的媒介中，戏剧无疑是最具特色且影响深远的。作为一种艺术形式，戏剧为我们留下了古代希腊最辉煌的建筑成就。尽管希腊戏剧表演的起源尚不明确，但可以确定的是，戏剧在公元前6世纪末期的雅典发展出了其特有的特征。与这一形成时期相关联的剧作家忒斯庇斯（Thespis）如今对我们而言几乎只留下一个名字（尽管他为我们留下了“thespian”这个词），但他的继承者埃斯库罗斯（Aeschylus）、索福克勒斯（Sophocles）和欧里庇得斯（Euripides）在悲剧领域，以及阿里斯托芬（Aristophanes）在喜剧领域，如同想象中的巨像，跨越了接下来的百年时光。

<span id="page-68-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_68_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the specific English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Sophokles, the great Athenian tragedian, composed more than 120 plays, of which just 7 have survived complete. But they are some of the most powerful dramas ever written, including* Antigone, Elektra*, and* Oedipus Tyrannos. *This bronze portrait head conveys an appropriate blend of insight and humane sympathy.*

> 索福克勒斯是伟大的雅典悲剧作家，他创作了120多部戏剧，但仅有7部完整保存下来。这些作品是历史上最有力的戏剧之一，包括《安提戈涅》《厄勒克特拉》和《俄狄浦斯王》。这尊青铜头像展现了一种恰到好处的融合，既包含洞察力，又体现出人性的同情。

Their plays were put on in the Theatre of Dionysos, on the south slope of the Athenian Acropolis. Each year, in spring, around 15,000 people assembled for several days at the City Dionysia, a festival in honour of the god Dionysos. Three tragedians each put on a tetralogy of plays, consisting of three tragedies followed by a 'satyr play'. In a separate event, five comic dramatists each put on a single work. A jury of citizens, chosen by lot, voted to determine the winner in each competition. The context was both religious and civic: in the centre of the front row of the audience sat the priest of Dionysos, presiding over these astonishing examples of communal self-exploration by the citizen group.

> 他们的戏剧在狄奥尼索斯剧场上演，该剧场位于雅典卫城南坡。每年春季，约有15,000人聚集在城 Dionysia 节上数日，这是一场献给酒神狄奥尼索斯的节日。三位悲剧作家各自上演一套四联剧，包括三部悲剧和一部“萨提尔剧”。在另一场活动中，五位喜剧作家各自上演一部作品。由抽签选出的公民陪审团投票决定每场竞赛的胜者。这一活动兼具宗教与公民性质：在观众前排中央位置坐着狄奥尼索斯祭司，他主持着这些令人惊叹的公民群体集体自我探索的实例。

<span id="page-69-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_69_Picture_0.jpeg)

> </think>

该文本的上下文涉及希腊神话，将以下英文文本翻译成中文：

*The Theatre of Dionysos, below the Athenian Acropolis. It was here that the great tragedies and comedies were staged, in competitions witnessed by an audience of many thousands. In the centre of the front row sat the priest of Dionysos, embodying the god's keen interest in the dramatic events being represented.*

> 狄奥尼索斯剧场，位于雅典卫城下方。这里曾上演伟大的悲剧和喜剧，举行着数以万计的观众见证的比赛。前排正中坐着狄奥尼索斯祭司，体现着神对戏剧事件的浓厚兴趣。

Tragedy, satyr play and comedy shared at one level a common set of conventions: a few masked actors took the principal individual roles, while a chorus, also masked, provided continuity, commentary, and a more 'collective' perspective on the action. Other aspects of form were common to the three genres: dance and song, to the accompaniment of the *aulos* (double pipe), were crucial to the effect and the meaning. But the generic differences were as great as the similarities.

> 悲剧、萨提尔剧和喜剧在某种程度上共享一套共同的惯例：少数戴面具的演员扮演了主要的个人角色，而同样戴面具的合唱队则提供了连贯性、评论以及更“集体”的视角。其他形式方面的要素在三个戏剧类型中也普遍存在：舞蹈和歌曲伴随着双管乐器阿乌洛斯（aulos）的伴奏，对戏剧的效果和意义至关重要。但三大戏剧类型之间的差异同样显著。

*Tragedy:* Tragedies dramatized events from the mythical past. Tales of the Trojan War and its aftermath figured large, as in Aischylos' *Oresteia* trilogy; *Philoktetes, Aias* and *Elektra* by Sophokles; and the *Trojan Women, Hekabe* and *Iphigeneia at Aulis* of Euripides. But the exploits of other mythical heroes and heroines were replayed with equal power, notably the events surrounding Herakles (Sophokles' *Women of Trachis*; Euripides' *The Madness of Herakles*) and the family of Oedipus (as in the two *Oedipus* plays by Sophokles, and the same playwright's *Antigone*). More rarely, but with no less dramatic effect,

> 悲剧：悲剧戏剧化了神话时代的事件。特洛伊战争及其后续故事占据了重要地位，如埃斯库罗斯的《俄瑞斯忒亚》三部曲；索福克勒斯的《菲洛克忒忒斯》、《阿喀琉斯》和《埃勒克特拉》；以及欧里庇得斯的《特洛伊妇女》、《赫卡柏》和《阿ulis的伊菲格enia》。但其他神话英雄和女英雄的传奇故事同样被戏剧化，例如赫拉克勒斯的事件（如索福克勒斯的《特拉奇斯的妇女》；欧里庇得斯的《赫拉克勒斯的疯狂》）以及俄狄浦斯家族（如索福克勒斯的两部《俄狄浦斯》戏剧，以及该剧作家的《安提戈涅》）。更少见的是，但同样具有戏剧效果，

episodes illustrating the relationships between the gods sprang into theat-rical life before the eyes of the Athenian populace: the *Prometheus Bound* ascribed to Aischylos is one example. Thanks to these and the other extant plays (over thirty tragedies have survived intact), we can still be unnerved by the horror, and be moved to tears by the pity, of the explorations of the human condition to which this art form gave expression.

> 在雅典民众眼前，描绘诸神关系的戏剧片段进入了戏剧生活：埃斯奇勒斯所作的《普罗米修斯的锁链》便是其中之一。正因为这些戏剧以及其它幸存下来的作品（现存三十多部悲剧），我们仍能被这种艺术形式所展现的人类境遇的探索所引发的恐惧所震撼，被其中的怜悯之情所感动，潸然落泪。

#### 4.3.2 *Laurence Olivier as Oedipus, in a 1945 production of the great Sophoklean tragedy* Oedipus Tyrannos.

> 3.5.3.2 劳伦斯·奥利弗饰演俄狄浦斯，在1945年上演的索福克勒斯悲剧《俄狄浦斯王》中。

*Satyr play:* In satyr play the representation of myth is strikingly different. Satyrs are wild, lewd, snub-nosed followers of Dionysos who are usually depicted with the ears and tails of horses. Cowardly and grotesque, they overturn the *status quo*, bursting in upon mythological narratives and reducing them to burlesque or farce. Unfortunately only two examples have survived more or less intact: Sophokles' *Trackers* and the *Cyclops* of Euripides. The flavour of the latter play – a reworking of the episode in which Odysseus outwits the one-eyed giant Cyclops – is given by a brief dialogue between the satyrs' old leader Silenos and the drunken and ominously frisky monster. Such is the effect on the Cyclops of the wine which Odysseus has given him that he mistakes the satyrs for the lovely Graces, and Silenos for Zeus' boy-love Ganymede:

> 萨梯罗斯戏剧：在萨梯罗斯戏剧中，神话的表现方式截然不同。萨梯罗斯是酒神狄俄尼索斯的随从，他们野性、轻浮，塌鼻梁，通常被描绘成拥有马耳和马尾的生物。他们怯懦而滑稽，颠覆传统，闯入神话故事，将其降格为滑稽剧或闹剧。不幸的是，只有两部作品基本完整地流传下来：索福克勒斯的《追踪者》和欧里庇得斯的《独眼巨人》。后者剧作的风格——对奥德修斯智斗独眼巨人这一情节的重新演绎——通过萨梯罗斯旧首领西伦与醉酒且充满危险活力的怪物之间的简短对话得以体现。奥德修斯给独眼巨人喝下的酒对后者产生了如此强烈的影响，以至于他将萨梯罗斯误认为美丽的美惠女神，将西伦误认为宙斯的少年爱侣甘尼美底。

*Cyclops* The Graces are tempting me! That's enough! With this Ganymede I'll get better relief than with the Graces! And anyway, I prefer boys to females. *Silenos* Am I Zeus' little Ganymede, Cyclops? *Cyclops* You are, by Zeus! The boy I stole from the land of Dardanos! *Silenos* I'm done for, lads. Dreadful things await me.

> 独眼巨人喀戎，美惠三女神在引诱我！够了！用这个伽倪摩多斯，我会比用美惠三女神得到更好的慰藉！再说，我更喜欢男孩而不是女孩。西伦，我是不是宙斯的小伽倪摩多斯，喀戎？喀戎，你就是，宙斯作证！我从达达诺斯之地偷来的男孩！西伦，我完了，伙计们。可怕的命运正等待着我。

<span id="page-72-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_72_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*The satyr play, in which Dionysos' grotesque and sex-obsessed followers played a central role, evoked on a wine cup (*c. *500–480 BC); Dionysos plays the lyre.*

> 萨提尔戏剧，其中狄奥尼索斯的怪诞且性欲狂热的追随者们扮演了核心角色，描绘于酒杯上（约公元前500–480年）；狄奥尼索斯弹奏里拉琴。

*Comedy:* Comic drama shows us a number of different faces in antiquity, from the riotously imaginative fantasies of 'Old Comedy' – whose most famous exponent was Aristophanes – to the more measured intricacies of plot and character to be found in the plays of Menander. So far as we can tell from surviving texts, the world of mythology impinges little on the theatre of Menander. But in Aristophanes, and in the many vase paintings apparently inspired by Old Comedy, we find traditional myths exploited, and turned upside down and inside out, for comic effect. Sometimes the intention is parodic, as when, in Aristophanes' *Women at the Thesmophoria*, Euripides' tragic version of

> 喜剧：喜剧戏剧向我们展示了古希腊时期多种不同的面貌，从充满狂野想象力的“旧喜剧”（其最著名的代表人物是阿里斯托芬）到米南德戏剧中更为严谨复杂的剧情与人物刻画。根据现存的文本，我们所能了解到的是，神话世界对米南德戏剧的影响微乎其微。但在阿里斯托芬的作品中，以及许多显然受旧喜剧启发的陶罐绘画中，我们却发现了传统神话被加以利用，并被彻底颠覆，以达到喜剧效果。有时这种意图是讽刺性的，例如在阿里斯托芬的《节庆妇女》中，对欧里庇得斯悲剧版本的改编就体现了这一点。

a myth concerning the hero Telephos is mercilessly sent up. At other times there is no such specific allusion: it is just that mythological figures, whether heroic or divine, cheerfully rub shoulders with individuals drawn from contemporary Athenian life. A typical example is Aristophanes' exuberant and lyrical play *Birds*, in which two ordinary Athenians, fed up with life in their home city, set out to find a better future in Cloudcuckooland; along the way they meet Tereus, who used to be a figure from mythology but, because of the unspeakable crimes he committed, was changed into a bird – in fact, into a hoopoe (see [p. 154](#page-349-0)).

> 关于英雄忒勒福斯的神话被无情地提及。在其他时候则没有如此具体的暗示：神话中的英雄或神祇人物，愉快地与来自当时雅典现实生活中的普通人并肩而行。一个典型的例子是阿里斯托芬充满活力且抒情的戏剧《鸟》，其中两个普通的雅典人厌倦了家乡的生活，决定前往“云 cuckooland”寻找更好的未来；在旅途中，他们遇到了特瑞斯，他原本是神话中的人物，但由于犯下了不可言说的罪行，被变成了鸟——确切地说，变成了一只环斑鸠（参见第154页）。

<span id="page-74-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_74_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*A common theme in Greek comedies is that of fantastic transformation. Here a vase-painter has depicted an actor dressed up as a cock.*

> 希腊喜剧中常见的一个主题是奇幻的变形。这里，一位花瓶画家描绘了一位装扮成公鸡的演员。

Our brief look at the three contrasting dramatic genres demonstrates once more that the world of Greek mythology is not closed or fixed or dominated by an orthodoxy, but mobile and fluid, responsive to the varying demands of context. Nowhere is this clearer than in the juxtaposition of two portrayals of Dionysos at the end of the 5th century. In Aristophanes' *Frogs* (405 BC) the god appears in cowardly and absurd guise, as he makes his riotously funny way down to Hades on his quest to bring the poet Euripides back up to the world of the living. Few of those whom he meets treat Dionysos with much respect ('Sit here, Fatty! …And stop fooling about!' bawls Charon, ferryman of the dead). Yet in Euripides' own

> 我们对三种对比鲜明的戏剧类型的简要考察再次表明，希腊神话的世界并非封闭、固定或受正统教条所主导，而是充满活力且不断变化的，能够根据不同的语境需求而变化。 nowhere 比这更明显 than 在公元前五世纪末对酒神狄俄尼索斯的两种形象的并置。在阿里斯托芬的《蛙》（公元前405年）中，这位神祇以懦弱荒诞的形象出现，他荒诞可笑地一路向下到冥界，试图将诗人欧里庇得斯带回人间。他所遇到的大多数人对他都不甚尊重（“坐这儿，胖子！……别胡闹了！”冥界摆渡人卡戎怒吼道）。然而，在欧里庇得斯本人的作品中，

*Bacchae* – staged soon after the poet's death, and thus separated from *Frogs* by just a few months – Dionysos is the terrifying punisher of the hapless King Pentheus, who denied the divinity of the irresistible newcomer to the ranks of the Olympian gods. The only traces of grim humour here reside in Dionysos' sadistic toying with his victim, who is eventually torn limb from limb by his own mother and her sisters.

> 《酒神女祭司们》——该剧在诗人去世后不久便上演，因此与《蛙》仅相隔数月——酒神狄奥尼索斯是那个拒绝承认不可抗拒的新神跻身奥林匹斯众神之列的不幸国王彭忒西勒亚的可怕惩罚者。此处唯一存在的阴森幽默，只在于狄奥尼索斯对受害者进行残忍的戏弄，最终受害者被自己的母亲及其姐妹肢解而死。

<span id="page-76-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_76_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*The spectacular theatre of Segesta in western Sicily seen from the air. Its magnificent situation provided the backdrop for plays by the great tragedians and comedians of ancient Greece.*

> 从空中俯瞰，位于西西里岛西部的塞杰斯塔壮观的剧院。其壮丽的地理位置为古希腊著名悲剧作家和喜剧作家的戏剧提供了背景。

### 4.4 **Written myths**

> 3.5.4 书面神话

Many of the performative contexts which we have just reviewed continued throughout antiquity to provide frameworks for oral mythological narration. To take an obvious example: one aspect of the extraordinary expansion of Greek culture in the wake of the conquests of Alexander the Great consisted in the building of *poleis*, 'cities'; within each *polis* the theatre was one of the most characteristic constructions; and with these theatres went the dramas represented in them, dramas whose original Athenian focus did not inhibit the subsequent, centuries-long, panhellenic popularity of the medium. But alongside such oralperformative contexts there began to develop more specifically literary settings for the retelling of the traditional stories. Signs of this process can be detected already in the Archaic period, but it gained momentum in Hellenistic times, particularly in the culturally intense atmosphere of the hellenized Egyptian city of Alexandria.

> 我们刚刚回顾的许多表演性语境在古代一直持续存在，为口头神话叙述提供了框架。举一个明显的例子：亚历山大大帝征服后希腊文化非凡扩展的一个方面，体现在城邦（poleis）的建设上；在每个城邦中，剧场是最具代表性的建筑之一；这些剧场伴随着其中上演的戏剧，这些戏剧最初以雅典为中心，但并未妨碍其后数个世纪内戏剧媒介在全希腊范围内的流行。但与此同时，也逐渐出现了更具体的文学性语境，用于重新讲述传统故事。这一过程的迹象早在古代时期就已经显现，但在希腊化时期，尤其是在文化氛围浓厚的希腊化埃及城市亚历山大城中，这一趋势得到了进一步加强。

We may distinguish three main ways in which material from the world of mythology was subjected to such literary reworking. First, we have many examples of texts which, although composed in the same genres as earlier, orally

> 我们可以区分神话世界中的素材受到这种文学再加工的三种主要方式。首先，我们有许多文本的例子，尽管它们的体裁与以往相同，但却是口头创作的。

performed myth-narrations, seem to have been primarily designed as texts-to-beread. An instance would be the *Argonautica* ('Expedition of the Argonauts'), the four-book epic poem composed in the 3rd century BC by Apollonios of Rhodes. The metre (dactylic hexameters), many features of the language, and numerous aspects of the plot, demonstrate that this intricate poem was a deliberate renewal of Homer's *Iliad* and *Odyssey*, aimed at a cultured and literate public which could appreciate not only the echoes of Homer, but also the countless, subtle divergences from the path which that great predecessor had trodden. There are also many similarities to and differences from the work of other poets who had handled the story of Jason and Medea before Apollonios, notably Pindar (in his 4th *Pythian Ode*) and Euripides (in his tragedy *Medea*). This complex process of allusion is described by some modern literary critics as 'intertextuality'.

> 进行神话叙述的作品似乎主要被设计为供阅读的文本。一个例子是《阿尔戈船英雄纪》（即“阿尔戈船远征”），这是公元前3世纪由罗德斯的阿波罗尼奥斯创作的四卷史诗。该诗采用抑扬六步调，语言的许多特征，以及情节的诸多方面，都表明这首复杂的诗作是有意复兴荷马的《伊利亚特》和《奥德赛》，旨在面向文化素养较高的、能够欣赏荷马作品回响的读者群体，同时也能够体会该诗作对荷马所走路径的无数微妙偏离。此外，该诗也与此前其他诗人处理伊阿宋和美狄亚故事的作品有许多相似之处和差异，尤其是品达的第四首皮提亚颂歌和欧里庇得斯的悲剧《美狄亚》。一些现代文学评论家将这种复杂的引用过程描述为“互文性”。

Secondly, we can trace the emergence of what seem to be entirely new genres of written mythological narrative. Once more, a decisive role was played by hellenized Alexandria. Two works may be taken as exemplifying the development to which we are referring. In his *Metamorphoses*, Antoninus Liberalis (probably 2nd century AD) retells in succinct prose the outlines of tales in which mortals and gods underwent miraculous transformation. Antoninus' collection lacks the literary exuberance and virtuosity which characterizes the greatest re-teller of such stories, namely the Roman poetic genius Ovid; but it does illustrate the fact that, at the periods when Antoninus and his Hellenistic sources were writing, there were reading publics keen to revisit the ancient tales of metamorphosis. Equally spare and unelaborate is the compilation which has come down to us under the name of the *Library of Mythology* attributed to one Apollodoros (date uncertain: some scholars have suggested 2nd century BC, others more plausibly 2nd century AD). This time there is no detectable thematic basis to the selection, as there was in the case of Antoninus' tales of transformation: Apollodoros relates, in bare outline, a narrative which indeed purports to be 'the complete world of Greek mythology'! For all its lack of pretension to literary quality, Apollodoros' compendium still repays re-reading, not least because its repeated signalling of the variant versions of any particular story reminds us of the cardinal fact that there was no one, single, orthodox way of retelling a given mythical episode.

> 其次，我们可以追溯到一些看似完全新颖的书面神话叙事体裁的出现。再次，希腊化时期的亚历山大城发挥了决定性的作用。两部作品可以作为我们所指的发展过程的典型例子。在《变形记》中，安托尼努斯·利比里乌斯（可能为公元2世纪）以简洁的散文重述了关于凡人与神灵经历奇迹般转变的故事梗概。安托尼努斯的集子缺乏像罗马诗人奥维德那样伟大的故事重述者所具有的文学华丽与技巧，但确实说明了在安托尼努斯及其希腊化来源所处的时代，存在一群热衷于重新阅读古代变形故事的读者群体。同样简略而未经修饰的是以阿波罗多洛斯（年代不详：一些学者推测为公元前2世纪，另一些人则更合理地认为是公元2世纪）之名流传下来的神话集。这次，所选内容没有明显的主题基础，正如安托尼努斯的变形故事所具有的那样：阿波罗多洛斯仅以简略的轮廓叙述了一个确实声称要展现“整个希腊神话世界”的故事！尽管缺乏对文学质量的自负，阿波罗多洛斯的这部简编仍值得反复阅读，尤其是其反复提示任何特定故事的不同版本，提醒我们，对于某个特定神话情节的重述，从没有一种单一、正统的方式。

A third type of text is perhaps the most significant of all, in terms of what it reveals about the location of myths within Greek culture. The authors of such texts do not so much retell myths as implicitly or explicitly explore the boundaries of 'the mythical', especially in opposition to a category such as 'the verifiable'; this opposition is sometimes expressed as a contrast between *muthos*

> 第三类文本或许在揭示神话在希腊文化中的位置方面最为重要。这类文本的作者并不主要是复述神话，而是隐含或明确地探讨“神话”这一范畴的边界，尤其是在与“可验证的”这一类别相对立时；这种对立有时被表达为“神话”与“可验证的”之间的对比。

*–* whence our term 'myth' – and *logos*, a term whose meanings extend from 'word' and 'story' to 'reason'. Some of the most spectacular works of imaginative and intellectual brilliance to have survived from classical antiquity conduct precisely this kind of boundary exploration. These works are conventionally assigned to the categories of 'history' and 'philosophy'.

> ——我们的“神话”一词便源于此——以及“logos”（λόγος），一个其含义从“词”和“故事”延伸至“理性”的术语。一些最具想象力和智慧的、从古典时代流传至今的杰作，正是在进行这种边界探索。这些作品传统上被归类为“历史”和“哲学”两大类。

#### 4.4.1 **Historians**

> 3.5.4.1 历史学家

To illustrate the various kinds of approach to 'the mythical' which historians could adopt, we may cite two of the very greatest: Herodotos and Thucydides. The project which Herodotos (5th century BC) sets himself in his *Enquiries* is to narrate, and to explain the causes of, the Greco-Persian Wars, fought in the first quarter of the 5th century BC. As part of the background to these violent events, Herodotos refers to earlier episodes, set in the time of myth, when Greeks and those whom they called 'barbarians' (i.e. non-Greek-speakers) came into conflict. What is important, and utterly typical of the way in which most Greeks viewed accounts of their mythical past, is that Herodotos follows a double strategy: on the one hand, he includes mythology within the evidence which a historian should evaluate; on the other hand, he subjects mythology to constant scrutiny from the point of view of its verifiability and plausibility. At the very beginning of his account, for instance, he mentions certain mythical heroines who were abducted by one side or the other – Io by Phoenicians (i.e. 'barbarians'), Europa by Greeks, then Medea by Greeks, and Helen by the 'barbarian' Paris. Herodotos presents such individual acts as forerunners of the later Greco-Persian wars. The myths are neither dismissed out of hand as 'mere' stories, nor regarded as repositories of a sacred and untouchable truth. They are simply more grist to the historian's mill. Herodotos implies that he accepts the historicity of these heroes and heroines, but passes in silence over some of the more extravagant details of their stories – such as Zeus' metamorphosis into a bull in order to abduct Europa.

> 为说明历史学家在面对“神话”这一概念时可能采取的各种不同方法，我们可以引用两位最杰出的史学家：希罗多德和修昔底德。希罗多德（公元前5世纪）在《历史》一书中，旨在叙述并解释公元前5世纪初爆发的希波战争的起因。在这些激烈冲突的背景中，希罗多德提到了更早时期的一些事件，那时希腊人与他们称为“蛮族”（即非希腊语使用者）之间曾发生冲突。重要的是，这体现了大多数希腊人看待其神话过去的方式：希罗多德采取了双重策略。一方面，他将神话纳入历史学家应评估的证据之中；另一方面，他又从可验证性和可信度的角度不断审视神话。例如，在他叙述的开篇，他提到某些神话中的女英雄被一方或另一方劫持——伊俄被腓尼基人（即“蛮族”）劫走，欧罗巴被希腊人劫走，接着是美狄亚被希腊人劫走，海伦则被“蛮族”帕里斯劫走。希罗多德将这些个人行为视为后来希波战争的前奏。这些神话既不被轻易视为“仅仅是”故事，也不被看作神圣不可触碰的真理。它们仅仅是为历史学家提供更多的素材。希罗多德暗示，他接受这些英雄和女英雄的历史真实性，但他却对一些更为夸张的故事情节保持沉默——例如宙斯为了劫走欧罗巴而变成公牛的传说。

<span id="page-79-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_79_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*In the Hellenistic period the kings of Pergamon in Asia Minor aspired to outdo the cultural achievements of Athens and Alexandria. The theatre at Pergamon is perfectly designed to fit the curving slope of the citadel.*

> 在希腊化时期，小亚细亚的帕加马王国的国王们渴望超越雅典和亚历山大城的文化成就。帕加马的剧院精心设计，完美契合城堡的弯曲坡道。

Tales of gods and heroes occupy but a tiny part of the historical narrative of Herodotos' great successor, Thucydides (second half of 5th century BC), who recorded and attempted to make sense of the Peloponnesian War. But in the early part of the narrative, where Thucydides uses 'ancient history' in order to show how much greater the Peloponnesian War was than any previous conflict, he does, for once, look back to mythology. Even more decisively than Herodotos had done, he examines his data with an eagle eye on its plausibility. Indeed, in his practice as a historian Thucydides claims explicitly to reject *to muthodes* ('the mythical', 'the story-like'): 'it may well be that my history will seem less easy to listen to because of the absence in it of *to muthodes*' – a barely disguised dig at Herodotos.

> 关于众神和英雄的故事仅占修昔底德（公元前5世纪后半叶）——这位赫罗多德的伟大继承者——历史叙述中微不足道的一部分。他记录并试图理解伯罗奔尼撒战争。但在叙述的早期部分，为了说明伯罗奔尼撒战争比以往任何冲突都更为重要，修昔底德使用“古代历史”这一概念，首次回溯到神话。他比赫罗多德更坚决地审视其资料的可信性。事实上，修昔底德在历史实践中明确声称拒绝“神话”（即“故事性”）：“我的历史或许会显得不易聆听，因为其中缺乏‘神话’”——这几乎是对赫罗多德的隐晦讽刺。

Yet even Thucydides accepts without demur the historicity of the Trojan War; it is just that, careful historian as he is, he subjects some of the claims made in the Homeric poems to the sceptical judgment of *Realpolitik* ('It was, I am inclined to think, because Agamemnon was the most powerful ruler of his day that he was able to assemble the expedition, and not so much because the suitors of Helen had bound themselves by the oaths which they had sworn to Tyndareos'). Once again,

> 然而，修昔底德甚至不加质疑地接受了特洛伊战争的历史真实性；只是作为一位严谨的历史学家，他将荷马史诗中的一些说法置于现实政治的怀疑性判断之下。（“我认为，之所以能够组织这次远征，是因为阿伽门农是当时最强大的统治者，而非因为海伦的求婚者们曾向廷达罗斯起誓所绑定的誓言。”）同样地，

myths are neither dismissed as pure fiction, nor accepted as unchallengeable gospel.

> 神话既不被视为纯粹的虚构，也不被视为不可置疑的真理。

### 4.5 **Philosophers**

> 3.5.5 哲学家

Just as historians conducted a dialogue with the world of mythology, so too did those we call 'philosophers'. One common approach to the history of ancient Greek philosophy has been to present it as the story of a select group of intellectual pioneers who wrestled with, and progressively worked towards overthrowing, the mythico-religious environment within which they and their fellows were living. The first of these pioneers are known collectively as 'Presocratics' – individual speculative thinkers, from a variety of different *poleis* in the Greek world, active in the 6th and early 5th centuries BC (hence the name: 'before Sokrates'). One such thinker is Xenophanes, in whose reported opinion the gods of Homer and Hesiod were immoral, 'stealing and committing adultery and deceiving each other'. More radically, Xenophanes argued that not just certain kinds of all-too-human divine behaviour, but the very nature of these anthropomorphic gods, must be called into question, if one reflects that humans formulate their ideas of gods in their own (human) image – Ethiopian gods being snub-nosed and black, Thracian gods having blue eyes and red hair. An even more impressive voice raised against traditional mythology (according to this same story of pioneers) is that of Plato (5th/4th century BC), who also railed against the immoralities of the divinities depicted by Homer and Hesiod, setting against such unworthy tales a more exalted and more absolute conception of divinity, to which one might hope to gain access through the power of dialectical argument. Ultimately there came Aristotle (4th century BC), whose intellectual priorities of empirical observation and logical argumentation could find no room for the fantasies of poets and other purveyors of tales.

> 正如历史学家与神话世界展开对话一样，我们称之为“哲学家”的人也如此。古代希腊哲学史的一个常见观点是，将其呈现为一群思想先驱者的故事，他们努力与并逐步试图推翻他们及其同辈所处的神话宗教环境。这些先驱者中的第一群被称为“前苏格拉底时期的思想家”——来自希腊世界不同城邦的个体思辨者，活跃于公元前6世纪至公元前5世纪初（因此得名“前苏格拉底”）。其中一位思想家是色诺芬尼，在他所报告的观点中，荷马与赫西俄德笔下的诸神是不道德的，“偷窃、通奸并欺骗彼此”。更激进地说，色诺芬尼认为，不仅某些过于人性化的神的行为需要质疑，就连这些拟人化神的本性本身，如果人们意识到人类是按照自己的形象（人类的形象）来构建神的概念的话，那么埃塞俄比亚的神会被描绘成鼻子扁平、肤色黝黑，色雷斯的神则有蓝色眼睛和红色头发。根据这一群先驱者的故事，反对传统神话的更有力声音是柏拉图（公元前5世纪至公元前4世纪），他也反对荷马与赫西俄德所描绘的神祇的不道德行为，以一种更加崇高的、绝对的神的概念来对抗这些不配的传说，人们或许能通过辩证法的力量获得这种神的观念。最终，亚里士多德（公元前4世纪）出现了，他的思想优先考虑经验观察与逻辑论证，这些都不容许诗人和其他讲故事的人所传播的幻想存在。

This story is a misleading simplification. It is certainly the case that a series of brilliant thinkers – amongst them, to be sure, the Presocratics, Plato and Aristotle – did define central aspects of their intellectual positions in opposition to the traditional myths. However, first, these innovative intellectual strategies had little discernible impact on more widely held, socially pervasive attitudes towards stories of gods and heroes – these speculations were, that is to say, by and large unrepresentative. Secondly, these very thinkers were, to varying degrees, themselves intellectually implicated in the assumptions underlying the traditional tales. Many of the beliefs of such Presocratic philosophers as Empedokles and Pythagoras might with as much justice be described as 'mystical' or 'magical' as 'philosophical' ('I walk amongst you all an immortal god', wrote Empedokles,

> 这个故事是一种误导性的简化。毫无疑问，一系列杰出的思想家——包括前苏格拉底哲学家、柏拉图和亚里士多德——确实在反对传统神话的基础上，定义了他们思想立场的核心方面。然而，首先，这些创新性的思想策略对更广泛、更普遍存在的社会态度，即人们对神祇和英雄故事的看法，几乎没有明显的影响——换句话说，这些推测基本上并不具有代表性。其次，这些思想家本身在不同程度上也参与了传统故事背后假设的思维。像恩培多克勒斯和毕达哥拉斯这样的前苏格拉底哲学家的许多信念，同样可以被恰当地描述为“神秘的”或“魔法的”，而非仅仅是“哲学的”。“我以不朽之神的身份与你们同在”，恩培多克勒斯写道。

'no longer a mortal'). Plato, for his part, was well aware of the contribution which stories, *muthoi*, could make towards the quest for truth, provided one remembered that such stories were not in themselves absolutely true. Such is the spirit in which he relates the 'myth of Er' at the end of his dialogue *The Republic*: this myth, speculating as it does on the fates of various types of mortal after death – and on the way in which the souls of the deceased could choose the sort of life into which they were to be reborn – ventures into territory inaccessible to the more orthodox procedures of Platonic dialectical argumentation. Er allegedly came to life again on his funeral pyre, and proceeded to relate the experiences which he had had in the Other World.

> “不再是凡人”。柏拉图本人深知，故事（神话）对追求真理能够做出的贡献，只要记住这些故事本身并不绝对真实。他在《理想国》一书的结尾讲述“埃鲁神话”时，正是秉持着这样的态度：这个神话探讨了不同凡人在死后命运的种种可能，以及亡灵如何选择他们重生入何种生命形式，因而涉及了柏拉图式辩证论证方法所难以触及的领域。据称，埃鲁在自己的葬火中再次获得生命，并讲述了他在另一个世界所经历的事情。

As for Aristotle, it is true that his writings on science and logic defer even less to mythology than do the investigations of Thucydides. Nevertheless, Aristotle concedes that the evidence of myth-tellers may from time to time need to be taken into account: indeed, given the Aristotelian belief that history is cyclic, the writings of the *muthologoi*, 'myth-tellers', may preserve a record of earlier explanations of which the contemporary scientist/philosopher needs to be aware. And we should remember too that even an Aristotle listened to tales as a child, handled all manner of mythologically decorated vessels, learned Homer at school, and went to the theatre, composing, in due course, the most influential single treatise ever written about the genre, namely *Poetics*, in which his brilliant analysis of tragedy turns on the role within it of the plot – which in Greek is designated by that astonishingly versatile term, *muthos.*

> 至于亚里士多德，确实他的关于科学和逻辑的著作比修昔底德的研究更少依赖神话。然而，亚里士多德承认神话讲述者的证据有时仍需加以考虑：事实上，鉴于亚里士多德认为历史是循环的，神话讲述者的作品可能保存了早期的解释，而当代的科学家或哲学家需要了解这些内容。我们也应记得，即使是亚里士多德，小时候也曾听故事，接触过各种装饰有神话图案的器皿，学校里学习荷马史诗，还去剧院观看戏剧，最终写出了有史以来关于这一文学体裁最具影响力的专著——《诗学》。在这部著作中，他精妙的悲剧分析着重于情节在其中所起的作用，而情节在希腊语中正是用那个出奇灵活的词“muthos”来表示的。

<span id="page-82-0"></span>![](_page_82_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the specific English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*These antique portrait heads give majestic – albeit idealized – form to four of Greece's greatest intellects. From top left to bottom right: the historians Herodotos and Thucydides, and the philosophers Plato and Aristotle.*

> 这些古希腊雕像头像以庄严而理想化的方式呈现了希腊最伟大的四位智者。从左上到右下：历史学家希罗多德和泰格利阿斯，以及哲学家柏拉图和亚里士多德。

## 5 *Plato Attacks Traditional Mythology*

> 3.6 柏拉图批判传统神话

In this extract from the *Republic*, Sokrates and his interlocutor Adeimantos discuss how undesirable it would be if some of the traditional tales about the gods were to be told in the ideal city:

> 在《理想国》这一段节选中，苏格拉底与其对话者阿德曼托斯讨论了如果某些关于诸神的传统故事在理想城邦中被讲述，将会是多么不理想的事情：

*Sokrates* First of all, it appears, we ought to supervise the

> 苏格拉底 首先，看起来，我们应该监督

myth-tellers, making sure that only noble stories are selected, while the rest are rejected. We shall

> 神话讲述者确保只选择高尚的故事，而其余的则被拒绝。我们将

persuade nurses and mothers to relate the selected stories to their children, so moulding them spiritually rather than corporally. Of the stories told at present, we must reject the

> 说服护士和母亲向他们的孩子讲述选定的故事，从而在精神上而非肉体上塑造他们。对于目前讲述的故事，我们必须摒弃那些……

majority.

> 多数

*Adeimantos* Which stories do you mean?

> 阿德伊曼托斯，您指的是哪几个故事？

*Sokrates* In the greater myths we shall be able to see the

> 苏格拉底 在更伟大的神话中，我们将能够看到...

lesser also; for the form and effect of the greater and lesser should be identical, don't you think?

> 较小的也；因为更大者和较小者的形态与效果应当相同，你难道不这么想吗？

*Adeimantos* Yes, I do. But I don't know what these 'greater'

> 阿德伊曼托斯 是的，我确实如此。但我不清楚这些“更伟大的”...

myths are that you're referring to.

> 这些神话就是你所指的。

*Sokrates* The ones which Hesiod, Homer and the other

> 苏格拉底，赫西俄德、荷马以及其他人的作品

poets used to tell us. For these people, I

> 诗人曾告诉我们。对于这些人，我

suppose, used to – and still do – compose false

> 假设，过去常常——并仍然如此——编造虚假的。

stories and relate them to mankind.

> 神话故事，并将它们与人类联系起来。

*Adeimantos* Which stories? And what objection do you have

> 阿德伊曼托斯，哪些故事？你有什么异议吗？

to them?

> 给他们？

*Sokrates* An objection which must be lodged

> 苏格拉底：一个必须提出的反对意见

emphatically at the outset, especially if the false

> 一开始就要明确强调，尤其是当涉及虚假时

tale is also an ugly one.

> 这个传说也是一件令人不快的事。

*Adeimantos* How do you mean?

> 阿德伊曼托斯，你是怎么想的？

*Sokrates* I mean when a story-teller makes a bad likeness

> 苏格拉底，我的意思是指当一个故事讲述者做出不准确的描绘时。

of the nature of the gods and heroes, like a painter making an inaccurate representation.

> 诸神与英雄的本质，如同画家不准确地描绘。

*Adeimantos* That's certainly something one ought to object

> 阿德伊曼托斯，这当然是一个应该反对的事。

to. But exactly what kinds of things are we

> 到。但是我们到底在谈论什么种类的事情呢？

talking about?

> 在说啥？

*Sokrates* Well, on the most important subject concerning

> 苏格拉底：那么，关于最重要的议题涉及...

the most important individuals, there was the ugly tale narrating the deeds which Hesiod ascribed to Ouranos, and the vengeance taken by Kronos upon him. As for the things which Kronos did and suffered at the hands of his son, even supposing they were true, these matters should not, I think, be carelessly related to those who are young and lacking in judgment. Rather, they should be passed over in silence; or, if they must be told, they should be communicated to a very few people in the context of secret mysteries, at a rite involving the sacrifice not just of a pig, but of an animal both large and difficult to procure. That way, such stories will be heard by the fewest people possible.

> 在这些最重要的个体中，有一个讲述希佩索斯归于乌拉诺斯的丑陋故事，以及克洛诺斯对他所采取的报复。至于克洛诺斯所做的以及在他儿子手下所遭受的事情，即使这些事情属实，我认为也不应随意向那些年轻且缺乏判断力的人讲述。最好是将其在沉默中略过；或者，如果必须讲述，应仅在秘密仪式的背景下，向极少数人传达，这种仪式不仅献祭猪，还要献祭一头大型且难以获取的动物。这样，这些故事将尽可能少地被听到。

*Adeimantos* These stories are certainly tricky to deal with.

> 阿德伊曼托斯说这些故事无疑难以处理。

*Sokrates* And they should not, Adeimantos, be told in our city.

> 苏格拉底，阿德曼图斯，他们不应该在我们的城邦中被提及。

The Platonic Sokrates goes on to rule out as unsuitable the myth of the Battle of the Gods and Giants, as well as all kinds of other feuds between divinities. A wonderful storyteller himself, Plato is only too well aware of the power, for good or ill, of imaginative narratives about divine and heroic exploits.

> 柏拉图笔下的苏格拉底继续排除掉神与巨人之战的神话，以及诸神之间各种其他纷争。作为一位出色的讲故事者，柏拉图深知富有想象力的叙述对于神祇与英雄的壮举所具有的影响，无论好坏。

# 4 <span id="page-85-0"></span>[II Myths of Origin](#page-6-6)

> 第四章第二节 起源神话

<span id="page-85-2"></span><span id="page-85-1"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_85_Picture_1.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，以便我进行翻译。

*Aphrodite rises from the sea-foam, born from the severed genitals of the sky-god Ouranos. The extraordinary sensuality of the drapery has made this marble relief into an icon of female beauty (the 'Ludovisi Throne'; perhaps part of an altar, mid-5th century BC).*

> 阿佛洛狄忒从海沫中升起，诞生于天空之神乌拉诺斯被割下的生殖器。衣褶所展现出的非凡性感使这件大理石浮雕成为女性美的象征（“路多维西宝座”；或许曾是祭坛的一部分，约公元前五世纪中叶）。

#### 0.1.1 *Stories of the Beginning*

> 4.1.1.1 混沌初开的故事

The quest for origins is universal. Just as individuals will seek to establish a sense of their own rootedness by tracing their ancestry, so social groups, communities, and even whole civilizations legitimize and authenticate their place in the scheme of things by making claims about where they came from. One of the most effective

> 探索起源是普遍的。正如个人通过追溯自己的族谱来寻找自身的根基，社会群体、社区，甚至整个文明也会通过宣称自己的来源来证实和确认自己在宇宙秩序中的地位。其中最有效的一种方式是……

ways of staking such claims is through myths; for a myth can seem to ground a claim to legitimacy in the natural order of things, tracing the merely contingent present back to a beginning in a more authoritative and 'definitive' era. But it is not just the social order of whose origin myths speak. They also reach back to the origin of the human race, and of the cosmos itself.

> 确立此类主张的方式是通过神话；因为神话似乎能够为合法性主张在自然秩序中提供依据，将偶然的当下追溯到一个更具权威性和“最终性”时期的开端。但神话所谈论的不仅仅是社会秩序的起源。它们还追溯到人类种族的起源，以及宇宙本身的起源。

The Greeks told many stories about beginnings. Some stories narrated the origins of particular cities or social groups; some the origins of humanity ('anthropogony'); some took a further step backwards, to ask about the origins of the gods ('theogony') or of the universe itself ('cosmogony'). All these kinds of myths will be discussed in this chapter. We begin with myths which explore the ultimate question: how did the cosmos originate?

> 希腊人讲述了许许多多关于起源的故事。一些故事讲述了特定城市或社会群体的起源；一些故事讲述人类的起源（“人类起源神话”）；一些故事则更进一步追溯，探讨神的起源（“神的诞生”）或宇宙本身的起源（“宇宙起源神话”）。本章将讨论所有这些类型的神话。我们将从探讨宇宙起源这一终极问题的神话开始。

### 0.2 <span id="page-87-0"></span>*[Cosmogony](#page-6-7)*

> 4.1.2 宇宙起源

Accounts of the origin and development of the universe can be found in many cultures throughout the world; and our contemporary, 'scientific' world has its own way of telling such stories through cosmological theory. Like the peoples of other cultures, the ancient Greeks told their own tales about the beginnings of all that is. Such tales do not bulk large within the whole corpus of Greek mythology, which devotes more attention to the deeds of divinities and heroes in the already constituted world. But occasionally speculation does reach back to the ultimate beginning. A striking example is found in one of the earliest surviving Greek literary works.

> 关于宇宙起源和发展的记载可以在世界上许多文化中找到；而我们当代的“科学”世界则通过宇宙学理论以自己的方式讲述这类故事。如同其他文化的人民一样，古希腊人也讲述他们自己关于万物起源的故事。这类故事在希腊神话的整个体系中并不占重要地位，因为希腊神话更关注已成形世界中众神和英雄的作为。但偶尔，推测也会追溯至终极的起源。一个鲜明的例子可见于最早幸存的希腊文学作品之一。

### 0.3 **Hesiod's** *Theogony*

> 4.1.3 赫西俄德的神谱

To profess to know about the ultimate beginning represents a claim to more-thaneveryday knowledge. In ancient Greece, poets – inspired by the Muses – were one group who might stake such a claim. The earliest surviving example of Greek cosmogonical speculation is Hesiod's poem *Theogony* (8th/7th century BC). Its themes are violent strife, desire, and the ultimate victory of the Olympian divinities under the sovereignty of Zeus, who is regarded as still holding sway in the poet's own day. We shall examine this complex narrative in some detail.

> 宣称了解终极起源意味着声称拥有超越日常的知识。在古希腊，受缪斯启发的诗人是可能提出此类主张的一群人。最早幸存的希腊宇宙起源推测例子是赫西俄德的《神谱》（公元前8至7世纪）。其主题包括激烈的斗争、欲望，以及在宙斯统治下奥林匹斯神祇最终的胜利，而宙斯在诗人所处的时代仍被认为掌握着权力。我们将详细探讨这一复杂的叙述。

At the beginning of everything there was Chaos; this does not mean 'Disorder', but rather 'Chasm', in the sense of a dark, gaping space. Afterwards came Gaia ('Earth') and Eros ('Sexual Love'), the principle whose existence is a prerequisite for all the subsequent acts of procreation by which the cosmos gradually became populated. From these three (with the possible addition, according to one reading of Hesiod's text, of Tartaros, a grim and terrifying region below the Earth) there originated all that exists.

> 万物之初，唯有混沌；这并非指“混乱”，而是指“深渊”，意指一片黑暗而深邃的虚空。随后出现的是盖亚（Earth，即“大地”）和厄洛斯（Eros，即“情欲”），后者是所有后续繁衍行为的前提，正是这些行为使宇宙逐渐被填满。从这三者（根据赫西俄德文本的一种解读，可能还包含塔尔塔罗斯，即位于大地之下的阴森恐怖之地）中，诞生了世间一切存在。

![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_88_Figure_0.jpeg)

![](_page_88_Figure_1.jpeg)

*\* On an alternative reading of Hesiod, the Chimaira's mother was the Hydra.*

> 在对赫西俄德的一种不同解读中，奇美拉的母亲是海德拉。*

Next there came into being more of the spatial and temporal features which define the world. Chaos generated Erebos (a dark, infernal realm similar to Tartaros) and Night who, after union with Erebos, produced Aither (something between 'Light' and 'Air') and Day. Gaia produced, without union, Ouranos ('Sky'), who would be a kind of permanent, matching covering for her; out of herself she also brought forth Mountains and Pontos ('Sea'). More significant from the point of view of the later development of the story are Gaia's children by Ouranos. These included a race of divinities known as the Titans, amongst whom were Okeanos (i.e. Ocean, the river which encircles the world) and Kronos, youngest and most devious of Gaia's offspring, as well as the mighty one-eyed Cyclopes, forgers of the thunderbolt, and the three monstrous Hundred-Handers, each with fifty heads and one hundred arms springing from his shoulders.

> 随后，更多定义世界的空间和时间特征相继产生。混沌生成了埃雷波斯（一个类似塔尔塔罗斯的黑暗、地狱般的领域）和夜，夜与埃雷波斯结合后，生出了阿伊瑟（介于“光”与“空气”之间的存在）和白昼。盖亚在没有结合的情况下独自生下了乌拉诺斯（天空），他将成为她的一种永恒匹配的覆盖；她自己也生出了山脉和蓬托斯（海洋）。从后来故事发展的角度来看，盖亚与乌拉诺斯所生的子女更为重要。这些子女中包括了一支被称为泰坦的神族，其中有奥 keanos（即环绕世界的海洋河流）和克洛诺斯（盖亚最年幼且最为狡诈的子女），还有强大的独眼巨人——雷霆锻造者，以及三个可怕的百臂巨人，每个都有五十个头和一百只手臂从肩上生长出来。

<span id="page-89-1"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_89_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Okeanos was the great river that flowed around the earth, and also the source of all fresh waters. In his human form as a bearded old man, Okeanos became a particularly familiar image in Roman times. This illustration is from the centre of a large silver salver from the Mildenhall treasure, 4th century AD*.

> 奥克亚诺斯是环绕地球流淌的大河，也是所有淡水的源头。在罗马时期，他以一位蓄着胡须的老人的人形形象尤为为人熟悉。这幅插图出自公元4世纪米德尔顿宝藏中的一件大型银盘中心部分。

#### 0.3.1 <span id="page-89-0"></span>**The gods' succession**

> 4.1.3.1 神的继位

The scene is now set for one of the poem's central themes: a violent struggle for succession. Gods do not voluntarily surrender power, nor does that power diminish with age. They are thus locked into a seemingly unwinnable war with their divine offspring: the immovable against the irresistible. However, the *Theogony* narrative shows that older deities can be circumvented by younger ones, provided the latter show enough ingenuity and daring. So it was with Ouranos and Kronos.

> 场景已为诗歌的一个核心主题铺开：一场激烈的继承权争夺战。神祇不会自愿交出权力，也不会因年岁增长而自动削弱权力。因此，他们陷入了看似无法取胜的战争中，与自己的神祇后代对抗：不可动摇者对抗不可抗拒者。然而，《神谱》的叙述表明，只要年幼的神祇展现出足够的机智和胆识，年长的神祇便可能被绕过。乌拉诺斯与克洛诺斯便是如此。

Ouranos sought to prevent his offspring by Gaia from emerging into the light: he hid them away in a recess of Gaia/Earth as each was born. This act of blocking is a counterpart to another aspect of Ouranos' behaviour, namely his desire for unremitting intercourse with Gaia – which is another way of saying that, in the time 'before', Earth and Heaven were an indissoluble whole.

> 奥洛诺斯试图阻止由地神所生的子女进入光明，他每生下一个孩子就将他们藏在地神/大地的隐秘处。这种阻隔的行为与奥洛诺斯的另一个行为特征相对应，即他渴望与地神不断交合——这另一种说法是在“之前的时代”，大地与天空是一个不可分割的整体。

It took an act of calculated, horrific violence on the part of one of Ouranos' sons to alter this situation. As Ouranos spread himself once more in the act of intercourse, Kronos sprang out of hiding and cut off his father's genitals with a sickle made of adamant (an unimaginably strong substance); the act of separation was sealed as Kronos hurled the severed genitals away. In spite of Kronos' act, Ouranos' ability to procreate was not yet quite exhausted. The prodigious fertility of the combined semen-and-blood could not fail to generate new life. Wherever drops fell from the cast-away flesh, they inseminated Gaia: this is how the Furies (Erinyes), Giants and Nymphs (the Meliai) came into being. As for the genitals themselves, they created the goddess Aphrodite, who distilled in her own nature the sexuality implicit in the manner of her conception.

> 改变这一局面需要奥洛诺斯的一个儿子采取了一次经过精心策划的、极其残忍的暴力行为。当奥洛诺斯再次在交配过程中展开身体时，克洛诺斯从藏身之处跃出，用由钻石制成的镰刀割下了他父亲的生殖器；克洛诺斯将割下的生殖器抛掷出去，从而完成了分离的举动。尽管克洛诺斯采取了这一行动，奥洛诺斯的繁殖能力仍未完全枯竭。由结合了精液和血液所产生的巨大生育力，必然孕育出新的生命。当被扔掉的肉体上的液体滴落之处，便使大地女神盖亚受孕：这就是复仇女神（厄里尼厄斯）、巨人和仙女（梅利亚）的诞生。至于那些生殖器本身，则创造了爱与美的女神阿佛洛狄忒，她自身便蕴含着其受孕方式中所隐含的性欲。

## 1 *The Birth of Aphrodite*

> 4.2 阿芙罗狄忒的诞生

<span id="page-91-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_91_Picture_1.jpeg)

> The text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*The skill of the sculptor of the 'Ludovisi Throne' (see [pp. 42](#page-85-1)[–43](#page-85-2)) brings out Aphrodite's sweet desirability.*

> “路多维西宝座”雕塑家的技艺展现了阿佛洛狄特甜美可人的魅力。（见第42–43页）

About [the genitals] a white foam grew from the immortal flesh, and in it a girl formed. First she approached holy Kythera; then from there she came to sea-girt Cyprus. And out stepped a modest and beautiful goddess, and the grass began to grow all round beneath her slender feet. Gods and men call her Aphrodite, because she was formed in foam [*aphros*], and Kythereia, because she approached Kythera, and Cyprus-born, because she was born in wave-washed Cyprus, and laughter-loving [*philommeides*], because she appeared out of genitals [*medea*]. Eros and fair Desire attended her birth and accompanied her as she went to join the family of gods. And this has been her allotted province from the beginning among men and immortal gods: the whisperings of girls; smiles; deceptions; sweet pleasure, intimacy, and tenderness.

> 关于生殖器，不朽之躯上生长出白色的泡沫，其中形成了一位女孩。她首先来到神圣的基塞拉，然后从那里前往环海的塞浦路斯。一位谦逊而美丽的女神现身，她的纤细脚踝下，四周的草木开始生长。众神与凡人称她为阿佛洛狄忒，因为她诞生于泡沫（aphros）；又称为基塞里亚，因为她接近基塞拉；又称为塞浦路斯所生，因为她诞生于波涛拍打的塞浦路斯；又称为爱笑的（philommeides），因为她从生殖器（medea）中现身。爱神厄洛斯与美丽的欲望伴随她诞生，并陪伴她前往奥林匹斯神族。自古以来，她在这群人和不朽神祇中被赋予的领域便是：少女的低语；微笑；欺骗；甜蜜的愉悦，亲密与温柔。

(from Hesiod's *Theogony)*

> 出自赫西俄德的《神谱》

#### 1.1.1 **Hesiod's** *Theogony***: genealogy**

> 4.2.1.1 赫西俄德的《神谱》：家谱

The separation of Ouranos and Gaia inaugurated a definitive change: henceforth, the imprisoned Titans could emerge to wield their power and to produce their own progeny. Before reaching that culminating part of his narrative, however, Hesiod

> 乌拉诺斯和盖亚的分离标志着一个决定性的转变：此后，被囚禁的泰坦神族得以现身，施展他们的力量，并繁衍自己的后代。然而，在抵达他叙述的高潮部分之前，赫西俄德...

fills in another part of the genealogical matrix, by listing the offspring (and their offspring) of two of his primeval beings, Night and Pontos/Sea. In the case of both sets of descendants a clear 'mythical logic' is at work.

> 通过列举其两位原始神灵——夜（Nyx）和海（Pontos）的后代（及其后代），补充了谱系结构的另一部分。在两组后代的情况中，清晰的“神话逻辑”正在起作用。

Amongst Night's grim progeny were Doom, Death, Blame and Misery (because the night is gloomy); Sleep and Dreams and Deceit and Sexual Affection (because they belong at night); the Fates (because one's fate is accomplished at death, which also belongs to Night); and Discord, who in her turn generated such ineluctable aspects of existence as Hunger, Pains, Battles, Slaughterings and Quarrels.

> 在黑夜的阴森子嗣中，有厄运、死亡、责备与苦难（因为黑夜昏暗）；有睡眠、梦境、欺诈与情欲（因为它们属于夜晚）；有命运（因为人的命运在死亡时实现，而死亡也属于黑夜）；还有纷争（她又产生了诸如饥饿、痛苦、战斗、屠杀与争吵这样不可避免的存在方面）。

From Pontos/Sea descend many of the creatures, lovely or monstrous, that populate the world, especially its waters. One of Pontos' sons, Nereus, the righteous Old Man of the Sea, fathered fifty lovely sea-nymphs in union with Doris, a daughter of Okeanos. Thaumas ('Marvellous'), another son, enjoyed a similar liaison with Elektra; among his children were the wind-swift Harpies and Iris, divine messenger and goddess of the rainbow. A third son of Pontos, Phorkys, lay with his own sister Keto ('Sea Monster'), and together they generated a troop of monstrous creatures: the Graiai (women old already at birth), the Gorgons, and probably also (the text is uncertain) cave-dwelling Echidna, nymph above the waist and serpent below it; Echidna in her turn would count among her descendants the Chimaira and the Sphinx; for it is a regular pattern within Greek mythological genealogy that a monstrous liaison will generate a monstrous progeny.

> 从潘托斯/海中诞生了许多充斥着世界，尤其是水域的生物，它们或美丽或怪异。其中，潘托斯的儿子涅柔斯，这位正直的海之老人，与海洋之神奥刻anos的女儿多里斯结合，生下了五十位美丽的海仙女。另一位儿子塔玛斯（意为“奇妙的”），则与伊莱克特拉结为伴侣；他的子嗣中有迅捷如风的花岗石鸟和彩虹女神伊里斯。潘托斯的第三位儿子福尔科斯，与自己的妹妹克托（海怪）结合，共同孕育了一群怪异的生物：灰眼睛女妖、蛇发女妖格莱娅，以及可能还有（文本不确定）居住洞穴的奇美拉蛇女妖；而奇美拉蛇女妖的后代中又包括了火龙奇美拉和狮身人面兽斯芬克斯。在希腊神话的谱系中，这种怪异的结合往往会产生怪异的后代。

<span id="page-93-0"></span>![](_page_93_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，以便我为您进行翻译。

*The old sea-god Nereus, son of Pontos, moves about his domain mounted on a 'hippocamp', a marvellous, hybrid creature of the deep. Attic cup,* c*. 520 BC*.

> 古老的海神内瑞斯，波塞冬之子，骑乘着“海马”，在自己的领地内游走。雅典陶杯，约公元前520年。

The narrative now renews contact with the Titans – that is, with the descendants of Ouranos and Gaia. Okeanos, the river that surrounds all, lay with his sister-Titan Tethys: their offspring were rivers great and small, and the nymphs, said to number 3000. Hesiod's catalogue of the names of some of them illustrates the kind of incantatory magic which a 'mere' list can work on a hearer:

> 叙述现在重新与泰坦巨人建立联系——也就是乌拉诺斯和盖亚的后代。环绕一切的俄刻阿诺斯与他的姐妹泰坦巨人泰希斯结合，他们的子嗣包括大小河流以及据说有三千之众的宁芙。赫西俄德列举的一些宁芙的名字目录，展示了“单纯”列表对听者所产生的咒语般的魔力：

Peitho and Admete, Ianthe and Elektra, Doris, Prymno, and godlike Ourania, Hippo, Klymene, Rhodeia, and Kallirhoe, Zeuxo, Klytia, Idyuia, and Pasithoe, Plexaura and Galaxaura, and lovely Dione, Melobosis, Thoe, and fair Polydora…

> 佩索、阿德梅特、伊安忒、伊莱克特拉、多里斯、普里姆诺、奥兰尼亚、希波、克吕米涅、罗迪亚、卡利洛伊、泽乌索、克吕提亚、伊迪乌亚、帕西托、普莱克苏拉、加拉克苏拉、狄俄涅、梅洛波西斯、理俄、波利多拉……

By no means all these nymphs have a perceptible connection with water (see pp. 184–85). One who does [is Styx, the stream whose waters take on fearsome powers](#page-413-0) [under the regime of Zeus; she it is by whom the gods swear their oaths \(see pp. 208–](#page-457-0) 9).

> 并非所有这些仙女都与水有明显的联系（见第184–85页）。其中有一位是斯提克斯，即那条在宙斯统治时期其河水具有可怕威力的溪流；众神正是通过她发誓立约（见第208–9页）。

#### 1.1.2 **Hesiod's** *Theogony***: the birth of Zeus**

> 4.2.1.2 赫西俄德的神谱：宙斯的诞生

The Titans inhabited a time which was both 'before' and transitional – a time when the world's physical framework was still being created. Thus when two Titans, Theia and Hyperion, lay together, their children were Helios ('Sun'), Selene ('Moon'), and Eos ('Dawn'); in her turn Eos gave birth to the winds and stars. Two other pairs of Titans bring the Hesiodic narrative to the brink of the Olympian era. The offspring of Phoebe and Koios is Leto, soon to bear Apollo and Artemis. More significant still is the union of Rheia and Kronos, which heralds the next stage in the cosmicdynastic struggle for succession.

> 泰坦们所处的时代既属于“之前”，又处于过渡期——一个世界物理框架仍在被创造的时代。因此，当泰坦神忒亚和赫利俄斯结合时，他们的孩子是赫利俄斯（太阳）、塞勒涅（月亮）和厄俄斯（黎明）；随后，厄俄斯又生下了风与星辰。另外两对泰坦则将赫西俄德的叙述推向了奥林匹斯时代的边缘。菲贝与科奥斯的孩子是勒托，她即将生育阿波罗和阿尔忒弥斯。更为重要的是瑞亚与克洛诺斯的结合，这标志着宇宙王权继承斗争的下一阶段。

To Kronos Rheia bore Hestia ('Hearth'), Demeter, Hera, Hades, Poseidon and, finally, Zeus. Whereas Ouranos had tried to block succession by thrusting his offspring back into Gaia, Kronos' tactic was to swallow his children whole: since he could not kill immortal beings, he ingested them, having learned from Ouranos and Gaia to fear overthrow at the hands of his own child. In her grief at seeing her successive newborns disappear, Rheia sought the advice of her parents – the same Ouranos and Gaia. On their suggestion she took herself to Crete when she was about to bear Zeus, her youngest. In a cave – Hesiod locates it at Lyktos, but other sources speak of Mount Dikte or Mount Ida – she bore her son. To fool the voracious Kronos, she wrapped a stone in swaddling clothes; Kronos duly devoured this 'child' too. Soon Zeus' force grew. He made Kronos vomit up everything that he had swallowed: first the stone (which Zeus placed at Delphi 'to be a monument in time to come, and an object of wonder for mortal men'), then the children.

> 瑞亚为克洛诺斯生下了赫斯提亚（炉灶）、德墨忒尔、赫拉、黑德斯、波塞冬，最后是宙斯。与乌拉诺斯试图通过将子女重新放回大地母亲盖亚体内来阻止继承权不同，克洛诺斯采取的策略是将子女整个吞食下去：因为他无法杀死不朽的神灵，便学会了从乌拉诺斯和盖亚那里得知害怕被自己的孩子推翻，于是选择吞食他们。看到接连出生的孩子一个个消失，瑞亚悲痛欲绝，于是向她的父母——也就是乌拉诺斯和盖亚寻求建议。在即将生下宙斯，也就是她最小的孩子时，她听从父母的建议前往克里特岛。她在一座山洞中生下儿子——荷马将其地点定在利克托斯，但其他资料则提到迪克特山或伊达山。为了欺骗贪婪的克洛诺斯，她将一块石头裹上襁褓；克洛诺斯照做，将这“孩子”也吞了下去。不久后，宙斯的力量日益壮大。他让克洛诺斯吐出所有曾吞下的东西：先是那块石头（宙斯将其放置在德尔斐，以作为将来的一个纪念碑，以及凡人惊叹的对象），然后是他的孩子们。

<span id="page-94-0"></span>![](_page_94_Picture_3.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，以便进行翻译。

*Mount Ida (mod. Mount Psiloritis) in Crete was one of the places where tradition located the birth of Zeus. At 2,500 m (8200 ft) in height it is one of the most dramatic features of the Cretan landscape.*

> 克里特岛上的伊达山（现代名为普西洛里蒂斯山）是传统上认为宙斯诞生的地方之一。该山海拔2500米（8200英尺），是克里特岛景观中最引人注目的特征之一。

<span id="page-95-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_95_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*One of the most striking ancient representations of the Kronos myth is this marble relief (2nd century AD). Rheia offers the wrapped stone to Kronos – who believes the wrapping to contain his baby son Zeus. The figures' calm expressions belie the savagery surrounding this episode: Kronos devoured his children one by one in order to prevent them from usurping his power.*

> 最引人注目的古代对克洛诺斯神话的表现之一是这件大理石浮雕（公元2世纪）。瑞亚将裹着的石头献给克洛诺斯——克洛诺斯认为包裹中包含他的婴儿儿子宙斯。人物平静的表情掩盖了这一情节中的残酷：克洛诺斯为了防止他们夺取自己的权力，逐一吞噬了自己的孩子们。

#### 1.1.3 **Hesiod's** *Theogony***: the power of Zeus**

> 4.2.1.3 赫西俄德的《神谱》：宙斯的力量

The remainder of the *Theogony* details the means by which Zeus definitively established his power. First he outwitted Prometheus, son of the Titan Iapetos (see [pp. 54–](#page-110-1)[56](#page-115-0)). Then, with the formidable Hundred-Handers as his allies, Zeus led the Olympians against the Titans in a battle of truly cosmic proportions: 'the indescribable flame reached the holy sky, and the brilliant glare of the thunderbolt and the lightning dazzled the eyes of even the strongest'. Defeated at last, the Titans

> 神谱的其余部分详细描述了宙斯如何最终确立自己的权力。首先，他智胜了提坦巨人伊阿佩托斯之子普罗米修斯（参见第54–56页）。接着，宙斯率领奥林匹斯神众，联合强大的百臂巨人，与提坦巨人展开了一场真正具有宇宙规模的战争：“难以形容的火焰直抵神圣的天空，雷霆的耀眼光芒和闪电使就连最强壮的人也目眩神迷。”最终，提坦巨人被击败。

were confined in Tartaros; once more, the notion of imprisonment replaces that of killing, for immortals cannot die. Even now Zeus' power was under threat, this time from the monstrous Typhoeus, Gaia's child by the grim Tartaros (who is a person as well as a place):

> 他们被囚禁在塔尔塔罗斯；再次，囚禁的概念取代了杀死的概念，因为永生者无法死亡。即使如今，宙斯的力量也正受到威胁，这次来自可怕的堤丰，他是盖亚与阴森的塔尔塔罗斯（塔尔塔罗斯既是地名，也是一位神祇）所生的怪物：

Out of his shoulders there emerged a hundred terrifying snake-heads with black tongues licking, and the eyes in the strange heads flashed fire under the brows; and there were voices in all the monstrous heads, emitting every kind of inexpressible sound.

> 从他的肩上涌现出一百个可怕的蛇头，黑舌舔舐，奇异的头颅中眼睛在眉间迸发出火焰；所有怪物般的头颅中都发出声音，发出各种无法形容的声响。

Typhoeus too eventually succumbed to the thunderbolt, to be imprisoned in Tartaros (or, in some accounts, under Mount Etna).

> 泰丰斯也最终被雷电击毙，被囚禁在塔耳塔罗斯（或在某些记载中，被囚禁在埃特纳火山下）。

In a final genealogical section Hesiod outlines the dynastic marriages by which Zeus consolidated his rule. His first wife was Metis ('Crafty Intelligence'). When Zeus learnt that the son whom she would bear after her firstborn Athene would rule over gods and men, he took the by now obvious step of *swallowing* his wife, which meant that the soon-to-be-born Athene would be born from Zeus himself (in fact, born out of the top of his head, split open with an axe wielded by, according to different versions, Hephaistos or Prometheus). Succession was now definitively blocked, and the liaisons which Zeus contracted henceforth would produce children who would generally act in support of, rather than in opposition to, the broad framework of his rule. From Themis ('Due Order') he produced the three lovely Horai ('the Seasons)', by name Eunomia ('Lawfulness'), Dike ('Right') and Eirene ('Peace'), and also the three Moirai ('Fates'), namely Klotho, Lachesis and Atropos; by Eurynome, an Oceanid, he had Charites ('the Graces'): Aglaia, Euphrosyne and Thaleia; the Muses came from Zeus' dalliance with Mnemosyne ('Memory').

> 在最后的谱系部分，赫西俄德概述了宙斯通过王室联姻巩固统治的举措。他的第一位妻子是墨提斯（“精明智慧”）。当宙斯得知墨提斯将要生下的儿子（在她第一个孩子雅典娜之后）将统治众神与人类时，他采取了显然明智的举措，吞下了自己的妻子。这样一来，即将诞生的雅典娜便直接从宙斯本人（实际上是用赫菲斯托斯或普罗米修斯用斧头劈开他的头颅，从他的头顶诞生）诞生。至此，继承权已被彻底阻断，此后宙斯所缔结的婚恋关系所产生的子女，通常都会支持而非反对他统治的总体框架。从忒弥斯（“秩序”）那里，他生下了三位可爱的季女神（即“四季”），名字分别为欧诺米亚（“秩序”）、狄刻（“正义”）和厄尔忒涅（“和平”），同时还生下了三位命运三女神（即“命运”），即克洛托、克洛泰和阿特罗波斯；从欧律诺美（一位海洋女神）那里，他生下了三位卡里忒斯（即“美惠女神”），即阿格莱亚、欧福罗斯尼亚和塔莉亚；缪斯女神则源于宙斯与谟涅摩叙涅（“记忆”）的恋情。

Other unions led to the arrival of major new Olympian divinities: Apollo and Artemis (from Leto), Ares (from Hera), Hermes (from Maia, daughter of Iapetos' son Atlas). Hephaistos would emerge out of Hera alone as a kind of tit-for-tat for Zeus-born Athene (though the Homeric version gives Hephaistos' parents as, less anomalously, Zeus and Hera together). As for Dionysos, his perceived role as a latecomer is expressed by the fact that his mother is Semele, a mortal – which raises the issue of human beings (see [pp. 53;](#page-104-0) [54\)](#page-110-1).

> 其他结合导致了主要的新奥林匹斯神祇的诞生：阿波罗和阿耳忒弥斯（出自莱托），阿瑞斯（出自赫拉），赫尔墨斯（出自玛雅，伊阿佩托斯之子阿特拉斯的女儿）。赫菲斯托斯则会独自从赫拉诞生，作为对宙斯所生的雅典娜的一种报复（尽管荷马版本中赫菲斯托斯的父母是宙斯和赫拉共同所生，这显得不那么异常）。至于狄俄尼索斯，他被视为后来者，这一点体现在他的母亲赛墨勒是凡人，这引发了关于人类的问题（见第53页；54页）。

The establishment of Zeus' power is now complete. There is no suggestion that his is a temporary regime: in this conception of the state of the world (and Hesiod's view is here typical of that expressed by most Greeks), no 'twilight of the gods' is envisaged, on the pattern of the Norse idea of Ragnarök (the day of the destruction of the world, when the great god Odin would be swallowed up by the terrifying wolf Fenrir). In the Hesiodic scheme, Zeus' is the sovereignty, and Zeus' it will remain.

> 宙斯权力的建立现已完成。并无暗示他的统治是临时的：在这一对世界状态的理解中（赫西俄德的观点在此体现了大多数希腊人的看法），并未预示出‘诸神黄昏’的景象，这与北欧神话中的‘诸神黄昏’概念相仿（即世界毁灭之日，伟大的神奥丁将被恐怖的狼芬里尔吞噬）。在赫西俄德的体系中，宙斯拥有统治权，且这一统治权将永续不变。

<span id="page-97-0"></span>![](_page_97_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 忽略 <span> 标签，包含思考步骤在 </think>

用户提供的英文文本是关于希腊神话的，请提供需要翻译的具体英文内容，我将为您翻译成中文。

*The birth of Athene from the head of Zeus (black-figure vase, 6th century BC). The warrior goddess is armed, ready to take her place among the assembled Olympians. Perched on Zeus' throne is an owl, the inscrutable symbol of Athene and Athens.*

> 雅典娜从宙斯的头颅中诞生（黑绘陶罐，公元前6世纪）。战士女神身披盔甲，准备就位，与聚集的奥林匹斯众神同列。宙斯王座上栖息着一只猫头鹰，它是雅典娜与雅典的神秘象征。

#### 1.1.4 **Other versions of cosmogony and theogony**

> 4.2.1.4 其他版本的宇宙起源说和神祇起源说

Hesiod's account of first beginnings is the earliest and most detailed to survive, and was regarded, later in antiquity, as particularly influential. But it was not considered to be uniquely authoritative. There were numerous variants, some supplementing Hesiod and some diverging from him quite considerably.

> 赫西俄德关于最初起源的叙述是最早且最详细的幸存下来的说法，并且在古代后期被认为尤其有影响力。但它并未被视为具有唯一权威性。存在众多变体，一些补充了赫西俄德的观点，另一些则与他有相当大的差异。

*Aphrodite*: As a small but significant example of divergence we may take the parentage of Aphrodite. According to Homer's *Iliad* she was born, not from the severed genitals of Ouranos, but by a more orthodox route: her parents were Zeus and the goddess Dione, a little-known figure for whom our most extensive evidence relates to her role as Zeus' consort at the oracle of Dodona in Epeiros. Homer's genealogy thus shifts the emphasis away from Aphrodite-as-cosmic-principle to Aphrodite-as-anthropomorphic-deity. Indeed the Homeric Aphrodite is all too human: when wounded in the hand by the mortal hero Diomedes, *this* Aphrodite

> 阿佛洛狄忒：我们可以以阿佛洛狄忒的出身为例，来说明一个虽小但重要的分歧。根据荷马史诗《伊利亚特》的记载，她并非如某些神话中所说的那样，诞生于乌拉诺斯被割下的生殖器，而是通过更传统的方式：她的父母是宙斯和女神迪俄涅。迪俄涅是一个鲜为人知的神祇，我们目前所掌握的关于她的最详尽资料，主要涉及她在埃庇罗地区的多多纳神谕所中作为宙斯配偶的角色。荷马的家谱因此将重点从阿佛洛狄忒作为宇宙本源的象征，转移到了她作为人格化神祇的形象。事实上，荷马笔下的阿佛洛狄忒几乎过于人性化：当她被凡人英雄迪奥梅德斯刺伤手掌时，这位阿佛洛狄忒便显露出其凡人的特质。

rushes from the battlefield to be comforted by her mother, like a child who has hurt herself in the playground.

> 从战场奔向母亲以寻求安慰，如同一个在游乐场弄伤自己的孩子。

<span id="page-98-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_98_Picture_1.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*According to one version of the mythological tradition, the parents of Aphrodite were Zeus and Dione. The divine couple were worshipped at Dodona in Epeiros, where this coin depicting the pair was struck (late 3rd century BC).*

> 根据神话传统的一个版本，阿佛罗狄忒的父母是宙斯和迪俄涅。这对神祇在伊庇鲁斯的多多纳受到崇拜，此枚描绘这对神祇的硬币于公元前3世纪末铸造。

*Typhoeus/Typh(a)on*: Another divergence from the Hesiodic account concerns Zeus' monstrous adversary Typhoeus, whom other sources refer to as Typhon or Typhaon. Amongst the various versions of his parentage is one which relates that he was produced by Hera alone, as a counterpart to Zeus-born Athene. In the mythographic *Library* ascribed to Apollodoros, and in one of the metamorphosis stories compiled by Antoninus Liberalis, we find preserved a fascinating account of Typhon's struggles with the Olympians. In this version, the gods take flight to Egypt, transforming themselves into animals to avoid detection (the Greeks'

> 泰丰/泰丰翁：与赫西俄德记载的不同之处还涉及宙斯的怪物般对手泰丰，其他资料中称他为泰丰或泰法翁。关于他的出身有多种说法，其中一种说法认为他是赫拉独自所生，作为宙斯所生雅典娜的对应者。在阿波罗多罗斯的神话图书馆以及安东尼努斯·利比里乌斯编纂的变形故事之一中，我们发现保存了泰丰与奥林匹斯诸神斗争的引人入胜的记载。在这个版本中，诸神逃往埃及，化身为动物以躲避追捕（希腊人……）

perception of the Egyptians as believers in 'animal gods' doubtless plays a role in this story). In hand-to-hand combat with Zeus Typhon cuts through his adversary's tendons, and then carries him back to his cave-lair in Cilicia (southern Asia Minor). After recovering his tendons – which Typhon had hidden in a bearskin – and thus regaining his strength, Zeus at last overcomes his foe, for good measure hurling Mount Etna at him and pinioning him beneath it.

> 埃及人对“动物神”的信仰无疑在这一故事中起到了作用。泰丰与宙斯近身搏斗，切断了对手的肌腱，然后将他带回位于奇里乞亚（小亚细亚南部）的洞穴巢穴。在恢复了被泰丰藏在熊皮中的肌腱、从而重获力量之后，宙斯终于战胜了对手，作为额外的惩罚，他将埃特纳山投掷向泰丰，并将其牢牢压在山下。

#### 1.1.5 **The** *Homeric Hymns*

> 4.2.1.5 荷马赞美诗

In other cases we find mythological accounts which do not so much diverge from Hesiod as supplement him. Prime examples here are stories of the births of some of the principal Olympians, as narrated for instance in the so-called *Homeric Hymns*. Each of these compositions (ascribed in antiquity to Homer, though their authorship is unknown) celebrates the origins and exploits of a single deity; the hymns may well have been intended for performance at a festival for the god or goddess concerned.

> 在其他情况下，我们还会发现一些神话记载，并不那么偏离赫西俄德，而是补充他的内容。这类例子包括一些奥林匹斯主神诞生的故事，例如在所谓的《荷马颂歌》中所叙述的。这些作品（尽管其作者身份不明，但在古代被归于荷马）各自赞颂一位神祇的起源和事迹；这些颂歌可能原本是为相关神祇的节日庆典而创作的。

*Apollo*: Many slightly differing tales circulated about the birth of Apollo and his twin sister Artemis. The earliest and best-known version is that in the *Homeric Hymn to Delian Apollo*. For a long time the goddess Leto roamed Greece in search of a place to give birth. But her search was in vain, since the wrath of jealous Hera pursued her as it pursued so many of Zeus' other lovers: each place in turn rejected the distressed mother-to-be. But at last the humble, low-lying Cycladic island of Delos agreed to accept her, persuaded by Leto's promise that it would soon be the home of a glorious shrine to Apollo. According to a version related in the *Hymn to Delos* by Kallimachos (3rd century BC), Delos' reward for accepting Leto was that the isle, which had hitherto floated about in the sea, would henceforth be fixed for ever. Leto's sufferings were not yet at an end, however: for nine days and nine nights Hera contrived to keep the goddess of childbirth, Eileithyia, in ignorance of Leto's plight. But eventually Eileithyia reached Delos, and Apollo was born: 'so she cast her arms around a palm tree and knelt in the soft meadow; and the earth smiled beneath her. The child leaped out into the light, and all the goddesses raised a cry.' (According to Apollodoros' account, Apollo's twin sister Artemis was born first, and then acted as midwife for the birth of her brother.) Ever afterwards Apollo was linked as intimately with lowly Delos as he was with the peaks and chasms of Delphi.

> 阿波罗：关于阿波罗和他双胞胎妹妹阿尔忒弥斯的诞生，流传着许多略有差异的传说。最早且最为人熟知的版本见于《荷马献给德尔斐阿波罗的颂歌》。很久以来，女神莱托在希腊各地游荡，寻找一个可以分娩的地方。然而她的寻找徒劳无功，因为嫉妒的赫拉的愤怒一直追随她，就像她曾对宙斯的其他情人们所做的那样：每一个地方都拒绝了这位忧心忡忡的准母亲。但最后，卑微低洼的基克拉迪群岛中的德罗斯岛同意接纳她，这是莱托以承诺该岛将很快成为阿波罗辉煌神庙的所在地所劝服的。根据卡利马科斯（公元前3世纪）在《献给德罗斯的颂歌》中所述，德罗斯岛接受莱托的报偿是，这个此前在海上漂浮的岛屿从此将固定不动。然而，莱托的苦难并未就此结束：赫拉设法让生育女神埃莱提亚连续九天九夜不知情地忽视莱托的困境。但最终，埃莱提亚抵达了德罗斯岛，阿波罗出生了：“于是她将双臂绕住棕榈树，跪在柔软的草地上；大地在她脚下微笑着。孩子跃入光明，所有的女神都发出了一声欢呼。”（根据阿波罗多罗斯的记载，阿波罗的双胞胎妹妹阿尔忒弥斯是先出生的，随后又为哥哥的诞生担任助产士。）从那以后，阿波罗与卑微的德罗斯岛的关系就如同他与德尔斐山峰与峡谷的关系一样紧密。

<span id="page-100-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_100_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Delos, the tiny, low-lying island in the Cyclades which was the legendary birthplace of Apollo and his twin sister Artemis. In spite of its physical insignificance, Delos had great religious importance: it was a major centre for the worship of Apollo.*

> 得洛斯岛，位于基克拉迪群岛中的一座微小而地势低平的岛屿，是传说中阿波罗和其双胞胎姐姐阿尔忒弥斯的诞生地。尽管其地理位置微不足道，得洛斯岛却具有重大的宗教意义：它是阿波罗崇拜的主要中心。

<span id="page-101-0"></span>![](_page_101_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the specific English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Her head bent forward in an attitude of anguish, Leto supports herself against a palm tree as she prepares to give birth to Apollo and Artemis. Behind her is Eileithyia, goddess of childbirth; in front of her is Athene. The image was painted on a* pyxis*, a small container typically used for perfume or other items of female adornment (early 4th century BC).*

> 她的头低垂，呈现出痛苦的姿态，勒托倚靠着一棵棕榈树，准备分娩阿波罗和阿尔忒弥斯。她背后是分娩女神厄勒梯亚，面前是雅典娜。这幅图像绘制在一只皮克斯（一种通常用于存放香水或其他女性装饰品的小型容器）上（公元前4世纪初）。

*Hermes*: Apollo's brother Hermes is another Olympian whose origins are celebrated at length in a *Homeric Hymn*. Son of Zeus and the nymph Maia, he was born in a cave on Mount Kyllene in Arcadia. Having arrived in the world at dawn, by noon he was up to his characteristic tricks. He found a tortoise waddling along outside his cave, and in a trice had scooped out its flesh from the shell; with the addition of reeds, a stretched ox-hide, and strings made of sheep-gut, it became the first lyre. But as evening drew on, Hermes grew tired of his musical toy. With miraculous speed he made his way to Pieria in northeastern Greece, where Apollo's

> 赫尔墨斯：阿波罗的兄弟赫尔墨斯是另一位奥林匹斯神祇，其起源在荷马颂歌中有详细记载。他是宙斯与仙女马雅之子，出生在阿尔戈斯地区的科利尼山洞穴中。在黎明时分降临人世，到正午时分，他已经开始施展他惯常的诡计。他发现一只乌龟正缓慢地在洞穴外行走，转眼间便将乌龟的肉从壳中掏出来；加上芦苇、拉长的牛皮和羊肠制成的弦，它便成为第一把里拉琴。但随着傍晚来临，赫尔墨斯对他的音乐玩具感到厌倦。他以不可思议的速度前往希腊东北部的皮埃里亚，那里是阿波罗的……

cattle were pasturing. He stole fifty of them; to confuse pursuers he made the beasts walk backwards, and disguised his own footprints by wearing sandals plaited from twigs. In this way Hermes, the master of opposites, blended forward and backward – and also babyhood and adulthood, for his return to the cave coincided with a reversion to the state of infancy. However, in spite of the trickster's wiles, Apollo traced his property to the thief's lair; under Zeus' dispensation, the rift between brother and brother was healed, as Hermes' gift of the lyre to Apollo was reciprocated by Hermes' taking over of the patronage of herds.

> 牛群正在吃草。他偷走了五十头牛；为了迷惑追捕者，他让这些牲畜倒着行走，并用由树枝编织的凉鞋掩盖自己的脚印。通过这种方式，作为对立之主的赫尔墨斯将前进与后退融合在一起——同时也将婴儿时期与成年时期融合在一起，因为他的返回洞穴与重返婴儿状态恰好同时发生。然而，尽管诡计多端的骗子施展了各种花招，阿波罗还是追踪到了被盗物品的藏身之处；在宙斯的调解下，兄弟之间的裂痕得以弥合，正如阿波罗接受赫尔墨斯赠送的里拉琴作为回报，赫尔墨斯也接替了阿波罗对牧群的庇护职责。

<span id="page-103-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_103_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Mount Kyllene in Arcadia, legendary birthplace of Hermes. Like all Greek mountains, Kyllene was regarded as a place of wildness, where sacred and uncanny things might happen at any time.*

> 阿尔卡迪亚的基拉尼山，赫尔墨斯的传说诞生地。与所有希腊山峰一样，基拉尼山被视为荒野之地，神圣而神秘的事物随时可能发生。

<span id="page-104-1"></span>![](_page_104_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*Stories from Greek mythology were imaginatively retold in Etruria. On this Etruscan water-jar (*c*. 530 BC), baby Hermes sleeps – or pretends to – while Apollo remonstrates with Hermes' mother Maia over the theft of his cattle. The identity of the man on the far right is unknown; perhaps it is Maia's husband. The cattle, concealed in a cave, are visible proof of Hermes' guilt.*

> 希腊神话故事被富有想象力地重新讲述并传播到伊特鲁里亚。这件伊特鲁里亚水罐（约公元前530年）上描绘了婴儿赫尔墨斯睡着了——或者假装睡着了——而阿波罗正与赫尔墨斯的母亲迈亚争论着关于牛被偷的事情。画面中右侧的男子身份不明；也许他是迈亚的丈夫。藏在洞穴中的牛是赫尔墨斯有罪的显而易见的证据。

### 1.2 <span id="page-104-0"></span>**Dionysos**

> 4.2.2 狄俄尼索斯

The latest arrival amongst the Olympians was generally reckoned to be Dionysos, whose birth is recorded by Apollodoros:

> 奥林匹斯诸神中最新降临的通常被认为是狄俄尼索斯，其诞生被阿波罗多罗斯记载：

As for Semele [a mortal], Zeus fell in love with her, and lay with her without Hera's knowledge. Now Zeus had agreed to do whatever Semele asked, and, as the result of a deception by Hera, she asked him to come to her just as he had come when he was wooing Hera. Unable to refuse, Zeus came to her bedchamber in a chariot, complete with lightning and thunder, and hurled a thunderbolt. Semele expired from terror, but Zeus snatched her six-month abortive child from the fire, and sewed it into his thigh… When the due time arrived, Zeus brought Dionysos to birth by undoing the stitches, and handed him over to Hermes…

> 至于塞默勒[凡人]，宙斯爱上了她，并在赫拉不知情的情况下与她结合。当时宙斯曾答应她无论提出什么要求都会满足，然而由于赫拉的欺骗，她要求宙斯以追求赫拉时的模样出现。宙斯无法拒绝，便驾驶着装有闪电和雷鸣的战车来到她的卧室，并投下一道雷霆。塞默勒因恐惧而死，但宙斯从火中抢出她六个月未足月的胎儿，将其缝入自己的大腿……当预产期到来时，宙斯解开缝线，让狄俄尼索斯诞生，并将他交给赫尔墨斯。

As yet another product of an extramarital liaison on the part of Zeus, Dionysos could hardly expect to evade Hera's jealousy. When Hermes gave the baby to Semele's sister Ino and her husband Athamas to bring up as a girl – Dionysos' career as a boundary-crosser began early – Hera afflicted the couple with madness, in which state they murdered two of their own sons. But the offspring of Zeus could not be so easily eradicated. Zeus first rescued his little son by transforming him into a kid, and then had Hermes entrust him into the care of nymphs, who lived at a specially holy place called Nysa, located by various sources in different but equally 'remote'

> 宙斯又一个婚外情的产物，狄奥尼索斯很难指望能逃过赫拉的嫉妒。当赫尔墨斯将婴儿交给塞墨勒的妹妹英诺和她的丈夫阿塔马斯，让他们将他抚养为女孩时——狄奥尼索斯的越界者生涯从幼年时期就开始了——赫拉让这对夫妇陷入疯狂，使他们杀害了自己的两个儿子。但宙斯之子不可能如此轻易地被消灭。宙斯首先将他救出，将他变成一只小山羊，然后让赫尔墨斯将他托付给居住在一处特别神圣之地尼萨的仙女们照看，而尼萨的具体位置，据不同来源记载在不同的地方，但都同样偏僻。

regions, including Ethiopia, Arabia and Scythia. An alternative foster-parent of baby Dionysos was Silenos, the elderly and ugly leader of the satyrs.

> 包括埃塞俄比亚、阿拉伯和萨尔马提亚等地区。婴儿狄俄尼索斯的另一位养父母是硅琳诺斯，他是饮酒者的年迈且丑陋的领袖。

<span id="page-105-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_105_Picture_1.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*When Zeus sewed the unborn Dionysos into his thigh after annihilating the baby's mother Semele with a thunderbolt, he was acknowledging his paternity in a very intimate way. Here a south Italian vase-painter (*c. *400 BC) represents Dionysos' reemergence into the world to take his place amongst the Olympians.*

> 当宙斯用雷霆击毙婴儿的生母塞墨勒后，将未出生的狄俄尼索斯缝入自己的大腿时，他以一种极其私密的方式承认了自己作为父亲的身份。这里，一位南意大利陶罐画家（约公元前400年）描绘了狄俄尼索斯重新现身于世间，加入奥林匹斯众神的场景。

<span id="page-106-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_106_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The specific English text to be translated is missing. Please provide the text for translation.

*As befits a god who belongs 'outside', Dionysos is entrusted for his upbringing to divinities of the wild world of nature. Here Hermes – identifiable by the wings on his helmet and boots – hands the infant to Papposilenos, a kind of arch-satyr. Attic vase,* c*. 430 BC*.

> 正如一位属于“外部”的神祇所宜，狄奥尼索斯被托付给自然野性世界的神灵抚养。赫尔墨斯（以其头盔和靴子上的翅膀为特征）将婴儿交给了帕波西莱诺斯，这是一种典型的半人半兽的萨提尔。雅典陶罐，约公元前430年。

### 1.3 **The Giants**

> 4.2.3 巨人

Another episode in divine 'prehistory' for which we have to look beyond Hesiod concerns the Battle of the Gods and the Giants. Generated by the blood of castrated Ouranos where it dripped upon Gaia, the monstrous brood of Giants contested the Olympians' power, as had the Titans before them. Some accounts depict the Giants as huge and shaggy-haired, hurling such typically 'uncivilized' projectiles as rocks and trees. Other sources, including several visual representations, portray them as formidable warriors with more orthodox armour and weaponry. Their base was Pallene/Phlegrai, the most westerly of the three prongs of the Chalkidike peninsula.

> 另一段关于诸神“前史”的记载，我们需要超越赫西俄德的记载才能了解，那就是诸神与巨人的战争。这场战争源于被阉割的乌拉诺斯的血液滴落在盖亚身上所生成的怪物般的巨人族群，他们挑战奥林匹斯诸神的权威，正如泰坦巨人之前所做的那样。一些记载将巨人描述为高大且毛发旺盛的巨兽，投掷着典型的“未开化”投掷物，如岩石和树木。其他来源，包括多幅视觉图像，却将他们描绘为装备传统盔甲和武器的强大战士。他们的据点位于帕莱内/费勒格拉，即哈尔基迪基半岛三个分支中最西边的一个。

The Giants were as mighty as their names were resonant: Pallas, Porphyrion, Alkyoneus, Enkelados. But the Olympians triumphed in the end, thanks to the thunderbolts of Zeus, the arrows of his son Herakles, and some remarkable virtuosity by Athene: she flayed Pallas, used his resilient skin as a shield (adopting his name for good measure), and despatched Enkelados by throwing the island of Sicily at him.

> 巨人与他们名字一样威猛：帕拉斯、波吕斐隆、阿尔科涅乌斯、恩刻拉多斯。但奥林匹斯众神最终取得了胜利，多亏了宙斯的雷霆之击、他儿子海克力斯的箭矢，以及雅典娜非凡的技艺：她砍下帕拉斯的皮肤，用其坚韧的皮肤作为盾牌（顺便采用了他的名字），并将西西里岛扔向恩刻拉多斯，从而将他杀死。

## 2 *The Orphic Alternative*

> 4.3 俄耳甫斯教派的替代方案

<span id="page-108-0"></span>![](_page_108_Picture_1.jpeg)

> The specific English text to be translated is missing. Please provide the text you would like translated into Chinese.

*The Derveni Papyrus, which preserves unique evidence about ancient cosmogonical views.*

> 德文尼纸莎草纸保存了关于古代宇宙起源的独特证据。

Renowned though Hesiod's narrative was, it was by no means the only account of 'first beginnings' in circulation. A very different narrative, embracing anthropogony as well as cosmogony, was ascribed to the legendary poet Orpheus. Until recently our knowledge of so-called 'Orphic poetry' relied mainly on extensive quotations by later Neoplatonist writers, who noted many anticipations of their own philosophy in these poems; but a new development took place in 1962 in the Macedonian town of Derveni, with the discovery of a fragmentary papyrus containing part of a 4th-century BC commentary on an Orphic theogony. The consequences of this find are still being assimilated; indeed, a definitive scholarly edition of the papyrus is still awaited. But what is clear is that, already in the early Hellenistic period and probably long before that, a non-Hesiodic view of the development of the cosmos was being narrated.

> 尽管赫西俄德的叙述声名显赫，但绝不是关于“最初起源”的唯一说法。另一种截然不同的叙述，涵盖人类起源和宇宙起源，被归于传说中的诗人奥尔菲斯。直到最近，我们对所谓的“奥尔菲斯诗歌”的了解主要依赖于后来新柏拉图主义作家的大量引文，他们注意到这些诗歌中包含了许多预示他们自身哲学的内容；但1962年在马其顿达维尼镇发现了一片残破的纸莎草纸，其中包含了一部公元前4世纪对奥尔菲斯神谱的注释，这一发现带来了新的发展。这一发现的影响仍在被消化；事实上，一部权威的学术版纸莎草纸文本仍在等待之中。但可以明确的是，在早期希腊化时期，甚至可能更早以前，一种非赫西俄德式的宇宙发展叙述已经存在。

The Orphic narrative of origins differs from the Hesiodic in two major ways. First, it ascribes a fundamental role in creation to a divine figure who preceded not only Ouranos but even Night. This figure is referred to sometimes as Protogonos ('First Born'), sometimes as Phanes ('The One who Makes to Appear' or 'The One who Appears'), sometimes under other names (Eros, Bromios, Erikepaios, Metis, Zeus), as befits one who incorporates all existence within himself. What of the origin of First Born himself? According to our later sources, Time created a cosmic egg, from which First Born emerged, winged and bisexual – containing implicit within him all that would follow.

> 奥菲斯关于起源的叙述与赫西俄德的叙述在两个主要方面有所不同。首先，它将一个在创造中起根本作用的神祇置于首位，这位神祇不仅在乌拉诺斯之前，甚至在黑夜之前就已经存在。这位神祇有时被称为“原始神”（Protogonos），有时被称为“显现者”（Phanes，“使事物显现者”或“显现者”），有时又以其他名称出现（如爱洛斯、布罗米奥斯、埃里凯帕伊奥斯、梅提斯、宙斯），这些名称恰当地体现了他将所有存在都包含在自身之中的特性。那么，原始神本身又是如何起源的呢？根据我们后来的资料，时间创造了一个宇宙之卵，原始神便从其中诞生，拥有翅膀且具有双性特征——他内在蕴含着之后一切事物的雏形。

The second difference from the Hesiodic narrative is more radical. According to the Orphic theogony, Zeus' rule was succeeded by that of his son Dionysos, the product of a

> 与赫西俄德的叙述相比，第二个差异更为激进。根据俄耳甫斯的神谱，宙斯的统治被其子狄俄尼索斯的统治所取代，后者是……

union between Zeus and his own daughter Persephone. Urged on by a jealous Hera, the Titans cooked and devoured the baby Dionysos; in return, Zeus struck the Titans with his thunderbolt. At this point theogony becomes anthropogony, for it was from the soot of the incinerated Titans that humanity was formed. As for Dionysos, he was destined to be born again, regenerated by Zeus thanks to the fact that the baby's heart had been preserved by the Titans.

> 宙斯与他自己的女儿珀耳塞福涅的结合。在赫拉的怂恿下，泰坦巨人将婴儿狄俄尼索斯煮熟并吃掉；作为回报，宙斯用雷霆击打泰坦巨人。此时，神谱转变为人类的起源，因为人类正是由被焚毁的泰坦巨人的灰烬所形成。至于狄俄尼索斯，他注定要重生，宙斯使他重生，因为泰坦巨人保存了婴儿的心脏。

This last episode in the Orphic theogony combines the genesis of humanity with the death and rebirth of Dionysos. In so doing, it dovetails with certain rites linked with the name of Orpheus. Individual practitioners known as *Orpheotelestai* ('Initiators of Orpheus'), and groups who participated in 'mystery' cults in honour of Dionysos, were concerned with 'eschatology', that is, beliefs in the Afterlife. In order to maximize his or her chances of a successful transition to the world of the dead, a person needed to go through certain procedures, some of which were specified in ritual formulae inscribed onto thin leaves of gold, buried with the deceased. From leaves which have survived from sites in southern Italy (see [p. 212](#page-461-0)) and in Thessaly, it seems that Dionysos could be thought of as a potent intercessor on behalf of the dead, since (following the Orphic genealogy), he might be able to persuade his mother Persephone, Queen of the Dead.

> 俄耳甫斯神话体系中最后的篇章将人类的起源与狄俄尼索斯的死亡与重生结合在一起，从而与某些与俄耳甫斯之名相关的仪式相呼应。被称为“俄耳甫斯启示者”（Orpheotelestai）的个人实践者，以及参与以狄俄尼索斯名义举行的“神秘教派”仪式的团体，都关注“末世论”，即关于来世的信仰。为了在死后世界顺利过渡，个人必须经历某些程序，其中一些程序被写入刻有金箔的薄片上，并随逝者一同埋葬。从意大利南部（见第212页）和色萨利地区保存下来的金箔来看，狄俄尼索斯可以被视为亡灵的强力辩护者，因为（依据俄耳甫斯谱系），他或许能够说服其母珀耳塞福涅，即冥界的女王。

Understanding Orphic beliefs continues to pose problems. Some scholars used to believe that there was actually an 'Orphic religion', in virtual opposition to the 'normal' religion of the *polis*. Such a view is regarded as less tenable today. What is undeniable is that the existence of distinctively 'Orphic myths' provides yet one more instance of the richness and plurality of the Greek mythological network.

> 理解俄耳甫斯教派的信仰仍然存在困难。一些学者过去曾认为实际上存在一种“俄耳甫斯宗教”，实际上与城邦的“正常”宗教相对立。但如今这种观点被认为站不住脚。不可否认的是，具有鲜明“俄耳甫斯特色”的神话存在，又为希腊神话网络的丰富性和多样性提供了另一个实例。

## 3 <span id="page-110-0"></span>*[Origins of Humanity](#page-6-8)*

> 4.4 人类的起源

<span id="page-110-1"></span>In some of the accounts we have looked at so far, mortals put in an occasional, sometimes almost incidental appearance. It is now time to focus on the Greek myths which deal specifically with the beginnings of humankind. These myths are quite few in number, and often buried in texts whose main thrust lies elsewhere: we shall look in vain for a canonical equivalent of the biblical Old Testament Genesis story. Indeed, in keeping with the local differentiation which is so deeply characteristic of Greek mythology, we are more likely to hear about, for instance, the first Argive, or the first Arcadian, than we are to hear about the first man or woman. Nevertheless, from time to time Greek myth-tellers did speculate about humanity's generic origins.

> 到目前为止，我们所讨论的一些叙述中，人类偶尔甚至几乎只是次要角色出现。现在，我们应关注那些专门讲述人类起源的希腊神话。这些神话数量相当少，常常隐藏在其他文本之中，而这些文本的主要内容并不在此：我们很难找到与圣经《创世纪》故事具有权威对应版本的神话。确实，这与希腊神话中深深植根的地方性差异相一致，我们更可能听到关于第一个阿尔戈斯人或第一个阿尔卡迪亚人的故事，而不是第一个男人或女人的故事。尽管如此，希腊神话讲述者偶尔还是会探讨人类的普遍起源。

#### 3.1.1 **The Five Races**

> 4.4.1.1 五大族

One way of conceiving the human past makes it the product of leaps between a series of typologically different breeds, the differences being symbolized by a set of contrasts between metals. This conception has strong parallels in the thought of other ancient civilizations, especially Zoroastrianism; and it was to enjoy a profoundly influential afterlife in the form of the widespread belief in a decline from a paradisiacal 'Golden Age'.

> 一种理解人类历史的方式认为，历史是不同类型物种之间跳跃发展的产物，这些差异通过金属之间的对比来象征。这一观念在其他古代文明的思想中有着显著的相似之处，尤其是琐罗亚斯德教；这种观念以一种黄金时代衰落的普遍信念形式，在后世产生了深远的影响。

In the version narrated by Hesiod in his *Works and Days*, the series began when the gods created a Golden Race. These beings knew neither toil nor old age; life consisted of feasting; death came like sleep. It was a perfect existence, and it took place under the sovereignty of Kronos – which shows that, in spite of those gruesome tales of pre-Olympian castration and swallowing, one way of imagining 'the time before Zeus' was as a lovely idyll.

> 在赫西俄德所著的《工作与日子》中，这一系列故事始于诸神创造黄金种族之时。这些生物不知劳作与衰老，生命便是盛宴；死亡如同睡眠。这是一种完美的存在，当时由克洛诺斯统治——这表明，尽管有那些关于前奥林匹斯时代残酷故事（如阉割与吞噬）的描述，人们仍可将“宙斯之前的时代”想象成一种美好的田园牧歌式的生活。

In time the Golden Race passed away, turning into benign spirits who watch over the earth. Their successors, again made by the gods, were the Race of Silver, an inferior and rather immature breed:

> 随着时间推移，黄金人种消逝了，化为守护大地的善良神灵。他们的后裔，再次由诸神创造，是白银种族，一个低等且尚不成熟的族群：

For a hundred years a child would be reared by his careful mother, playing childishly at home, a big baby; but after reaching adolescence and the full measure of youthful prime, they lived only a short time, and in sorrow, because of their witlessness.
