For they could not keep from wrongful aggression against each other, nor would they serve the immortals, nor sacrifice on the sacred altars of the Blessed Ones, as is proper for human beings wherever they dwell.

> 一百年里，一个孩子由细心的母亲抚养长大，在家中天真地玩耍，像个大婴儿；但等他们进入青春期，达到青春的巅峰后，却只能活很短的时间，因他们的愚昧而悲痛。因为他们无法避免彼此之间的不义攻击，也不侍奉神灵，也不在神圣的圣坛上为圣者献祭，而这正是人类无论居住在何处都应有的行为。

By this time the universe was ruled by Zeus, who angrily put an end to the Silver Race.

> 到这时，宇宙由宙斯统治，他愤怒地终结了银族。

Third came the Race of Bronze, whom Zeus created out of ash-trees. The strength of the ash makes it suitable for spear-shafts: this was a warrior Race which devoted

> 第三代是青铜种族，宙斯用梣树创造出来。梣树的韧性使其成为矛杆的理想材料：这是一个以战斗为荣的种族，他们致力于……

itself to the works of Ares the war-god. In Hesiod's version they were not themselves *of* bronze (in other versions they were), but their armour, houses and tools certainly were made from that metal. There was no need for the gods to intervene to put an end to their aggression: the Men of Bronze exterminated themselves through their relentless fighting.

> 自身归于战神阿瑞斯的作品。在赫西俄德的版本中，他们自身并非由青铜制成（在其他版本中则是如此），但他们的武器、房屋和工具无疑都是用这种金属打造的。神明无需介入以终结他们的侵略行为：青铜时代的人们通过无休止的战斗彼此消灭。

The logic of the metallic series is now interrupted, since the next Race created by Zeus is that of Heroes, mighty mortals who fought and died at Thebes and Troy. In many ways they resembled the Bronze Race, but they won much glory, and a few of them were even said to have been rewarded with a post-mortem existence in the Isles of the Blessed, somewhere at the edge of the world beside Okeanos. What a contrast with what follows: the Race of Iron. This is our world, the world of the present. Toil and misery are our lot; and it will get worse. In the future there will be lack of respect, impiety, and every sort of villainy.

> 金属系列的逻辑现在被打断了，因为宙斯接下来创造的种族是英雄族，他们是强大的凡人，曾在底比斯和特洛伊战斗并死去。在许多方面，他们与青铜种族相似，但他们赢得了更多的荣耀，甚至有少数人据说被奖励到幸福岛度过永生，位于世界边缘俄刻阿诺斯旁边的乐土。与接下来的种族相比，这形成了多么鲜明的对比：铁种族。这就是我们的世界，当今的世界。辛劳与苦难是我们命运的常态；而且情况将会变得更糟。未来将充满缺乏尊重、不敬以及各种邪恶行为。

#### 3.1.2 <span id="page-111-0"></span>**Prometheus and fire**

> 4.4.1.2 普罗米修斯与火

Side by side with this story of decline – and not necessarily integrated with it in a seamless whole – we find another set of traditions which link human origins with the Titan Prometheus ('Forethought'), son of Iapetos and the Oceanid Klymene. How exactly Prometheus fitted into the story of anthropogony was, however, a matter of debate. Apollodoros mentions in passing that Prometheus fashioned humans from water and earth, and this story, although not found in our extant sources before the 4th century BC, may have been much older, and certainly spread widely: the traveller Pausanias, visiting the small community of Panopeus in Phokis, saw two rocks which were alleged to smell like human flesh, and which were said to be the remains of the clay out of which Prometheus had fabricated the human race. Much commoner, however, was the variant which made Prometheus the champion, rather than the creator, of humanity. According to the Titan himself, speaking in Aischylos' play *Prometheus Bound*, he had bestowed upon human beings every skill, from arithmetic to navigation to medicine to prophecy. But one gift above all marked him out as humanity's benefactor: fire.

> 与这一衰落的故事并行存在的，还有另一组传统，它们将人类的起源与泰坦普罗米修斯（意为“先见之明”）联系起来，他是伊阿佩托斯和海洋女神克吕墨涅的儿子。然而，普罗米修斯在人类起源故事中的确切地位却一直存在争议。阿波罗多罗斯只是提到，普罗米修斯曾用泥土和水塑造人类，尽管这一故事在公元前4世纪之前并未见于现存的文献，但它可能更为古老，而且传播范围很广：旅行家帕乌萨尼亚斯曾前往佛西斯地区的潘诺皮斯小社区，看到两块岩石，据说它们散发着人类肉体的味道，人们称其为普罗米修斯塑造人类所用的黏土残余。然而，更为普遍的变体则将普罗米修斯描绘为人类的捍卫者，而非创造者。根据泰坦普罗米修斯自己在埃斯奇勒斯的《被缚的普罗米修斯》中的叙述，他赋予了人类各种技能，从算术、航海到医药和预言。但有一件礼物尤其使他成为人类的恩人：火。

Hesiod famously relates how, when Zeus had hidden fire from mortals, Prometheus stole it and conveyed it to them in a hollow fennel-stalk. With fire humans could practise the ritual which, beyond all other rituals, made them, in Greek eyes, human: animal sacrifice. Here, too, Prometheus played a role as originator. At Mekone (said to be an ancient name for the city of Sikyon, near Corinth in the northeastern Peloponnese) Prometheus practised a deception which proved to be not only disastrous for himself, but also decisively aetiological for humanity. He laid out before Zeus two portions of food, one of which would henceforth be that of the gods, the other that of mortals: either a succulent dish of

> 赫西俄德著名地讲述了这样一个故事：当宙斯将火隐藏起来，不让人类使用时，普罗米修斯偷来了火，并将其藏在空心的茴香茎中带给人类。有了火，人类便能够进行一种仪式，这种仪式在希腊人看来，是使他们成为真正人类的关键：动物祭祀。在此过程中，普罗米修斯同样扮演了创始者的角色。在梅孔（据说这是古时斯科尼亚城的旧称，位于科林斯附近，今希腊东北部伯罗奔尼撒半岛）普罗米修斯施行了一个欺骗行为，这不仅对他本人造成了灾难性的后果，而且对人类而言具有决定性的起源意义。他向宙斯展示了两份食物，其中一份从此成为诸神的食粮，另一份则成为凡人的食物：一份是丰盛的菜肴，

meat and offal, unappealingly covered with an ox paunch; or a heap of bones, cunningly made to look tasty thanks to an overlay of fat. With the complex logic which Greek myths often exhibit, Zeus *both* saw through the trick *and* made the choice which would go against the gods' good. From that day forward, the division of the sacrificial spoils has remained fixed: the bones are burned for the gods, but the choicest meat and fat stay with mortals.

> 肉和内脏，不吸引人地覆盖着牛胃；或者是一堆骨头，巧妙地被脂肪覆盖层做成看起来美味的样子。凭借希腊神话常表现出的复杂逻辑，宙斯看穿了这个诡计，并做出了违背众神之利的选择。从那天起，祭祀分赃的划分一直保持不变：骨头被烧给神明，但最佳的肉和脂肪则归人类所有。

<span id="page-112-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_112_Picture_1.jpeg)

> 上下文涉及希腊神话。

*The sculptor of this Roman sarcophagus has portrayed Prometheus as a workman creating mini-humans. The scene is supervised by Athene, herself supreme in craftsmanship.*

> 这位罗马石棺的雕塑家将普罗米修斯描绘成一位创造微型人类的工匠。场景由雅典娜监督，她本人就是工艺的巅峰。

<span id="page-113-0"></span>![](_page_113_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Prometheus the creator, fashioning a human figure which uncannily resembles his own, beard included. Hellenistic gemstone.*

> 普罗米修斯，这位创造者，塑造了一个与他本人惊人相似的人形，连胡须也包括在内。希腊化时期的宝石。

The consequences of Prometheus' recklessness were dire, both for him and for the humanity he championed. For the erring Titan, Zeus devised a torture both agonizing and long-lasting. As the Aischylean tragedy *Prometheus Bound* vividly dramatizes, the blacksmith god Hephaistos, accompanied by Kratos ('Power') and Bia ('Violence'), was instructed to clamp Prometheus to a remote crag in the Caucasus with fetters that none could undo. There, each day for a near-eternity, he would be visited by a monstrous eagle which would peck out the lobes of his liver (a part of the anatomy which Greeks regarded as the site of strong passions). Each night the

> 普罗米修斯的鲁莽行为带来了可怕的后果，不仅对他本人，也对人类造成了深远的影响。为了惩罚这位犯错的泰坦神，宙斯设计了一种痛苦且持久的折磨。正如埃斯库罗斯悲剧《被缚的普罗米修斯》生动地描绘的那样，火神赫菲斯托斯在力量（克罗诺斯）和暴力（比阿）的陪同下，被命令用无人能解开的锁链将普罗米修斯锁在高加索山遥远的峭壁上。在那里，他几乎永恒地每天都会被一只怪物般的鹰啄食肝脏（希腊人认为这是强烈情感的所在）。每晚，他的肝脏都会被啄食，而每夜过后，肝脏又会重新生长。

liver would re-grow, enabling the torment to repeat itself down the millennia, until at last the fruit of Zeus' own loins should bring release: the eagle would fall to one of Herakles' arrows.

> 肝脏将重新生长，使折磨在数千年中反复发生，直到宙斯亲生之子带来解脱：那只鹰将被埃拉克勒斯的一支箭射落。

<span id="page-115-0"></span>![](_page_115_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*Prometheus, hands painfully bound behind his head, is at the mercy of the eagle, whose beak is fixed into his body. Etruscan gem, 5th century BC*.

> 普罗米修斯双手痛苦地被绑在头后方，他被鹰所控制，鹰的喙插入他的身体。埃特鲁里亚宝石，公元前五世纪。

![](_page_116_Picture_0.jpeg)

*The long nightmare draws to an end. Herakles launches a volley of arrows towards the eagle, as Prometheus looks helplessly on, tied to or impaled upon a stake. Attic vase, mid-6th century BC*.

> 漫长的噩梦即将结束。赫拉克勒斯向那只鹰射出一排箭矢，而普罗米修斯无助地注视着，被钉在木桩上。雅典陶罐，公元前六世纪中期。

#### 3.1.3 **Pandora**

> 4.4.1.3 潘多拉

For humanity, the results of Promethean defiance were even more far-reaching. Once more the agent was Hephaistos, master of immortal technology. Much as Prometheus did in other myths, so Hephaistos too used earth to mould a living creature. Just like the twin portions which Prometheus had offered to Zeus, this creature too had a deceptive exterior; but unpleasantness lay within. This was the first woman, the closest Greek mythology came to an 'Eve'. Each of the Olympians gave her a gift: hence her name, Pandora ('Allgift'). Prometheus had anticipated that Zeus might devise trouble for mortals, and advised his unheeding brother Epimetheus ('Afterthought') to beware of unsolicited presents. He was too late. Hermes, divine go-between, brought Pandora to humanity, and Epimetheus accepted her with open arms. What happens next 'explains' the presence of woe in the world. However, at least in some of its tellings, this myth is as perplexing as it is memorable.

> 对人类而言，普罗米修斯反抗所带来的结果更为深远。再一次，造物者是赫菲斯托斯，神秘工艺的大师。正如普罗米修斯在其他神话中所做的那样，赫菲斯托斯也用泥土塑造出一个生命体。这生命体的外表如同普罗米修斯献给宙斯的双份礼物一般诱人，但内在却充满苦涩。这便是第一个女人，也是希腊神话中最为接近“艾娃”的存在。每位奥林匹斯众神都赠予她一份礼物，因此她的名字叫潘多拉（意为“全能的赠礼”）。普罗米修斯预见到宙斯可能会给凡人带来麻烦，便劝告他的弟弟后知后觉的埃庇米修斯小心那些未经邀请的礼物。然而他已为时已晚。混沌之神赫尔墨斯将潘多拉带到人类面前，埃庇米修斯热情地接纳了她。接下来发生的事情“解释”了世间苦难的根源。然而，至少在某些讲述版本中，这个神话既令人困惑又令人难忘。

<span id="page-117-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_117_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*Pandora, sometimes called Anesidora, stands looking out towards the viewer: she is ready to descend upon humanity. To the left and right of her on this vase-painting (mid-5th century BC) are various Olympian deities: Zeus, Poseidon, Iris, Ares and Hermes.*

> 普罗米修斯之女潘多拉，有时被称为安提西德拉，正站在那里注视着观者，似乎即将降临人间。在这幅公元前五世纪中期的陶罐彩绘中，她的左右两侧分别描绘了多位奥林匹斯神祇：宙斯、波塞冬、伊里斯、阿瑞斯和赫尔墨斯。

The phrase 'Pandora's Box' has become proverbial (thanks to the Renaissance scholar Erasmus), but, in our Greek sources, what the first woman brings with her is not a box, but a *pithos* – a very large storage jar, of the kind that archaeological excavations have frequently brought to light. Pandora's crucial action is to open her jar, allowing what was formerly within it to escape into the world – with the sole exception of Elpis ('Hope' or 'Expectation'). But what exactly does the jar contain? And what does the 'escape' signify? According to one of antiquity's lesser-known writers, Babrios (a 2nd-century AD teller of fables), the jar originally contained *good things*; when the lid was removed, all these goods escaped from humanity's grasp, with the lone exception of Hope – which is all we have left to console us. Hesiod, though, tells a very different story. In his version, the jar originally contained any number of *evils*: their escape meant that they roamed the world for humans to encounter, while only Hope was left behind. But does that amount to saying that humans do have Hope (because it is still at their disposal, in the storage jar), or that they do not (because, unlike the evils, it has not flown out into the world)? The myth of Pandora perfectly illustrates several key characteristics of Greek myths: their capacity to intrigue, to engage, and sometimes to baffle, and their power to generate fresh and sometimes mutually contradictory variants.

> “潘多拉的盒子”这一短语已成为谚语（这要归功于文艺复兴时期的学者埃拉斯谟斯），但在我们的希腊资料中，第一个女人带来的并非盒子，而是一个叫做“皮托斯”（pithos）的大陶罐——这种陶罐在考古发掘中经常被发现。潘多拉关键的行为是打开这个陶罐，使原本其中的东西逃逸到世界上——唯一的例外是“希望”（Elpis，意为“希望”或“期待”）。但陶罐中到底装着什么？“逃逸”又意味着什么？根据一位鲜为人知的古代作家巴比奥斯（一位公元2世纪的寓言讲述者）的说法，陶罐原本装满了美好的事物；当盖子被打开后，所有这些美好之物都离开了人类的掌控，唯一留下的便是希望——这也是我们唯一能用来安慰自己的东西。然而，赫西俄德讲述的却是一个截然不同的故事。在他版本中，陶罐原本装满了各种邪恶；它们的逃逸意味着这些邪恶在世界上四处游荡，供人类遭遇，而唯有希望被留在了原处。但这是否意味着人类拥有希望（因为希望仍然存放在陶罐中，可供他们使用），还是说他们没有希望（因为与邪恶不同，希望没有逃逸到世界上）？潘多拉的神话完美地体现了希腊神话的几个关键特征：它们能引发好奇、吸引人参与、有时令人困惑，并且具有产生新颖、有时相互矛盾的变体的能力。

<span id="page-118-0"></span>![](_page_118_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*Pandora, 1869, by Dante Gabriel Rossetti. Already escaping from Pandora's box are the world's evils – here envisaged in the form of a smoky vapour which surrounds the woman responsible for their release.*

> 潘多拉，1869年，但丁·加百列·罗塞蒂作。已从潘多拉魔盒中逃逸出世间灾祸——此处被设想为环绕着那位释放它们的女子的烟雾状蒸汽。

<span id="page-119-0"></span>![](_page_119_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*According to ancient accounts, it was a large storage jar – this one is from Mallia in Crete – that Pandora imprudently opened, letting misfortunes fly out to plague humanity.*

> 根据古代记载，这是一个巨大的储藏罐——这个陶罐来自克里特岛的马利亚——潘多拉轻率地打开了它，让灾祸飞出，使人类遭受苦难。

### 3.2 **The Flood**

> 4.4.2 大洪水

Many traditions have exploited the theme of a great Flood, from the Ancient Mesopotamian *Epic of Gilgamesh* (in which the sole survivor was Ut-napishtim), to the Hebrew Bible (Noah), to many native American stories. Greek accounts, too, record the occurrence of a primeval Flood, which had the effect of eradicating the humans who had existed hitherto, and of regenerating a new breed from which all subsequent humans are descended.

> 许多传统都利用了大洪水的主题，从古代美索不达米亚的《吉尔伽美什史诗》（其中唯一幸存者是乌特纳皮什提姆）到希伯来圣经（诺亚），再到许多美洲原住民的故事。希腊的记载也记录了远古洪水的发生，这场洪水彻底消灭了此前存在的所有人类，并重新培育出一个新的族群，所有后来的人类都是由此族群繁衍而来。

The survivors of the Flood are named (by Apollodoros, among others) as Deukalion and his wife Pyrrha. The genealogy of this pair – Deukalion is the son of

> 大洪水的幸存者被命名为（艾波罗多罗斯等人）德卡利翁和他的妻子比拉。这对夫妇的家族谱——德卡利翁是……的儿子。

Prometheus, and Pyrrha is the daughter of Epimetheus and Pandora – seems to imply that they are seen as 'doubles' of Prometheus and Pandora. Indeed one way of interpreting Deukalion's role is as analogous to that of Prometheus: like the Titan, he succeeds in circumventing the will of Zeus by championing humanity. According to Apollodoros, Zeus wanted to eliminate the Race of Bronze (a motif paralleled in Near Eastern accounts, including Genesis), and so flooded the earth with a deluge (Hesiod's Bronze Race had, of course, saved Zeus the trouble, by wiping themselves out). On the advice of his father Prometheus, Deukalion built a chest and climbed into it with Pyrrha. The waters rose, but the chest floated on; after nine days and nine nights it came ashore on Mount Parnassos, above Delphi. Interestingly, in a variant preserved by Aristotle the Flood is said to have occurred in the area 'round Dodona and the river Acheloös'; could it be that the two influential oracles at Delphi and Dodona competed with each other in making claims about which was the oldest inhabited part of Greece?

> 普罗米修斯，以及珀尔拉是伊庇墨修斯和潘多拉的女儿——这似乎暗示他们被视为普罗米修斯和潘多拉的对应者。事实上，解读德卡利翁的角色之一种方式，就是将其与普罗米修斯相类比：就像泰坦神，他成功地通过支持人类来绕过宙斯的意志。根据阿波罗多罗斯的记载，宙斯想要消灭青铜种族（这一情节在近东的叙述中也有类似主题，包括《创世纪》），因此他让洪水淹没大地（当然，赫西俄德笔下的青铜种族早已替宙斯省去麻烦，因为他们自己就灭绝了）。在父亲普罗米修斯的建议下，德卡利翁造了一只船，并与珀尔拉一同进入其中。洪水上涨，但船却漂浮在水面；经过九天九夜后，船停在帕纳索斯山上，位于德尔斐之上。有趣的是，在亚里士多德保存的一个变体中，洪水据说发生在多多那以及阿刻洛俄斯河周边地区；难道德尔斐和多多那这两个重要的神谕之地曾互相竞争，宣称哪一处才是希腊最早有人居住的地方吗？

<span id="page-121-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_121_Picture_0.jpeg)

> Please provide the specific English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Mount Parnassos, where the 'ark' of Deukalion and his wife Pyrrha finally came to rest. Even in this land of spectacular panoramas, few sights are more breathtaking than this one: truly a place for a new beginning.*

> 帕纳索斯山，德卡利翁和妻子皮拉的方舟终于在此停泊。即使在这片壮丽全景之地，也难有比这更令人屏息的景象：这确实是一个全新的开始之地。

Unlike his father, Deukalion prudently sacrificed to Zeus, who reciprocated by granting him a favour: to populate the earth. As Apollodoros puts it: 'On Zeus' instructions, he picked up stones and threw them over his head; and the stones that Deukalion threw became men, and those that Pyrrha threw became women.' All over Greece the flood waters receded, leaving their trace in collective belief: within the precinct of the temple of Olympian Zeus in Athens the traveller Pausanias was shown a broad cleft in the earth, which, people said, was where the water ran away after the Flood. For Greece it was a new beginning. Among the descendants of the people-of-stone were many of the greatest heroes and heroines of Greek mythology.

> 与他的父亲不同，德卡利翁明智地向宙斯献祭，宙斯也回报了他一个恩惠：使大地重获生机。正如阿波罗多罗斯所说：“按照宙斯的指示，他捡起石头并将其抛向头顶；德卡利翁投掷的石头变成了男人，而皮拉投掷的石头变成了女人。”洪水退去后，希腊各地留下了集体信仰的痕迹：旅行者帕乌萨尼亚斯在雅典奥林匹亚宙斯神庙的圣域中，看到了一处宽阔的地裂，人们说这就是洪水退去后水流排出的地方。对希腊而言，这标志着一个新的开始。许多希腊神话中最伟大的英雄和女英雄都是石头人的后裔。

## 4 <span id="page-122-0"></span>*[Local Origins](#page-6-9)*

> 4.5 本地起源

#### 4.1.1 **Ants and snakes**

> 4.5.1.1 蚂蚁和蛇

The pluralism which characterizes so many aspects of Greek civilization is reflected also in its myths of origin. Much more interest was expressed in the origins of individual communities than in those of the entire human race.

> 希腊文明的多元性特征也体现在其起源神话中。人们更关注个别社群的起源，而非整个人类种族的起源。

One pattern of such tales was to explain how this or that region or city was not only ancient, but also in some way naturally embedded in the ground where its people lived. A good example is provided by the myth of origin of a community which does not, on the whole, feature prominently in our knowledge of ancient Greece: the island of Aigina, situated between Attica and the Argolid region. The story went that the first inhabitant of the island was Aiakos, son of Zeus and the nymph Aigina, homonym of the island itself: Aiakos could not have been more 'rooted' than that. But he was lonely, for there were no other human beings apart from himself. Out of pity, Zeus changed all the Aiginetan ants (*murmekes*) into humans, who became known as 'Myrmidons'. They were to have a glorious history as warriors: Aiakos' son Peleus became in his turn the father of Achilles, mighty leader of the Myrmidons – who had by then migrated northwards to Phthia in southern Thessaly – in the Trojan War. Aiakos, for his part, would be honoured alongside Minos and Rhadamanthus as one of the judges of the souls of the dead, as befitted one whom Pindar called 'the best of men on earth'. Even on this point, however, we may note the ineradicable pluralism of Greek mythology: in the irresistibly comic vision realized by Aristophanes in the *Frogs*, Aiakos is portrayed as a kind of over-the-top stage villain, revelling in the cruelty to which his role as Hades' doorkeeper entitles him.

> 这类故事的一个模式是解释某一地区或城市不仅古老，而且以某种方式自然地嵌入其居民所居住的土地之中。一个很好的例子是关于一个社区起源的神话，这个社区在我们对古希腊的了解中并不占据重要地位：位于阿提卡与阿尔戈斯地区之间的埃吉纳岛。传说中，埃吉纳岛的第一个居民是阿基俄斯，他是宙斯与仙女埃吉娜的儿子，与岛屿同名。阿基俄斯可谓根深蒂固，无可比拟。然而他却感到孤独，因为除了他之外，再没有其他人类。出于怜悯，宙斯将所有埃吉纳的蚂蚁（murmekes）变成了人类，他们被称为“迈锡尼人”。他们后来以战士的身份拥有了辉煌的历史：阿基俄斯的儿子佩琉斯又成为迈锡尼人的领袖阿喀琉斯的父亲——这些迈锡尼人此时已经迁徙到色萨利南部的菲提亚地区。作为回报，阿基俄斯本人将与米诺斯和拉达曼提斯一同被尊为冥界亡灵的审判者，正如品达所称的“世上最优秀的人”。然而，即使在这一点上，我们也可以注意到希腊神话中根深蒂固的多元性：在阿里斯托芬的喜剧《蛙》中，阿基俄斯被描绘成一个夸张的舞台恶棍，沉迷于他作为冥界守门人所拥有的残酷权力。

<span id="page-123-0"></span>![](_page_123_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The English text to be translated is missing. Please provide the specific content you would like translated into Chinese.

*This vase (south Italian, 4th century BC) depicts a scene in Hades, with Aiakos standing between Triptolemos and Rhadamanthus, both of whom had roles as judges of the dead.*

> 这尊陶罐（南意大利，公元前4世纪）描绘了冥界的场景，阿伊阿科斯站在特里普托勒摩斯和拉达曼提斯之间，其中特里普托勒摩斯和拉达曼提斯均担任亡灵审判官的角色。

Ants – the original, prehuman form of the Myrmidons – live in the ground; and so do snakes. A common mythological way of expressing rootedness – and also sacredness, since snakes were felt to have a numinous quality which associated them with the more-than-human – was to link the ancestry of a community with a serpent. Kekrops, one of the earliest kings of Athens, was imagined as having the upper body of a human, but the tail of a snake. He was a perfect embodiment of autochthony (Apollodoros says explicitly that Kekrops was earth-born), and, by his ambivalent form, mediator of the dichotomy between nature and culture. Through his binary shape, Kekrops expressed the idea of humanity's growth out of animality. He was said to have made a number of important innovations in the Athenians' way of sacrificing to the gods, and also to have introduced the institution of marriage.

> 蚂蚁——迈里蒙人的原始形态，早在人类出现之前便生活在地下；蛇也是如此。一种常见的神话表达方式，用来表达扎根性——同时也表达神圣性，因为蛇被认为具有神秘特质，与超自然存在相联系——是将一个群体的祖先与蛇联系起来。雅典最早的国王之一基克洛普斯（Kekrops）被想象成拥有上半身是人、下半身是蛇的形态。他是本土性的完美体现（阿波罗多罗斯明确指出基克洛普斯是土地所生），并且通过他矛盾的形态，成为自然与文化二元对立的调解者。通过他二元的形态，基克洛普斯表达了人类从动物性中成长出来的理念。据说他为雅典人献祭诸神的方式带来了许多重要革新，还创立了婚姻制度。

<span id="page-124-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_124_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The provided text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you'd like translated into Chinese.

*The mythical Athenian king Kekrops, whose earthborn quality is expressed by his partially snaky form, is here seen in the company of Athene. Between them is an olive tree, symbol of Athene and Athens alike. Attic vase, end of 5th century BC*.

> 神话中的雅典国王刻克罗斯，其大地之子的特质通过他部分蛇形的身躯体现出来，此处他与雅典娜相伴。他们之间是一棵橄榄树，这既是雅典娜的象征，也是雅典的象征。雅典陶罐，公元前5世纪末。

### 4.2 **Theban beginnings**

> 4.5.2 底比斯的起源

A more decisive and violent way of imagining human development depicted it as an aggressive overcoming of animality – the human equivalent of Zeus' defeat of Typhon, and, as we shall see later [\(p. 73\)](#page-149-0), of Apollo's defeat of the serpent Python when the god seized control of the Delphic oracle. The complex foundation myth of Thebes graphically illustrates the point.

> 一种更具决定性和暴力性的想象方式将人类发展描绘为对动物性的侵略性克服——这相当于宙斯战胜提丰，正如我们稍后将看到的（第73页），也相当于阿波罗在夺取德尔斐神谕控制权时战胜蛇形的Python。底比斯复杂的起源神话以直观的方式展示了这一点。

The legendary founder of Thebes was Kadmos, one of the sons of Agenor the king of Phoenicia. When Kadmos' sister Europa was abducted by Zeus, Agenor sent his sons out into the world to search for her. Kadmos' travels brought him to Greece, where he consulted the Delphic oracle. Kadmos was by no means the only enquirer to whom the Delphic priestess gave an unanticipated answer: in his case, the instruction he received was to follow a distinctively-marked cow and to found a city where it lay down to rest. Finding and following the cow, Kadmos came at last to the site of the future Thebes, in Boiotia. Since the best way of expressing gratitude to the gods was to sacrifice the cow to them, and since sacrifice required not just fire

> 底比斯的传奇创始人是卡德摩斯，他是腓尼基国王阿格诺尔的儿子。当卡德摩斯的妹妹欧罗巴被宙斯劫走后，阿格诺尔派他的儿子们到世界各地寻找欧罗巴。卡德摩斯的旅程最终将他带到了希腊，他在那里请教了德尔斐神谕的女祭司。卡德摩斯绝不是唯一一个得到意外回答的询问者：在卡德摩斯的情况下，女祭司指示他跟随一头有明显标记的牛，并在牛休息的地方建立一座城市。找到并跟随这头牛后，卡德摩斯终于到达了未来底比斯的所在地——希腊的玻俄提亚地区。因为向神明表达感激的最佳方式是将牛献祭给神明，而献祭不仅需要火，还需要……

but also purificatory water, Kadmos sought water from a nearby spring. But Greek springs were sacred (see [pp. 188–](#page-417-1)[89\)](#page-421-0), and this one belonged to the god Ares; a monstrous serpent lay there as its menacing guardian. Kadmos slew the serpent, and on the instructions of the goddess Athene sowed its teeth in the earth. Up sprang armed warriors ready for combat; but, once more prompted by Athene, Kadmos threw a stone in the midst of his adversaries. In their consternation they attacked and killed each other – all but five of them, who became the ancestors of later Thebans, under the name of the 'Spartoi' ('Sown Men').

> 但不仅仅是净化用水，卡德摩斯还从附近的泉眼中寻求水源。然而，古希腊的泉水被视为神圣（参见第188–89页），而这眼泉则属于战神阿瑞斯；一条巨大的蛇作为其威慑性的守护者盘踞在那里。卡德摩斯斩杀了那条巨蛇，并在雅典娜女神的指示下，将蛇齿撒入土地。顿时，武装战士们跃然而出，准备战斗；但雅典娜再次引导他，将一块石头掷入敌群中央。他们惊恐万分，互相攻击，最终全部战死，唯有五人幸存，成为后来底比斯人的祖先，被称为“斯巴尔托伊”（意为“播种之人”）。

Thus far, the Thebans' origins were complex enough: myth presented them as originating both from outside Greece (from Kadmos the Phoenician) and from deep within it (those earthborn ancestors, deriving from an 'autochthonous' snake). But the complexity is even greater than that. When Kadmos took a wife, the favour of the gods smiled upon him: he was permitted to marry Harmonia, daughter of Ares and Aphrodite. Their wedding was blessed by the presence of the gods, and their union produced five children. Thanks to intermarriage between the Sown Men and the descendants of Kadmos and Harmonia, the population of Thebes could be said to have grown from the combined seeds of animality, humanity and divinity.

> 到目前为止，底比斯人的起源已经相当复杂：神话将他们描述为既源于希腊境外（来自腓尼基人卡德摩斯），又源自希腊内部深处（那些土生土长的祖先，源自一种“本土的蛇”）。但这种复杂性远不止于此。当卡德摩斯娶妻时，诸神的恩典眷顾着他，允许他娶了赫尔摩尼亚为妻，她是战神阿瑞斯和爱神阿佛洛狄忒的女儿。他们的婚礼受到诸神的祝福，他们的结合生下了五个孩子。由于播种者（即土生土长的祖先）与卡德摩斯和赫尔摩尼亚后裔之间的通婚，可以说底比斯的人口是由动物性、人性和神性的结合种子所生长出来的。

<span id="page-125-0"></span>Not even the illustrious Kadmos lived a life unblemished by sorrow: what mortal did? The fates of his four daughters were as unhappy as they were various: Semele was struck by a thunderbolt when she begged to see her lover Zeus in all his pomp; Ino killed her own child when maddened by Hera; Autonoe and Agaue were the mothers of Aktaion and Pentheus respectively, each of whom died a horrible death for opposing the gods. A descendant of Kadmos' one son, Polydoros, was Oedipus, whose own visit to Delphi was to precipitate the revelation of ills beyond imagining.

> 甚至著名的卡德摩斯也未能过上毫无悲伤的一生：凡人谁能做到呢？他四个女儿的命运同样不幸且各异：塞墨勒因恳求目睹心上人宙斯的威仪而被雷电击中；伊诺在赫拉的诅咒下陷入疯狂，亲手杀死自己的孩子；安托诺厄和阿伽俄是阿卡忒俄斯和彭透斯的生母，他们各自因反抗诸神而惨死。而卡德摩斯之子波吕多罗斯的后裔则是俄狄浦斯，其本人亲自前往德尔斐神殿，却引发了超越想象的苦难的揭示。

The last episode in the tale of Thebes' origins returns us, in a manner of speaking, to where we started. For Kadmos and Harmonia were metamorphosed into snakes, and were, like some other heroes, preserved from death in a perpetual existence in the Elysian Fields. Their humanity had passed away, leaving a distinctive mixture of the animal and the divine.

> 忒拜起源故事的最后一幕以某种方式将我们引回了起点。因为卡德摩斯和哈耳摩尼亚被蜕变成了蛇，如同其他一些英雄一样，他们以永恒的存在被保留在极乐世界中，免于死亡。他们的灵性已消逝，只留下动物与神灵的独特结合。

![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_126_Picture_0.jpeg)

*As various divinities look on from above, Kadmos prepares to hurl a stone at the snake guarding Ares' spring. To the right of the snake is a figure, perhaps personifying the city of Thebes, or the spring itself. South Italian vase,* c*. 330 BC*.

> 各种神祇从上方注视着，卡德摩斯准备向守护阿瑞斯泉源的蛇掷出一块石头。蛇的右侧是一位人物，或许象征着底比斯城，或象征着泉源本身。南意大利陶罐，约公元前330年。

## 5 <span id="page-127-0"></span>*[Bringers of Culture](#page-6-10)*

> 4.6 文化传播者

#### 5.1.1 **Phoroneus, Pelasgos and civilization**

> 4.6.1.1 菲罗纽斯、帕拉斯基奥斯与文明

We have already seen how the Titan Prometheus was credited with introducing not only fire, but also various skills and practices which the Greeks regarded as necessary for human culture (see [pp. 54–55](#page-111-0)). But Prometheus was far from being the only such mythological culture-bringer. As befits the fundamental pluralism of the Greeks' mental universe, it is no surprise to find that different regions, indeed different cities, had their own views about who brought the wherewithal for social life.

> 我们已经看到泰坦普罗米修斯如何被归功于不仅引入了火种，还引入了希腊人视为人类文化必不可少的各种技能和实践（见第54–55页）。但普罗米修斯绝不是唯一的神话中的文化传播者。鉴于希腊人思维宇宙的根本多元性，不同地区，甚至不同城市，对于谁带来了社会生活所需的资源，都有自己不同的看法，这并不令人意外。

The culture-hero of the Peloponnesian city of Argos was, Pausanias tells us, Phoroneus. Few traditions have survived about him, but it is clear that he, no less than Aiakos and Kekrops, was felt to be embedded in his native territory: his father was the local Argive river-god Inachos. Phoroneus was the local equivalent of Prometheus, the bringer of fire (the Argives kept a 'fire of Phoroneus' burning in his honour) and the introducer of that basic quality of human social development, the art of living together in a *polis* ('city', 'citizen state').

> 巴苏安尼阿斯告诉我们，阿尔戈斯城的文化英雄是普罗诺厄斯。关于他的传统记载很少，但显然，他与阿伊阿科斯和基克罗斯一样，被认为深深扎根于本土地区：他的父亲是本地的阿尔戈斯河神伊那科斯。普罗诺厄斯在当地被视为火的带来者（阿尔戈斯人为了纪念他，一直维持着“普罗诺厄斯之火”的燃烧），同时也是人类社会发展基本品质的引入者，即城邦中共同生活的技艺（“城邦”或“公民国家”）。

The Arcadians, however, had a story just as good. To the rest of the Greeks, Arcadia was a backwater, but to the Arcadians themselves, Arcadia had its own narrative of the arrival of civilization. At the beginning, they said, there was Pelasgos, aboriginal inhabitant of the country. (Typifying the Greeks' willingness to turn the eye of common sense upon their myths, Pausanias comments that 'it seems likely that there were other people with Pelasgos and not just himself alone, or else whom could he have ruled over?') Pelasgos began the process of socialization, teaching people to build huts, to wear sheep-skins, and to give up eating leaves, grasses and roots in favour of acorns. Pelasgos' son Lykaon took the process of acculturation further: he founded the city of Lykosoura and introduced athletic competitions in honour of Zeus. But progress is fraught with danger: Lykaon made a massive mistake when he sacrificed a human child on Zeus' altar on Mount Lykaion, and was turned into a wolf as punishment (see [pp. 88](#page-186-0)–[89\)](#page-186-1). Eventually, though, Arcadian culture resumed its forward momentum when Arkas, Lykaon's grandson, introduced a further set of markers of civilization: the cultivation of crops; breadmaking; spinning and weaving.

> 然而，阿卡迪亚人也有一个同样精彩的故事。对于其他希腊人来说，阿卡迪亚是一个偏僻之地，但对阿卡迪亚人自己而言，阿卡迪亚有着自己关于文明到来的叙述。他们说，最初有佩拉斯戈斯，他是这片土地的原住民。（体现希腊人愿意用常识的目光审视自己的神话，帕乌萨尼亚斯评论道：“看来很可能还有其他与佩拉斯戈斯一起的人，而不仅仅是他自己，否则他又能统治谁呢？”）佩拉斯戈斯开始了社会化的进程，教人们建造小屋、穿着羊皮，并放弃食用树叶、草类和根茎，转而以橡果为食。佩拉斯戈斯之子利卡翁则进一步推动了文明化进程：他建立了利科索拉城，并在宙斯的名义下引入了竞技比赛。然而，进步充满危险：利卡翁在利卡翁山上宙斯祭坛上献祭了一个孩童，犯下了巨大的错误，结果被惩罚变成了一只狼（见第88–89页）。尽管如此，最终当利卡翁的孙子阿卡斯引入了另一套文明的标志——种植庄稼、制作面包、纺纱织布后，阿卡迪亚文化再次恢复了前进的动力。

<span id="page-128-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_128_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The English text to be translated is missing. Please provide the specific text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Mount Lykaion, in a remote region of Arcadia. This is the place where, it was said, Lykaon was transformed into a wolf for his wickedness.*

> 吕卡翁山位于希腊阿尔戈斯地区的偏远地区。据说，这是吕卡翁因邪恶行为而被变成狼的地方。

The innovations ascribed to these culture-heroes of the Argives and Arcadians, not to mention the gifts bestowed by Prometheus, illustrate a characteristic pattern, sometimes known as that of the 'first finder': an object or practice deemed in some way typical of or vital to human social life is attributed to a named individual from the mythological past. Sometimes the invention derives from the ingenuity of the gods, like the lyre which Hermes made from tortoiseshell and sheep gut, or the *aulos* (oboe-like double pipe) which Athene made (and then rejected because blowing it made her look ugly) – Hermes and Athene being, of all the deities, especially linked with the notion of cleverness. Other inventions were seen as the product of merely mortal intelligence, albeit of a high order of sophistication: in this respect no one surpassed Daidalos (see [p. 92](#page-193-0)), among whose brilliant feats was said to have been the first creation of the 'walking pose' for figurative statues. Important as such innovations were felt to be for a 'cultivated' existence, they could hardly compare with the gift brought to mortals by Demeter's mortal protégé Triptolemos, who distributed corn, and the arts of its cultivation, to humankind.

> 这些阿卡迪亚人和阿卡亚人的文化英雄所归功的创新，以及普罗米修斯所赐予的礼物，展示了一种特征性的模式，有时被称为“首次发现者”模式：某种被视作人类社会生活典型或至关重要的物品或习俗，往往被归于神话过去中的某个具体人物。有时，这种发明源于神祇的机智，如赫尔墨斯用龟甲和羊肠弦制成的里拉琴，或雅典娜制成的双管笛（后因吹奏时显得丑陋而被她抛弃）——赫尔墨斯和雅典娜在所有神祇中最为人们所认为是机智的象征。其他发明则被视为凡人智慧的产物，尽管这种智慧达到了高度的精巧程度：在这方面，达伊达罗斯（见第92页）无人能及，据说他曾经首创了用于雕像的“行走姿态”。这些创新虽被认为对“文明生活”至关重要，但与得墨忒耳的凡人弟子特里波勒摩斯带给凡人的馈赠相比，却显得微不足道，因为特里波勒摩斯将谷物及其种植技艺传播给了全人类。

<span id="page-129-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_129_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 您提供的待翻译文本缺失，请补充需要翻译的具体内容。

*Triptolemos is one of several mortals to whom Greek myths sometimes assigned a special role after death: in his case, as one of the judges of the dead. But his main province was agricultural fertility. On this Hellenistic gem, he holds a plough in one hand, and a poppy and ears of corn in the other.*

> 特普洛墨斯是几位在死后被希腊神话赋予特殊使命的凡人之一：在他身上，这一使命表现为成为死者的审判者之一。但他的主要领域是农业丰饶。在这枚希腊化时期的宝石上，他一手握着耕犁，另一手握着罂粟和玉米穗。

## 6 <span id="page-130-0"></span>*[Colonies](#page-6-11)*

> 4.7 殖民地

Especially during the Archaic period, but also before and long after it, Greeks exported their civilization to other areas of the Mediterranean world through the establishing of new *poleis* (cities). The whole enterprise of founding these new communities was shot through with ritual procedures, from the standard, preliminary consultation of an oracle (very frequently that of Apollo at Delphi), to the significant role played by seers in the inauguration of the new community, to the transference of sacred fire from the hearth of the mother-city to the equivalent location in the daughter community. But alongside ritual, mythology too functioned as a means of affirming a continuity of cultural experience from mother-city to new foundation. Just as the cities of the Greek homeland grounded their claims to antiquity and cultural priority in the network of mythological narrative, so too 'second-order' foundations – often referred to as 'colonies' – were woven into the same set of tales. Here, then, is yet another kind of origin for which mythology provided legitimation.

> 特别是在古代希腊时期，但也在其之前和之后很长一段时间内，希腊人通过建立新的城邦（polis，即城市）将自己的文明传播到地中海世界的其他地区。建立这些新社区的整个过程都渗透着仪式程序，从最初的占卜（通常是德尔斐的阿波罗神谕）到占卜者在新社区成立时所起的重要作用，再到从母城的灶火中转移动圣火到女儿城的相应位置。然而，除了仪式之外，神话同样发挥着作用，以确认从母城到新建立的城邦之间文化体验的连续性。正如希腊本土的城邦依靠神话叙事来证明其古老和文化优先地位一样，这些“次级城邦”——通常被称为“殖民地”——也被编织进相同的神话故事中。因此，神话又为另一种起源提供了正当性。

One example of such legitimation is associated with the 5th century BC foundation of the Sicilian city of Aitna. This was indeed a fresh beginning, inaugurated by the despot Hieron when he established a new community to be ruled over by his son. Weaving a celebration of this event into a poem (*Pythian* 1) about Hieron's Pythian chariot victory in the year 470, Pindar establishes an intricate set of parallels between, on the one hand, Hieron's act of foundation – which is presented as imposing the values of firm government and bountiful rule upon a raw and untamed landscape – and, on the other hand, Zeus' primordial overwhelming of his hundredheaded adversary Typhon. This adversary, although pinioned for all time beneath Mount Aitna (i.e. Etna), retains the terrible power to pour forth 'pure streams of unapproachable fire from the hidden places within', as Pindar puts it. The poem's mythological imagery enforces not only a sense of the remarkable achievement which Aitna's foundation is held to represent, but also a sense of the fragility of any human claim to be able definitively to tame this unpredictable landscape.

> 这种合法化的例子之一与公元前5世纪在西西里岛建立艾特纳城的事件有关。这确实是一个全新的开始，由暴君希埃隆发起，他创建了一个由其子统治的新社区。品达在《皮媞亚颂第1首》中，将这一事件融入一首颂诗，以纪念希埃隆于公元前470年赢得皮媞亚赛马冠军的胜利。品达通过这首诗，巧妙地将希埃隆建立艾特纳城的行为与宙斯战胜百头巨怪泰丰的神话事件进行复杂而细致的类比。一方面，希埃隆的建城行为被描述为将稳固的统治和富饶的治理强加于一片原始而未驯服的自然景观；另一方面，宙斯则以原始的力量战胜了泰丰。尽管泰丰被永久地锁链束缚于艾特纳山（即埃特纳山）之下，但其可怕的威力依然存在，能够从隐藏的深处涌出纯净的、不可接近的火焰，正如品达所描述的那样。这首诗中的神话意象不仅强化了艾特纳城建立所象征的非凡成就，也突显了人类任何宣称能够彻底驯服这种不可预测自然景观的主张所具有的脆弱性。

For a further example of the way in which myth-tellers shaped perceptions of colonial foundation, we may think back for a moment to the tale of the foundation of Thebes, with its narrative of the cow which guided Kadmos, and the overcoming of the serpent of Ares, before the new city could be established. Thebes is, so to speak, a 'first-order' foundation – in the heart of mainland Greece itself. But literally dozens of narratives about colonial foundation much further afield use the same story-patterns. Cows feature regularly as guides, but other animals which guide founders to the sites of their future settlements include crows, dogs, doves, eagles, foxes, rats and seals. As for the symbolic overcoming of a beastly adversary, snakes – those typical 'indigenous' inhabitants of the soil – appear very frequently; but we

> 为了进一步说明神话讲述者如何塑造人们对殖民奠基的认知，我们可以回想起忒拜城建立的故事。这个故事讲述了卡德摩斯被一头牛引导，以及在新城市建立之前克服阿瑞斯之蛇的叙事。可以说，忒拜是一个“第一级”的奠基——位于希腊大陆腹地。然而，实际上在更遥远地区关于殖民奠基的数十个故事中，都使用了相同的叙事模式。牛经常作为引导者出现，但其他引导奠基者找到未来定居地的动物还包括乌鸦、狗、鸽子、鹰、狐狸、老鼠和海豹。至于象征性地克服野蛮对手，蛇——这些典型的“土著”土壤居民——则频繁出现；但我们

hear of many other animals also. Byzas, founder of Byzantium, had to overcome a bull; Seleucus Nicator (a 4th/3rd century BC Seleucid ruler in the post-Alexander Hellenistic East) killed a boar which sprang out at him, and used its blood to trace out the perimeter of the new city of Laodicea in northern Syria.

> 听说还有许多其他动物的故事。拜占庭的创始人拜占庭曾战胜一头公牛；塞琉古·尼卡托尔（公元前4至3世纪塞琉古王国的统治者，亚历山大大帝之后希腊化时期的东方统治者）曾杀死一头突然冲向他的野猪，并用它的血勾勒出新城市拉迪凯亚（位于叙利亚北部）的边界。

Throughout antiquity, Greek myths provided a language in which to confer legitimacy through the invoking of the notion of origin. Any new foundation needed all the help it could get, practical as well as symbolic, to shore it up against potential foes. To this end, the rich symbolic treasury of the mythological network was exploited to the full.

> 在古代，希腊神话提供了一种通过援引起源概念来赋予合法性的语言。任何新的基础都需要尽可能多的帮助，既有实际的也有象征性的，以巩固其抵御潜在敌人的能力。为此，神话网络中丰富的象征性资源被充分利用。

<span id="page-131-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_131_Picture_2.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*The Sicilian city of Kamarina was a colony of Syracuse; Syracuse itself had been settled by Greeks from Corinth. The citizens of both Kamarina and Syracuse used myths to express their cities' identities. The silver coin illustrated here (415–405 BC) comes from Kamarina.*

> 西西里岛的城市卡马琳是叙拉古的殖民地；叙拉古本身是由科林斯的希腊人建立的。卡马琳和叙拉古的市民都利用神话来表达他们城市的身份。此处展示的银币（公元前415–405年）来自卡马琳。

*One side (left) shows Athene driving a chariot, with the winged figure of Nike ('Victory') holding a crown above her; the other side (right) portrays Herakles, unmistakable in his lionskin.*

> 左侧描绘的是雅典娜驾战车，身旁有翼神尼刻（胜利）手持冠冕高举于她之上；右侧则刻画了赫拉克勒斯，他身披狮子皮，身份 unmistakably 显而易见。

<span id="page-132-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_132_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 希腊英雄赫拉克勒斯的故事

*At over 3,300 m (10,800 ft) high, Mount Etna (ancient Greek form: Aitna) is Europe's highest active volcano. It was active too in antiquity: its constant stream of smoke and lava was believed to derive from Zeus' monstrous foe Typhon, vanquished but still wrathful, imprisoned for eternity under the volcano.*

> 海拔3300米（10800英尺）的埃特纳山（古希腊语形式：Aitna）是欧洲最高的活火山。在古代它也十分活跃：人们相信它不断喷出的烟柱和岩浆源自宙斯可怕的对手堤丰，堤丰虽已被击败，但仍充满愤怒，被永远囚禁在火山之下。

# 5 <span id="page-133-0"></span>[III The Olympians: Power, Honour, Sexuality](#page-6-12)

> 5 III 奥林匹斯众神：权力、荣誉、性

<span id="page-133-2"></span><span id="page-133-1"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_133_Picture_1.jpeg)

> Please provide the English text you want translated.

*Dionysos reclines by the side of his bride Ariadne, a mortal woman raised to the condition of unageing divinity so that she might be the god's lovely consort for ever. From a large bronze* krater *(*c. *330 BC) found at Derveni near Thessaloniki (see ill. on [p. 83\)](#page-172-0).*

> 狄奥尼索斯倚靠在新娘亚里阿德涅身旁，这位凡人女子被提升至不朽神性的境地，以便成为这位神永恒美丽的伴侣。此图来自在萨洛尼基附近德尔维尼出土的一件大型青铜酒罐（约公元前330年）（见第83页插图）。

#### 0.1.1 *Divinities of Myth and Cult*

> 5.1.1.1 神话与宗教中的神明

Throughout classical antiquity, from one end of the Greek world to another, the gods who dwelt on Mount Olympos were the recipients of worship. Through sacrifices, votive offerings, prayers, oaths, curses and incantations, mortals involved their gods in every conceivable human activity, from politics to love-making, from warfare to

> 在整个古典时期，从希腊世界的这一端到另一端，居住在奥林匹斯山上的诸神都是受到崇拜的对象。通过献祭、许愿供品、祈祷、誓言、诅咒和咒语，人类将诸神卷入了所有可以想象到的人类活动，从政治到恋爱，从战争到……

burial, from athletic competition to the public performance of drama and music. No neat separation can therefore be made between 'religion' and 'society': each pervaded the other. What were the distinctive contributions made by cult and myth to the Greeks' perceptions of the sacred?

> 埋葬、竞技比赛，直到戏剧和音乐的公开展示，宗教与社会之间因此无法明确区分：彼此相互渗透。崇拜和神话在希腊人对神圣事物的感知中各自作出了哪些独特贡献？

Cult tended to focus on specific features of divine power, singling out the particular quality in virtue of which a god was being invoked: Zeus Horkios ('of oaths'), Poseidon Hippios ('of horses'), Athene Poliouchos ('holder of the citadel'), and so forth. Moreover, different localities highlighted quite different characteristics of a given divinity. On the evidence of cult alone, therefore, it is impossible to speak of a single, homogeneous Greek religion.

> 祭祀往往侧重于神灵权力的特定方面，突出地强调被呼唤的神灵所具有的特定品质：如宙斯·赫尔基奥斯（掌管誓言）、波塞冬·希皮奥斯（掌管马匹）、雅典娜·波里乌赫奥斯（城塞守护者）等。此外，不同地区还突出了同一神灵的完全不同的特征。因此，仅凭祭祀活动的证据，我们无法谈论一种单一而统一的希腊宗教。

In mythological narratives, by contrast – and here one thinks first, as so often, of the Homeric and Hesiodic poems – we may detect a tendency to articulate the discrete behaviours of ritual into something approaching an interrelated system, depicting a shared and sometimes even 'Panhellenic' view of divine power and action. Still, the degree of internal coherence in this system should not be exaggerated: one should never underestimate the pluralism of Greek culture. Just as rituals varied from place to place and from historical period to historical period, so did the ways in which different myth-tellers portrayed those mighty figures of the imagination, the Olympian divinities.

> 在神话叙事中，相比之下——这里首先让人想到的是荷马和赫西俄德的史诗——我们可以察觉到一种趋势，即将分散的仪式行为转化为一种接近相互关联的体系，描绘出一种共享的、有时甚至可以说是“泛希腊”的神力与行动观。然而，这一体系内部的连贯性程度不应被夸大：人们不应低估希腊文化的多元性。正如仪式因地点和历史时期的不同而各异，同样，不同神话讲述者对这些想象中的强大人物——奥林匹斯神祇的描绘方式也各不相同。

### 0.2 <span id="page-135-0"></span>*[Powers and Spheres of Influence](#page-6-13)*

> 5.1.2 权能与影响领域

## 1 *Greek Divinities and their Principal Attributes*

> 5.2 希腊神祇及其主要属性

|------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Zeus       | sky, weather; numerous aspects of<br>social life, including hospitality,<br>supplication, oaths; 'father of gods and<br>mortals'                              | thunderbolt, eagle, sceptre                                                                                                              |
| Hera       | consort of Zeus; consumed by<br>jealousy at his serial infidelity;<br>supports the integrity of marriage                                                      | often a sceptre or crown;<br>peacock                                                                                                     |
| Poseidon   | sea; earthquake; the raw energy of the<br>horse and the bull                                                                                                  | trident; typically surrounded by<br>sea-creatures                                                                                        |
