|------|--------------|--------|
| 赫尔墨斯 | 神界使者；亡灵引路者；传递信息者；使 flock 肥育 | 长笛（kerukeion）；双翼凉鞋；宽边帽 |
| 酒神狄俄尼索斯 | 欢愉；醉态；酒；动物与植物的狂野与危险 | 常春藤；葡萄藤；豹；藤杖（thyrsos）；以常春藤绑扎的芹菜杖 |
| 潘 | 孤独的乡村自然之神；引发恐慌 | 半人半羊的混合形态 |
| 普里阿波斯 | 性欲之神 | 巨大且持久的勃起 |
| 爱神厄罗斯 | 性欲 | 弓与箭 |

#### 1.1.1 **Zeus**

> 5.2.1.1 宙斯

Related as he is, etymologically speaking, to other Indo-European sky gods (compare Indic Dyaus pitar, Roman Diespiter/Jupiter), Zeus has at the centre of his being a configuration of what we would describe as 'natural phenomena': not only sky, but also weather, storm, lightning, and thunderbolt. Already in Homer, one of the god's epithets is *nephelegereta*, 'marshaller of clouds'. When Semele, a mortal who caught Zeus' roving eye, begged her lover to appear to her in his full glory, he was obliged to do so as Lord of Lightning, with predictably fatal results. Countless images portray Zeus with thunderbolt in hand.

> 从词源学角度来看，宙斯与其他印欧语系的天空之神（如印度的迪亚马斯·皮塔尔，罗马的迪斯皮特/朱庇特）有着密切联系。然而，宙斯的本质核心却体现着我们所称的“自然现象”：不仅包括天空，还包括天气、风暴、闪电和雷霆。在荷马史诗中，宙斯的一个称号便是“云之统帅”（nephelegereta）。当凡人塞墨勒因爱上了宙斯而请求他以全貌显现时，宙斯不得不以雷霆之主的身份出现，结果却导致了不可避免的致命结局。无数艺术形象都描绘着宙斯手持雷霆的形象。

This terrifying power translates into various other kinds of supremacy. First, there is domination by sheer physical strength. Again we may turn to the *Iliad*, in a passage where Zeus boasts of his awesome superiority even to the other Olympians:

> 这种令人惊惧的力量体现为各种其他形式的至高无上。首先，是凭借纯粹体力的支配。我们再次可以参考《伊利亚特》中的一段，其中宙斯夸耀自己甚至对其他奥林匹斯神祇也具有令人敬畏的优越地位：

Let down out of the sky a cord of gold; take hold of it all you who are gods and all who are goddesses, yet not even so could you drag down Zeus from the sky to the ground, not Zeus the high lord of counsel, though you tried until you grew weary. Yet whenever I might strongly be minded to pull you,

> 从天空中垂下一根金绳，所有诸神和所有女神都抓住它，但即使如此，你们也无法将那位高高在上的智慧之主宙斯从天空拽到地面，尽管你们尝试到筋疲力尽。然而，只要我有意将你拉下来，

I could drag you up, earth and all and sea and all with you, then tie the golden rope about a peak of Olympos

> 我可以用力量把你拉上来，连同大地和海洋一起，然后将金绳系在奥林匹斯山的峰顶上。

and make it fast, so that all should dangle in mid air.

> 并将它系牢，使一切悬吊在空中。

So much stronger am I than the gods, and stronger than mortals.

> 我比诸神强大得多，也比凡人强大。

To supremacy by brute force we may add supremacy through sovereignty. Zeus' symbolic equivalent in the animal world is the eagle, lord of birds; another of the god's attributes is the sceptre, which confers authority upon human rulers. Yet the sovereignty of mere mortals pales beside that of Zeus, whose will is enacted with a nod of the head.

> 以武力取得的至高无上，我们还可以加上以主权取得的至高无上。在动物界中，宙斯的象征化身是鹰，即鸟之王；另一个宙斯的属性是权杖，它赋予人类统治者以权威。然而，凡人的主权相比之下显得黯然失色，因为宙斯的意志只需以点头即可实现。

<span id="page-138-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_138_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*One of the symbols of Zeus' authority is the eagle, mightiest of birds, as depicted on this Lakonian cup,* c. *560 BC. Zeus himself is bearded, long-haired, enthroned, and clad in an elaborate cloak.*

> 宙斯权威的象征之一是鹰，即鸟类中最强大的生物，如这件约公元前560年的拉科尼亚陶杯所描绘的。宙斯蓄有胡须，长发飘逸，端坐在宝座上，身披华丽的长袍。

For another aspect of Zeus' dominion we must look to the context of the family. An epithet consistently bestowed upon Zeus is 'father of gods and men'. As regards the generality of mankind, this paternity is metaphorical; but many of the gods were quite literally his children. He stood at the head of the family of the Olympians – an unruly family, though, and one whose members were quite capable of challenging, if only for a time, the authority of their head. Modelled as it was on an institution of human society, the concept of the divine family was an image (unlike, for instance, the notion of god-as-thunderbolt) which brought the gods' behaviour at least partly within the framework of the comprehensible.

> 为了理解宙斯统治的另一层面，我们必须关注家庭这一背景。一个始终被赋予宙斯的称号是“众神和人类之父”。就全人类而言，这种父性是隐喻性的；但许多神祇实际上确实是他亲生的孩子。他在奥林匹斯神族中居于首位——这是一个桀骜不驯的家族，其成员完全可能在一段时间内挑战他们首领的权威。这个神祇家族的概念是以人类社会的制度为模型建立的，因此它是一种形象（不同于诸如“雷电之神”这样的概念），至少使神祇的行为部分地纳入了可理解的范畴。

How far Zeus' authority can be said to be *moral* is a very complex question. On the one hand, myth (for instance, Hesiodic myth) depicts him as father of Dike ('Right') and consort of Themis ('Due Order'); he is also represented as the punisher of oath-breakers (as Zeus Horkios) and of those who reject suppliants (as Zeus Hikesios). On the other hand, Zeus' own conduct places him well beyond the norms of *human* morality – witness his incest with his sister Hera, and his serial adultery. There is a tension at the heart of the portrayal of the father of the gods, for he can be thought of as simultaneously the source of right and the source of everything.

> 宙斯的权威在多大程度上可以被视为道德，这是一个非常复杂的问题。一方面，神话（例如赫西俄德的神话）将他描绘为正义（Dike）之父和命运（Themis）之配偶；他同时也被表现为对毁誓者（如宙斯·赫尔基奥斯）和拒绝求援者（如宙斯·希基奥斯）的惩罚者。另一方面，宙斯自身的行为已远远超越了人类道德的规范——例如他与妹妹赫拉的乱伦，以及他多次的通奸行为。众神之父的形象中存在着内在的张力，因为一方面他可以被视为正义的源泉，另一方面他又是万物的源泉。

<span id="page-140-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_140_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*Every four years representatives from all over the Greek world assembled in Elis to celebrate the Olympic Games. The Games were held in honour of Zeus, whose head adorns this coin (4th century BC) struck in Elis.*

> 每四年，来自希腊各地的代表齐聚埃利斯，庆祝奥林匹克运动会。这些运动会是为宙斯举行的，宙斯的头像装饰着这枚（公元前4世纪）在埃利斯铸造的硬币。

<span id="page-141-0"></span>![](_page_141_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*The ruins of Zeus' great temple at Olympia (*c*. 470 BC). The temple was a treasurehouse of mythology: the metopes represented the twelve Labours of Herakles; among the sculptured reliefs on the pediments were the preliminaries to the chariot race between Pelops and Oinomaos; inside the temple was Pheidias' world-renowned statue of Zeus, which alas no longer survives.*

> 奥林匹亚宙斯宏伟神庙的遗迹（约公元前470年）。这座神庙是神话的宝库：庙宇的浮雕展示了赫拉克勒斯的十二项伟业；山墙上的浮雕则描绘了珀洛普斯与欧诺玛俄斯赛车前的种种情节；庙内曾矗立着菲迪亚斯所雕刻的举世闻名的宙斯雕像，可惜这尊雕像已不复存在。

No less difficult than the question of Zeus' morality is the question of the limits of his power. Here is a prime example of the effect which the pluralism of Greek mythology can have. Some of our texts speak of the perfection and completeness of Zeus. Amongst these texts is the remarkable *Hymn* by the Stoic Kleanthes (4th/3rd century BC), who elevates Zeus to a kind of universal principle:

> 与探讨宙斯道德问题一样困难的，是探讨其权力界限的问题。这里有一个希腊神话多元性所产生的影响的典型例子。一些文本提到宙斯的完美与完备性。其中就包括公元前4世纪至3世纪斯多葛学派的克雷安提斯所作的引人注目的赞美诗，他将宙斯提升到一种普遍原则的高度：

This entire cosmos which moves round the earth follows where you lead it, and is willingly mastered by you… Nothing happens in the world without you, god, neither in the divine air of the sky, nor in the sea, except for that which evil men do in their folly.

> 这个围绕地球运转的宇宙，皆跟随你引导的方向，甘愿被你掌控……没有你的存在，世间万物都不会发生，无论是天界的神圣之气，还是海洋，唯独邪恶之人愚昧中所行之事除外。

But other texts depict Zeus as emphatically within, rather than above, the causality of events. Again it is the *Iliad*, the location of so many of the strands of later Greek belief, which touches on the central issues. In a pivotal and deeply moving scene, Zeus looks down upon the impending death of one of his own sons, the Trojan warrior Sarpedon. Zeus could in theory intervene to save him, even though this would overturn what is 'allotted'; but, as Hera is quick to point out, by doing so he would arouse the implacable hostility of the other gods. So even Zeus must bow to the inevitable:

> 但其他文本将宙斯描绘为明确地处于事件的因果关系之中，而非凌驾其上。再次，正是《伊利亚特》，这后来希腊信仰众多线索的汇聚之地，触及了核心问题。在一个关键而感人至深的场景中，宙斯俯视着自己儿子、特洛伊战士萨尔珀冬即将来临的死亡。理论上，宙斯可以干预以拯救他，尽管这将颠覆“命运”的安排；但赫拉迅速指出，这样做将引发其他神祇的不可调和的敌意。因此，就连宙斯也必须屈服于不可避免的命运：

So she spoke, nor did the father of gods and men disobey her; but he wept tears of blood that poured to the ground, honouring his beloved son, whom Patroklos was presently to kill, by generous Troy and far from the land of his fathers.

> 于是她说道，众神与人类之父并未违抗她；但他流下血泪，流至地面，以悼念他心爱的儿子，正是帕特罗克洛斯即将在繁荣的特洛伊杀死他，远离他的故土。

#### 1.1.2 <span id="page-142-0"></span>**Hera**

> 5.2.1.2 赫拉

As the sister and consort of Zeus, the great goddess Hera was worshipped at sanctuaries throughout the Greek world. Especially renowned were one temple on the island of Samos and another between Argos and Mycenae; but she also had major shrines at Olympia, at Perachora near Corinth, and at south Italian sites such as Croton, Paestum and Foce del Sele. The progression which typifies Hera's rites in these sanctuaries is the transition from virgin to bride. In some localities, cults celebrated her as Parthenos ('Maiden'); elsewhere it was her presiding over the institution of marriage (e.g. as Zugia, 'She who Unites') which concentrated the worshipper's attention; in still other places she was worshipped under both aspects, as in the Boiotian city of Plataea, where she had, Pausanias reports, two statues, one as Numpheuomene ('Bride') and one as Teleia ('Consummated').

> 作为宙斯的姐妹和配偶，伟大的女神赫拉在希腊各地的圣地受到崇拜。其中尤以萨摩斯岛上的一个神庙和位于阿尔戈斯与迈锡尼之间的另一个神庙最为著名；但她也在奥林匹亚、科林斯附近的珀拉科拉，以及南意大利的克罗顿、帕埃斯图姆和福切德尔塞莱等地设有重要的神庙。这些圣地中赫拉仪式的典型演变过程是少女向新娘的过渡。在一些地方，崇拜者以“少女”（Parthenos）之名祭祀她；在另一些地方，她则以主持婚姻制度（例如作为“联合者”Zugia）而受到崇拜；而在其他地方，她则同时以这两种形象被崇拜，例如在比奥提亚的普拉提亚城，帕乌萨尼亚斯记载，她有两尊雕像，一尊是“新娘”（Numpheuomene），一尊是“完婚”（Teleia）。

<span id="page-142-1"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_142_Picture_3.jpeg)

> Please provide the specific English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*The temple of Hera at Olympia (*c. *600 BC), the oldest temple in the sacred precinct. It was dwarfed by the later temple of Zeus.*

> 奥林匹亚的赫拉神庙（约公元前600年），圣地内最古老的神庙。它被后来的宙斯神庙所超越。

Given the prominence and geographical spread of Hera's sanctuaries, it is somewhat paradoxical that Hera's place in mythology is relatively sub-ordinate, being dominated, in virtually every respect, by her role as consort of Zeus. Occasionally her myths hark back to her pre-nuptial days, such as the time when Zeus wooed her in the form of a cuckoo. But much more often her mythology dwells on a related but more sombre theme: her vindictiveness towards Zeus' lovers and their children. Her pursuit of the pregnant goddess Leto continued relentlessly

> 鉴于赫拉神庙的显赫地位及其在地理上的广泛分布，她在我神话中的地位相对次要，几乎在所有方面都被她作为宙斯的妻子这一角色所主导。偶尔，她的神话会追溯到她婚前的时光，例如宙斯化身为布谷鸟向她求爱的时期。但更常见的是，她的神话围绕着一个相关但更为阴暗的主题展开：她对宙斯情人们的报复及其子女。她对怀孕的女神勒托的追捕毫不停歇。

throughout the length and breadth of the Greek world, until at last the humble island of Delos was willing to accept the anguished mother-to-be, soon to give birth to the glorious children Apollo and Artemis. When Io, Hera's own mortal priestess at Argos, found favour with Zeus, Hera's spite took an ingenious form: transformed into a cow, Io wandered from land to land, driven mad by the stinging of the fly which the queen of the gods sent against her. Zeus' liaison with another mortal, Semele, provoked Hera to equal fury. First Hera tricked her rival into precipitating her own death by asking to see her lover in the shattering fullness of his cosmic power. Then she turned her attention to Dionysos, Semele's son by Zeus. She drove him mad, and caused two mortals (Athamas and his wife Ino) who had dared to nurse the infant god, to murder their own children in fits of insanity.

> 在整个希腊世界范围内，直到最后，这平凡的德尔岛才愿意接受这位痛苦的准母亲，她即将诞下辉煌的子女阿波罗和阿尔忒弥斯。当伊俄——赫拉在阿尔戈斯的女祭司——赢得宙斯的青睐时，赫拉的报复以巧妙的形式呈现：她被变成一头牛，四处游荡于各国，被众神之王（赫拉）派遣来对付她的苍蝇的叮咬所逼疯。宙斯与另一位凡人塞墨勒的私情激怒了赫拉。首先，赫拉设法让她的对手请求亲眼目睹她的情人释放其宇宙力量的全部威力，从而导致她自己的死亡。接着，她将目光转向了塞墨勒与宙斯之子狄俄尼索斯。她使他发疯，并导致两位凡人（阿塔马斯和他的妻子英诺）因疯狂而杀害了自己的孩子，因为他们曾胆敢哺育婴儿神明。

The list of Hera's vendettas could be considerably dwelt upon; but they pale beside her all-consuming hatred for Herakles, Zeus' son by yet another of his mortal lovers, Alkmene. When Alkmene was about to be delivered, Zeus proclaimed before the gods (*Iliad* Book 19) that the honour of ruling over Mycenae was to be the birthright of that descendant of his own who was about to be born. It was Herakles for whom Zeus believed he was predicting glory. But he reckoned without the intervention of Hera. For there was another child soon to be born in the Peloponnese, namely Eurystheus, who, as grandson of another of Zeus' sons – Perseus – would also qualify as 'a descendant of Zeus'. Hera persuaded the Eileithyiai (goddesses of childbirth) to block Alkmene's delivery until Eurystheus' mother had been prematurely delivered. Thereafter, Hera never relented in her persecution of Herakles – whose name, with supreme irony, meant 'Glory of Hera'. Subject as he was to the domination of the inferior and cowardly hero Eurystheus, Herakles nevertheless completed astonishing exploits; but at the height of his powers he was driven insane by Hera, so that he slaughtered his own wife and children (see [p. 122](#page-267-0)).

> 赫拉对复仇的清单可以展开详述，但相比之下，她对海格力斯的深仇大恨更为突出。海格力斯是宙斯与另一位凡人情人阿尔克墨涅的儿子。当阿尔克墨涅即将分娩时，宙斯在众神面前（《伊利亚特》第19卷）宣称，统治迈锡尼的荣誉将属于他即将诞生的儿子，即他自己的后裔。宙斯认为他预言的是海格力斯的荣耀。然而，他却低估了赫拉的干预。因为不久之后，伯罗奔尼撒还会有另一个孩子诞生，即尤斯提斯，他是宙斯之子珀尔修斯的孙子，因此也可以说是“宙斯的后裔”。赫拉说服了埃莱提亚女神（生育之神）阻止阿尔克墨涅分娩，直到尤斯提斯的母亲提前分娩。此后，赫拉对海格力斯的迫害从未停止——而海格力斯的名字，以极端讽刺的方式，意为“赫拉的荣耀”。尽管他受制于懦弱无能的英雄尤斯提斯的支配，海格力斯仍完成了令人惊叹的壮举；然而在他力量巅峰之时，赫拉却使他发了狂，导致他杀害了自己的妻子和孩子（见第122页）。

<span id="page-144-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_144_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*The 'sacred marriage' of Zeus and Hera is evoked on this metope (Temple E, Selinus, Sicily;* c. *470 BC). By gripping Hera's wrist Zeus seems to be trying to prevent her from veiling herself. His languid pose may be a hint at his amorous intentions.*

> 这件浮雕描绘了宙斯与赫拉的“神圣婚姻”（塞利努斯的E殿，西西里岛；约公元前470年）。宙斯紧握赫拉的手腕，似乎试图阻止她遮住脸。他慵懒的姿态可能暗示了他热烈的爱意。

Hera's cults and myths turn predominantly upon the theme of marriage, but with contrasting emphases. Whereas her cults stress the fundamental necessity of this socio-psychological transition, the myths explore the reverse side of the picture – what happens when fulfilment gives way to the bitterness of rejection. But not the least of the paradoxes concerning the great queen of Olympos is that her triumphs were only temporary. Even Herakles, whom she thwarted and humiliated at every turn, succeeded at last in joining the company of the gods on Olympos.

> 赫拉的崇拜与神话主要围绕婚姻主题展开，但侧重点却各有不同。她的崇拜强调这一社会心理转变的根本必要性，而神话则探索了画面的另一面——当满足感让位于被拒的苦涩时会发生什么。但奥林匹斯大女王所面临的悖论之一便是，她的胜利终究只是暂时的。就连她曾处处阻挠并羞辱的赫拉克勒斯，最终也成功加入了奥林匹斯诸神的行列。

#### 1.1.3 **Poseidon**

> 5.2.1.3 波塞冬

When Kronos' inheritance was divided, his sons received the portions which would locate and shape their powers for ever afterwards. Zeus took the sky, Hades the Underworld; to Poseidon was allotted the sea. The Mediterranean in general, and the Aegean in particular, was and is two-faced: mirror-smooth one day, tempestuous the next. Correspondingly, the sovereignty of Poseidon was imagined as expressing itself both through serene authority and through raw, explosive violence. Nowhere is this latter capacity more graphically described than in Homer's *Odyssey*, where Poseidon, in revenge for Odysseus' blinding of the Cyclops (Poseidon's son), uses every weapon in his armoury to impede the homecoming of Odysseus to Ithaca:

> 当克罗诺斯的遗产被分割时，他的儿子们各自获得了确定并塑造其力量的部分。宙斯获得了天空，哈迪斯获得了冥界，波塞冬则被分配到海洋。地中海总体上，特别是爱琴海，历来都是双面的：一天平静如镜，第二天却狂风暴雨。相应地，波塞冬的统治权被想象为既通过平静的权威表现出来，也通过原始而爆发性的暴力展现。这种后一种能力在荷马的《奥德赛》中被最为生动地描述，其中，波塞冬因奥德修斯刺瞎其子（波塞冬之子）独眼巨人而怀恨在心，动用武器库中的每一件武器，阻碍奥德修斯返回伊萨卡的归途：

So he spoke, and drew the clouds together, in his hands gripping the trident, and stirred up the sea, and urged on all the stormblasts of all the winds together, and swathed earth and sea alike in cloud. Night sprang from heaven. East Wind and South Wind clashed together, and the bitter blown West Wind and the North Wind born in the bright air rolled up a heavy sea.

> 他开口说道，双手紧握三叉戟，将云彩聚拢，激起海浪，驱使所有风的狂暴风暴，用云雾裹住大地和海洋。夜幕自天而降，东风与南风交锋，而刺骨的西风与在明亮空气中诞生的北风卷起滔天巨浪。

As symbol of his stormy energy Poseidon wields the trident, used by fisherfolk to spear their prey, but by the god of the deep to raise havoc among the waves.

> 作为他狂暴能量的象征，波塞冬持着三叉戟，这工具被渔民用来叉捕猎物，但对海神而言，却用来在海浪中掀起混乱。

Although Poseidon's power centres on the sea, on land too he stirs up the turbulence of nature. He is Ennosigaios, 'Shaker of the Earth', prone to punishing his foes by breaking up the ground where they stand. Such activity is by no means confined to the 'long ago': Thucydides records that it was to Poseidon that people attributed the catastrophic Spartan earthquake of 464 BC.

> 尽管波塞冬的力量主要集中在海洋，但在陆地上他也搅动自然的动荡。他被称为“地动神”（Ennosigaios），习惯于通过摧毁敌人站立之地来惩罚他们。这种活动远不限于“很久以前”：修昔底德记载，人们将公元前464年发生的灾难性斯巴达地震归因于波塞冬。

The third form of natural energy represented and released by Poseidon is that of animals. He is linked especially closely with the horse, through the cult of Poseidon Hippios. Several myths express this proximity in graphically sexual terms. For example, according to a story associated with Thessaly and Athens, it was when the god's semen spurted onto a rock that the first horse was conceived. Another myth with a similar implication derives from Arcadia: when Demeter turned herself into a mare in order to avoid the attentions of Poseidon, his metamorphosis into a stallion enabled him to fulfil his desire.

> 波塞冬所表现并释放的第三种自然能量是动物的能量。他特别紧密地与马联系在一起，这通过波塞冬·希皮奥斯（Poseidon Hippios）的神庙崇拜体现出来。多个神话以隐晦的性暗示方式表达了这种亲近关系。例如，根据与色萨利和雅典相关的一个故事，当神的精液喷洒在岩石上时，第一匹马便诞生了。另一个有类似含义的神话源自阿尔戈斯：当德墨忒尔为了躲避波塞冬的追求而将自己变成一匹母马时，他变成公马的变形使他得以实现欲望。

<span id="page-145-0"></span>For a terrifying dramatization of how Poseidon's power expresses itself through the raw energy of beasts, we need look no further than Euripides' tragedy *Hippolytos*. The eponymous hero, cursed by his own father Theseus (who mistakenly believes him to have violated the honour of Phaidra, Theseus' wife and Hippolytos' stepmother), is being forced into exile. As his chariot drives along the seashore, a monstrous bull manifests itself from the ocean, driving Hippolytos' horses into a frenzy which precipitates a fatal crash. The curse whose invocation

> 若想恐怖地展现波塞冬的力量如何通过野兽的原始能量表现出来，我们无需远眺，只需回顾欧里庇得斯的悲剧《海伦》。这位同名英雄因自己的父亲忒修斯（误以为他侵犯了妻子菲德拉的名誉，而菲德拉正是忒修斯的妻子，也是海伦的继母）的诅咒而被迫流亡。当他乘坐的战车驶过海岸时，一头怪物般的公牛从海洋中显现，驱使海伦的战马陷入狂乱，最终导致致命的翻车事故。这个诅咒的召唤……

called the bull into existence was granted to Theseus by Poseidon (who in this version of the myth is Theseus' father). The congruence of foaming sea and awesome beast epitomizes Poseidon's placing within the Greeks' world of the sacred.

> 将公牛唤入存在的能力被授予忒修斯，由波塞冬（在这一神话版本中，他是忒修斯的父亲）赐予。翻腾的海浪与威猛野兽的结合，体现了波塞冬在希腊人神圣世界中的地位。

<span id="page-147-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_147_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 您需要提供具体的英文文本以便进行翻译。当前未给出需要翻译的内容。

*This image of the trident-wielding Poseidon is from a coin,* c. *530 BC, of the city of Poseidonia (Paestum, southeast of Naples). The inscription 'POS' represents the beginning of the name of both the city and the god.*

> 这幅手持三叉戟的波塞冬图像来自一枚约公元前530年的硬币，该硬币出自波塞冬尼亚城（帕埃斯图姆，位于那不勒斯东南部）。铭文“POS”代表该城和神名的开头。

#### 1.1.4 **Demeter**

> 5.2.1.4 得墨忒耳

As the goddess associated with corn, Demeter symbolizes the source of the bread which formed the Greeks' staple diet. Although the notion of 'fertility goddess' has been extensively overused in the study of ancient religions, in Demeter's case the phrase has some degree of justification. At the Thesmophoria, her main and widespread festival, the women of a given community practised an annual autumn (i.e. pre-sowing-time) rite whose explicit function was to promote the success of the following year's harvest: piglets were thrown into pits, their decayed remains were recovered and placed upon altars, and 'it is believed,' (reports the ancient commentator who is our chief source) 'that anyone who takes of this and scatters it with seed will have a good harvest.'

> 作为与谷物相关的女神，得墨忒尔象征着构成希腊人主食——面包的来源。尽管“丰饶女神”这一概念在古代宗教研究中被过度使用，但在得墨忒尔的情况下，这一说法在某种程度上是合理的。在她的主要且广泛流传的节日忒斯摩福里亚节上，某一社区的妇女们每年秋季（即播种前）举行一项仪式，其明确目的是促进来年的丰收：小猪被扔进坑中，腐烂的遗骸被收回并放置在祭坛上。古代评论家（我们的主要史料来源）报告称：“人们相信，谁若取用这些遗骸并将其与种子一起撒播，就会获得丰收。”

![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_148_Picture_0.jpeg)

*Regal authority combined with power over vegetative growth: these are the impressions of Demeter created by the painter of this Corinthian plate (5th century BC). In the goddess' right hand is a torch, in her left, poppies and corn ears; in front of her – perhaps on an altar – there is a pomegranate*.

> 王权与对植物生长的掌控：这是这位科林斯陶盘（公元前5世纪）画师笔下对德墨忒尔女神的印象。在女神右手中握着火炬，左手中则握着罂粟花和玉米穗；在她面前——或许置于祭坛上——有一颗石榴。

But Demeter is concerned with more than corn and fertility. She is also a mother – in fact, arguably the most powerful image of a mother in the whole of Greek mythology. One myth above all expresses the essence of how the goddess was imagined by the Greeks: the tale of her reaction to the abduction of her daughter Persephone by Hades, god of the Underworld. In the *Homeric Hymn to Demeter*, a poem that probably dates from the Archaic period, we have a rich narrative of Demeter's grief and its partial assuaging. After wandering the world in search of her daughter, she comes at last to Eleusis, between Athens and Megara. Disguised as an old woman, Demeter receives hospitality from King Keleos and his family. She is even granted the privilege of nursing Demophon, the baby son of Keleos and his wife Metaneira. In gratitude for her treatment, Demeter secretly sets about conferring immortality on Demophon by anointing him with the divine substance ambrosia and holding him in the hearth so as to purge his mortality by fire. In the logic of Greek myth, however, the boundary between god and mortal is hard to cross. Discovered in the act by the horrified and uncomprehending Metaneira, Demeter reveals her divine identity and commands the Eleusinians to build her a

> 但德墨忒尔所关注的远不止谷物和丰饶。她也是一位母亲——事实上，可能在整个希腊神话中，她是最强大的母亲形象之一。其中一则神话尤其表达了希腊人如何想象这位女神的本质：她对女儿珀耳塞福涅被冥界之神哈迪斯绑架的反应。在一首可能创作于古希腊时期、名为《德墨忒尔颂歌》的诗中，我们读到了一个关于德墨忒尔悲痛情绪及其部分缓解的丰富叙述。在四处寻找女儿的旅程之后，她最终抵达了雅典与麦加拉之间的埃莱夫西斯。她伪装成一位老妇人，受到国王凯罗斯及其家人的款待。她甚至获得了抚养凯罗斯与妻子梅塔涅拉所生的婴儿儿子德莫丰的特权。为感谢他们的款待，德墨忒尔暗中开始用神酒涂抹德莫丰，并将其置于灶火中，以火焰净化他的凡人之躯，使他获得永生。然而，在希腊神话的逻辑中，神与凡人的界限却难以跨越。梅塔涅拉在惊恐与困惑中发现了这一行为，德墨忒尔随即揭示了自己的神性身份，并命令埃莱夫西斯人建造她的……

great temple. When Demeter, still grieving for her daughter, withdraws into this shrine, all fertility disappears from the earth along with her. It is left to Zeus to avert disaster by requiring Hades to restore Persephone to her mother, though for only a part of the year: since the god of death has induced his bride to eat the seed of a pomegranate, she is bound to return to him each year for four months (six, in some versions), when fertility once more abandons the earth.

> 宏伟的神庙。当德墨忒尔仍在为女儿哀悼时，她退入这座神庙，大地上的所有丰饶随之消失。唯有宙斯能够通过迫使哈迪斯将珀耳塞福涅送回她的母亲身边，才能避免灾难，尽管只能持续一年中的部分时间：由于死神诱使他的新娘吃了石榴籽，她每年必须返回他身边四个月（某些版本中为六个月），届时大地再次失去丰饶。

#### 1.1.5 <span id="page-149-0"></span>**Apollo**

> 5.2.1.5 阿波罗

The embodiment of youthful masculine beauty, Apollo (son of Zeus and Leto) has often been taken to represent 'the Greek ideal'. Yet he is simply *a*, not *the*, Greek god. Like all the other divinities he has his own specific provinces of activity which differentiate him from his fellow Olympians. He is especially linked with music, healing, purification and prophecy; in later Greek thought he is also associated with the Sun.

> 阿波罗（宙斯和勒托之子）象征着青春男性之美，常被视作“希腊理想”的化身。然而，他仅仅是众多希腊神祇中的一位，而非唯一的希腊神。如同其他神祇一样，他也有自己特定的活动领域，这使他与其他奥林匹斯神祇区分开来。他尤其与音乐、治疗、净化和预言相关联；在后来的希腊思想中，他也与太阳联系在一起。

Two locations form the centres for Apolline cult and its related mythology: Delos and Delphi. The island of Delos, at the hub of the Cyclades, was from at least the 8th century BC the site of cults to Apollo, his sister Artemis, and their mother Leto; correspondingly, as we have already seen, a myth narrated how, when every other place had rejected Leto's pleas, little Delos alone braved the fury of Hera and accepted the forlorn mother-to-be. Equally strong was Apollo's connection with Delphi, the oracular shrine wonderfully situated just below Mount Parnassos. The oracle's foundation-myth told of Apollo's overcoming of a monstrous snake whose corpse then putrified, thereby lending an epithet to 'Pythian Apollo' and to the 'Pythian Games' held at Delphi in Apollo's honour (*puthein* in Greek means 'to cause something to rot').

> 阿波罗崇拜及其相关神话的中心有两个地点：达洛斯岛和德尔斐。达洛斯岛位于希腊群岛的中心地带，自至少公元前8世纪起便是阿波罗、他的姐姐阿尔忒弥斯以及他们的母亲莱托的崇拜中心；相应地，正如我们之前所见，有一个神话讲述了当时其他地方都拒绝了莱托的恳求，唯有小小的达洛斯岛勇敢地承受了赫拉的怒火，接纳了这位孤苦无助的准母亲。阿波罗与德尔斐的联系同样牢固，德尔斐的预言神殿奇妙地坐落于帕纳索斯山脚下。关于德尔斐神谕神殿起源的神话讲述了阿波罗战胜了一条怪蛇，而那条蛇的尸体随后腐烂，由此赋予了“比提尼亚阿波罗”这一称号，并也赋予了在德尔斐为阿波罗举行的“比提尼亚竞技会”（希腊语“puthein”意为“使某物腐烂”）。

One connecting thread running through many of Apollo's divine powers is harmonious order. This quality is self-evidently present in the god's patronage of music, especially the music of the lyre: Greeks drew a contrast between the soothing Apolline lyre and what were perceived to be the more exotic and ecstatic forms of music associated with Dionysos. (This is one area where the opposition between Apollo and Dionysos, so prominent in the work of the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche (see [pp. 235–36\)](#page-516-0), finds some justification in the ancient sources.) Another kind of harmony is that of a healthy body, and here too Apollo played a role in his capacity as healer, that is, as restorer of the disorder brought about by illness; the god's son Asklepios was the prototypical doctor. A third context in which the restoration of order may operate, according to Greek belief, is that of the purification which redresses a state of religious 'pollution'. The offering of this kind of fresh start was another of Apollo's provinces. Most famously, it was to the Delphic oracle that Orestes came as a polluted outcast after having murdered his mother

> 贯穿阿波罗众多神力中的一个共同线索是和谐的秩序。这种品质显然体现在这位神对音乐的庇护中，尤其是里拉琴音乐：希腊人将舒缓的阿波罗里拉琴音乐与他们认为更异域且充满激情的狄俄尼索斯相关音乐形式进行对比。（在这一方面，阿波罗与狄俄尼索斯之间的对立，正如哲学家弗里德里希·尼采在其著作中所强调的那样，见第235–36页，在古代资料中也找到了一定的依据。）另一种和谐体现在健康的身体上，同样，阿波罗以其作为医者的身份发挥了作用，即恢复疾病带来的混乱；这位神的儿子阿斯克勒庇俄斯则是典型的医生。根据希腊人的信仰，恢复秩序的另一种情境是净化，以纠正宗教上的“污染”状态。提供这种新鲜开始的方式也是阿波罗的职责之一。最著名的是，奥瑞斯忒斯在谋杀母亲后，作为被污染的被放逐者来到德尔斐神谕处寻求净化。

Clytemnestra. Even in defiance of the Furies, who legitimately pursued everyone guilty of shedding kin-blood, Apollo defended Orestes, enabling him ultimately to escape from the mesh of blood-guilt in which he had become entangled (a tale brilliantly staged in the third play of Aischylos' *Oresteia* trilogy).

> 克吕提厄涅斯特拉。即使在埃里尼女神的追捕下，她们合法地追捕所有杀害亲属之人，阿波罗仍为奥列斯特辩护，使他最终得以逃脱自己陷入的血债之网（这一故事在埃斯库罗斯的《奥列斯特亚三部曲》第三部中被精彩地呈现）。

<span id="page-150-0"></span>![](_page_150_Picture_1.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*Temple of Apollo, Delphi. Through the voice of his priestess, the Pythia, Apollo spoke oracles in response to human enquirers. But human minds are fallible: the god's message was always liable to misinterpretation.*

> 阿波罗神庙，德尔斐。通过女祭司皮媞亚的声音，阿波罗向人类询问者传达神谕。但人类的思维是易出错的：神的讯息总是容易被误解。

Since knowledge may be regarded as a form of order, it might be felt that Apollo's patronage of prophecy is one more respect in which the god represents the capacity to control and harmonize experience. However, one of the most insistent themes in the whole of Greek mythology – above all, in the mythology explored in tragedy – is the impossibility for humans to anticipate the future with accuracy. When a mortal – be it Oedipus or anyone else – consults an oracle, the voice of the god is relayed indirectly, through fallible human interpreters. One of Apollo's epithets is 'Loxias', which may be translated as 'he who talks obliquely'.

> 由于知识可以被视为一种秩序的形式，因此人们或许会认为阿波罗对预言的庇护，是这位神祇在另一个方面展现其掌控和协调经验的能力。然而，在整个希腊神话中，有一个最为突出的主题——尤其是悲剧所探讨的神话中——就是人类无法准确预知未来。当一个凡人——无论是俄狄浦斯还是其他人——向神谕求教时，神的声音是通过不可靠的人类解释者间接传达的。阿波罗的一个称号是“洛克斯”（Loxias），其含义可能是“拐弯抹角者”。

<span id="page-151-0"></span>![](_page_151_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The text you provided does not include the actual content to be translated. Please provide the English text you'd like translated into Chinese.

*Apollo, on the east frieze of the Parthenon, Athens (mid-5th century BC). He is flanked by Poseidon and Artemis. This exquisite sculpture helps to explain why the name of Apollo has become synonymous with an idealized male beauty.*

> 阿波罗位于雅典帕特农神庙东侧浮雕（公元前5世纪中叶）。他被波塞冬和阿尔忒弥斯所环绕。这尊精美的雕塑有助于解释为何阿波罗的名字已成为理想化男性美的代名词。

The stress on order and harmony should not be taken to imply that Apollo was somehow benevolent or non-violent in a way that Zeus and Poseidon, say, were not. On the contrary: Apollo's slaying of the Pythian snake typified the manner in which his power might sometimes manifest itself. As Zeus brandished the thunderbolt and Poseidon the trident, so Apollo too held an aggressive weapon as his iconographical signature: a bow, which he was well capable of using to defend his honour and his interests. When his priest Chryses was dishonoured by the Greeks at Troy, the god reacted with lethal fury, striding down from Olympos (according to the *Iliad*) 'like night', and shooting plague-bearing arrows at man and beast alike; the obverse of the capacity to heal was the capacity to destroy with disease. This attack is the more terrifying because it is inflicted from a distance: a group of linguistically related epithets by which Apollo is regularly described may be translated as 'he who shoots from afar'. Whether he acts with benevolence or with aggression, there is always something distant about Apollo.

> 对秩序和和谐的强调不应被理解为意味着阿波罗以某种方式比宙斯和波塞冬更为仁慈或非暴力。相反，阿波罗杀死皮媞亚蛇的行为恰恰体现了他力量可能以何种方式表现出来。正如宙斯挥舞雷霆，波塞冬挥舞三叉戟，阿波罗也以一种具有攻击性的武器作为其形象标志：弓。他完全有能力用这把弓来捍卫自己的荣誉和利益。当他的祭司克吕塞斯在特洛伊被希腊人侮辱时，这位神祇以致命的怒火作出反应，从奥林匹斯山下来（根据《伊利亚特》的描述）“如同黑夜一般”，向人和牲畜 alike 射出携带瘟疫的箭矢；治愈能力的另一面是通过疾病进行毁灭的能力。这次攻击更具威胁性，因为它是从远处施加的：一组语义相关的称号，用来常规描述阿波罗，可翻译为“远射者”。无论他以仁慈还是侵略的方式行动，阿波罗始终带有一种疏离感。

<span id="page-152-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_152_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*The link between Apollo and the taut string could express itself in violence (bow and arrows) or, as here, in the harmony of the lyre. Attic vase,* c. *430–420 BC*.

> 阿波罗与紧绷之弦之间的联系可能以暴力形式展现（如弓箭），或如此处所示，在里拉琴的和谐中体现。雅典陶罐，约公元前430–420年。

### 1.2 **Artemis**

> 5.2.2 阿耳忒弥斯

If we attempt to draw a profile of Apollo's sister Artemis on the basis of her place in religious practice, the closest we come to a unifying schema is the notion of 'transition to adulthood'. For young people of both sexes, sanctuaries of the goddess provided the focus for rites of passage, ranging from the initiation ceremonies for young Spartan men at the shrine of Artemis Orthia, to rituals performed for Artemis by young girls as 'bears' at Brauron in Attica. A related form of transition in which Artemis was deemed to play a role was childbirth: in several places she was identified with the goddess Eileithyia, whose specific province this was. Some scholars press the notion of 'transition' still further by relating it to the tendency for

> 如果我们试图根据她在宗教实践中的地位来勾勒阿波罗妹妹阿尔忒弥斯的形象，那么最接近统一框架的概念就是“成年过渡”。对于男女青年而言，女神的神庙是人生过渡仪式的中心，从斯巴达青年在阿尔忒弥斯·奥尔提亚神庙举行的入伍仪式，到雅典附近的布拉隆地区，少女们以“熊”的身份为阿尔忒弥斯举行的仪式，都属于此类。与之相关的另一种过渡形式是生育，阿尔忒弥斯被认为在其中扮演了角色；在多个地方，她被视作埃莱提亚女神，而生育正是埃莱提亚的专属领域。一些学者进一步强调“过渡”这一概念，将其与生育倾向联系起来。

some of Artemis' sanctuaries to be located in liminal places such as marshes or the sea shore. That would, in turn, chime in with another feature of the symbolism of Artemis' worship, namely 'wildness'. As is hinted at by the imagery of bears, Artemis was regarded as the mistress of wild animals; indeed at the festival of Artemis Laphria in Patrai (mod. Patras, on the north coast of the Peloponnese), a remarkable ceremony took place in which a virgin priestess, riding in a deer-drawn chariot, presided over a holocaust sacrifice of wild beasts.

> 阿尔忒弥斯的一些神庙位于边缘地带，如沼泽或海岸。这在一定程度上与阿尔忒弥斯崇拜象征中的另一个特征相呼应，即“野性”。正如熊的意象所暗示的那样，阿尔忒弥斯被视为野兽之主；事实上，在帕特雷（今帕特拉斯，位于伯罗奔尼撒半岛北海岸）举行的阿尔忒弥斯·拉弗里亚节上，曾举行过一项引人注目的仪式：一位处女祭司乘坐鹿拉战车，主持对野兽进行完全焚烧的献祭。

<span id="page-153-0"></span>The myths in which Artemis figures both reflect and refract these motifs. When Homer in the *Odyssey* seeks to evoke the virginal beauty of the young princess Nausikaa, he compares her to the peerless divine huntress Artemis,

> 在涉及阿尔忒弥斯的神话中，既反映了又折射出这些主题。当荷马在《奥德赛》中试图唤起年轻公主瑙西卡娅纯洁的美貌时，他将她比作无与伦比的狩猎女神阿尔忒弥斯。

who showers arrows, moves on the mountains either along Taygetos or on high-towering Erymanthos, delighting in boars and swift deer…

> 谁射箭如雨，游走于泰格托斯山或巍然高耸的埃里曼托斯山之间，喜食野猪与迅捷的鹿……

Artemis loves the beasts of the wild, yet like her brother Apollo she carries a bow: she will hunt those same beasts, and her aim is unerring. An analogous paradox occurs in the poignant story of Iphigeneia, the virgin daughter of Agamemnon (commander of the Greek expedition against Troy). When Agamemnon offends Artemis – according to one version, he boasts that he is a better hunter than she – the goddess demands the sacrifice of Iphigeneia in recompense. The protectress of young girls will not flinch from demanding their death if her own divine honour is threatened; though in one version of the myth the goddess relents at the last moment, whisking Iphigeneia away to safety and replacing her with a sacrificial deer.

> 阿尔忒弥斯热爱野兽，却像她的兄弟阿波罗一样持弓，她会猎杀那些野兽，箭无虚发。类似悖论在伊菲革涅亚的感人故事中再次出现，她是阿伽门农（希腊远征特洛伊的统帅）的处女女儿。当阿伽门农冒犯阿尔忒弥斯——据一种说法，他自夸自己比她更擅长狩猎——这位女神便要求以献祭伊菲革涅亚作为补偿。作为少女的守护者，她若尊严受到威胁，即使要求少女们献出生命也毫不迟疑；不过在神话的一个版本中，女神在最后一刻心软，将伊菲革涅亚瞬间带走，换上一只祭品鹿。

<span id="page-154-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_154_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The English text to be translated is missing. Please provide the specific content you would like translated into Chinese.

*This Archaic image of Artemis – the handle of a bronze water-jar from Sparta,* c*. 600 BC – characteristically locates her at the centre of a group of wild animals.*

> 这幅阿尔忒弥斯的早期图像——来自斯巴达的青铜水罐把手，约公元前600年——典型地将她置于一群野生动物的中心。

The key notions of transition and adolescence, as well as the connection with animals, find echoes in the recurrent narratives in which Artemis is accompanied by young, unmarried hunters, both male and, in stark contrast to the practice of real life, female. These hunters – males such as Hippolytos, Orion and Aktaion, females such as Kallisto – fall under the patronage of the virgin huntress because they have not, as she has not, made the transition to full sexual life within marriage; socially speaking, they still belong 'in the wild'. However, Artemis' relationship to virginity is far from unambiguous. As is suggested by the fact that one of her roles in cult is as goddess of childbirth, she is by no means hermetically sealed off from sexuality. Her virginity is dangerously fragile: although never actually violated, it is constantly under implied threat.

> 转变与青春期的概念，以及与动物的联系，在艺术作品中反复出现，这些作品中阿尔忒弥斯总是陪伴着年轻的、未婚的猎人，包括男性和与现实生活形成鲜明对比的女性。这些猎人——如希波吕托斯、奥里翁和阿卡提翁等男性，以及卡利斯托等女性——都受到这位处女猎手的庇护，因为他们尚未像她一样，完成从单身到婚姻中的性成熟转变；从社会角度来看，他们仍属于“野生”状态。然而，阿尔忒弥斯与贞洁的关系绝非单一明确。正如她在祭祀中扮演生育女神的角色所暗示的那样，她并非完全隔绝于性欲。她的贞洁极为脆弱：虽然从未真正被侵犯，但始终面临潜在的威胁。

Myths express this fragility in two ways. First, the goddess' young huntercompanions often find their attachment to the group compromised by sexuality. Kallisto, for example, was a devotee of Artemis, but was raped by Zeus (who had deviously taken the form of Artemis); according to one of several versions of her punishment, she was shot by Artemis herself. Another favourite companion was the mighty hunter Orion, but he too fell from grace because of a sexual liaison, either the rape of a maiden or an affair with Eos, goddess of the Dawn; in one version of the story it was Artemis who engineered his death, by sending a giant scorpion to kill him.

> 神话以两种方式表现这种脆弱性。首先，狩猎女神的年轻猎手同伴们往往因性欲而使对群体的依附受到损害。例如，卡利斯托曾是阿尔忒弥斯的虔诚信徒，但却被宙斯（他狡猾地化身为阿尔忒弥斯）强奸。根据她惩罚的多个版本之一，她最终被阿尔忒弥斯本人射杀。另一个受喜爱的同伴是强大的猎手奥里翁，但他同样因性关系而失宠，要么是强奸了一位少女，要么是与黎明女神伊俄斯发生了恋情；在其中一个版本的故事中，正是阿尔忒弥斯策划了他的死亡，她派出一只巨大的蝎子将他杀死。

In yet another variant of the Orion story, Artemis herself was sexually attracted to Orion; this time it was Apollo who intervened to kill the rash transgressor. This points to a second way in which mythology highlights the fragility of Artemis' virginity, with tales in which it is not Artemis' companions, but she herself, who comes uncomfortably close to a sexual liaison. The nature of Aktaion's offence against the goddess is variously reported: either he lusted after her, or, more mildly, he unwittingly came across her and her companion nymphs bathing in a mountain spring. Either way, Aktaion's infringement of Artemis' divine space was enough to condemn him: metamorphosed into a stag, he was ripped apart by his own hounds. The giants Otos and Ephialtes made a similarly fatal mistake by trying to win the hands of Artemis and Hera respectively. It was Artemis who effected their downfall. She turned herself into a deer and ran between them: each hurled a javelin, missing the deer, but killing his fellow giant.

> 在另一个关于俄里翁的传说版本中，阿耳忒弥斯本人对俄里翁产生了性吸引力；这一次，是阿波罗出手干预，杀死了这位鲁莽的冒犯者。这表明神话以另一种方式突出了阿耳忒弥斯处女身的脆弱性，即在一些故事中，不是她的同伴，而是她自己，几乎陷入性关系的尴尬境地。关于俄刻阿诺斯冒犯女神的行为，有不同说法：一种说法是他因性欲而觊觎阿耳忒弥斯，另一种较为温和的说法是他无意中撞见了阿耳忒弥斯和她的伴神女们在山泉中沐浴。无论如何，俄刻阿诺斯侵犯了阿耳忒弥斯的神圣领域，便足以招致他的灭亡：他被变为鹿形，最终被自己的猎犬撕碎。同样地，巨人俄托斯和埃菲阿尔特斯也犯下了致命的错误，他们试图赢得阿耳忒弥斯和赫拉的青睐。阿耳忒弥斯导致了他们的灭亡。她把自己变成一只鹿，在他们之间奔跑：他们各自投掷标枪，未能击中鹿，却杀死了自己的巨人同伴。

<span id="page-156-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_156_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文。

*The death of Aktaion, as Artemis looks on. Aktaion's own hounds tear him apart, believing him to be a stag; the metamorphosis is alluded to through the deerskin slung around his shoulders. Metope from Temple E at Selinus, Sicily,* c*. 460 BC*.

> 阿卡冬之死，阿尔忒弥斯目睹。阿卡冬自己的猎犬撕碎了他，误以为他是一只鹿；他肩头披挂的鹿皮暗示了变形的传说。西西里岛塞利努斯的E庙饰带，约公元前460年。

### 1.3 **Hermes**

> 5.2.3 奎俄斯

The son of Zeus and the nymph Maia, Hermes became quite literally a household word to the Greeks through his characteristic sculpted image, a four-sided pillar with a bust on the top and an erect phallus projecting from the front. Rigid and potent, such 'herms' were found in many types of situation – in the city, in the country, at crossroads – but the most typical location was as a boundary-marker outside the threshold of an ordinary Greek house. When conspirators mutilated the herms in the city of Athens on the eve of the departure of the mighty military expedition to Sicily in 415 BC, the event was held to forbode disaster – not surprisingly since, as sacred

> 作为宙斯和宁芙迈亚之子，赫尔墨斯通过其标志性的雕塑形象，成为希腊人耳熟能详的神祇。这些雕像通常呈现为四面柱形，顶部刻有头像，前面突出的阴茎。它们在城市、乡村和十字路口随处可见，但最典型的安置地点是作为普通希腊房屋门槛外的界标。公元前415年，当阴谋者在远征西西里岛前夕破坏了雅典城中的赫姆斯雕像时，这一事件被认为预示着灾难——这并不令人意外，因为它们是神圣的象征。

markers delimiting one man's territory from another, herms articulated the most basic structure of the city-state.

> 作为划分个人领地的标记，赫姆斯确立了城邦最基本的结构。

Hermes' mythology both contrasts with and complements the iconography of the herm. On the one hand, the mythological Hermes is a god of movement, of travel, of deft alternation between opposed states – quintessentially mobile, as against the herm's fixity. On the other hand, the mythological Hermes is – as the form and location of the herm imply – a god of fertility (especially that of flocks and herds) and a god of boundaries.

> 赫尔墨斯的神话既与赫尔墨斯柱的形象学形成对比，又与其相辅相成。一方面，神话中的赫尔墨斯是一位运动之神，是旅行之神，是能在对立状态间灵活转换的神，本质上具有移动性，与赫尔墨斯柱的固定性形成对照。另一方面，神话中的赫尔墨斯——正如赫尔墨斯柱的形式和位置所暗示的——是一位丰饶之神（尤其与羊群和畜群的繁殖有关），同时也是一位掌管边界的神。

Three episodes from Homeric epic exemplify contexts in which Hermes oscillates between opposites. First, in the final Book of the *Iliad* the disguised god guides Priam, aged king of Troy, from his beleaguered city to the Greek camp, in order to ransom the corpse of his beloved son Hektor; Hermes, that is, operates in 'no man's land'. The second episode occurs in Book 5 of the *Odyssey*, when Hermes flies down from Olympos bearing a message for Kalypso, the goddess who dwells on an island at the end of the earth. Since he is capable of spanning sky and land, mountain and sea, Hermes is the perfect courier. Finally, near the end of the same poem, when those suitors who schemed to usurp Odysseus' home and marry his wife have been slaughtered by the returned hero, then:

> 荷马史诗中的三个片段展示了赫尔墨斯在对立面之间摇摆的情境。首先，在《伊利亚特》的最后一卷中，这位伪装成凡人的神引导特洛伊年迈的国王普里阿摩斯离开被围困的城市，前往希腊营地，以赎回他心爱的儿子赫克托耳的尸体；也就是说，赫尔墨斯此时处于“无人之地”。第二个片段发生在《奥德赛》第五卷，此时赫尔墨斯从奥林匹斯山飞下，向居住在世界尽头岛屿上的女神卡吕普索传达信息。由于他能够跨越天空与大地，山川与海洋，赫尔墨斯是完美的信使。最后，在同部史诗的结尾附近，当那些图谋篡夺奥德修斯家园并娶其妻的求婚者被归来英雄杀死后，便出现了以下情形：

Hermes of Kyllene summoned the souls of the suitors to come forth, and in his hands he was holding the beautiful golden staff, with which he bewitches the eyes of those mortals whom he would bewitch, or wakes again the sleepers. Moving them on with this, he drove them along, and they followed, gibbering.

> 基勒涅的赫尔墨斯召唤了求婚者的灵魂，使他们现身。他手中握着那根美丽的金杖，用它来迷惑那些他想要迷惑的凡人，或唤醒沉睡者。他用这根权杖驱使他们前行，他们便语无伦次地跟随在他身后。

<span id="page-158-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_158_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you'd like translated into Chinese.

*The erection displayed by 'herms' such as this is a marker of power as well as sexuality.*

> 此类“赫尔墨斯雕像”所呈现的阳具，既是权力的象征，也是性欲的标志。

Life and death; sleep and waking; Olympos and the Underworld: Hermes' favourite role is to move between such polarities.

> 生命与死亡；睡眠与觉醒；奥林匹斯与冥界：赫尔墨斯最喜爱的角色是在这些对立面之间穿梭。

This ability has been with him since the very day of his birth on Arcadian Mount Kyllene. As we learn from the delicious *Homeric Hymn to Hermes*, the resourceful newborn, child of Zeus and the nymph Maia, celebrates his arrival into the world with a spot of cattle-rustling; the victim is his own half-brother Apollo. With his mission accomplished, the young god withdraws to his base, passing through the keyhole of his shelter: the ultimate example of controlling a boundary.

> 这种能力自他出生那天起便与他相伴，那是在阿尔卡迪亚的基利尼山上。正如引人入胜的荷马颂歌《赫尔墨斯颂》所讲述的，这位机智的新生儿——宙斯与仙女迈亚之子——在降临人世时便以偷盗牛群的方式庆祝自己的到来；受害者正是他的半兄阿波罗。完成使命后，这位年轻神祇退回到自己的据点，穿过庇护所的钥匙孔——这正是控制边界的终极典范。

Hermes' province, then, is theft, guile, craft. He lacks the capacity for brute force which typifies some of the other Olympians: his trademark symbol, a staff, is not an aggressive weapon. He it is who recommends that Odysseus use the plant *moly* as an

> 因此，赫尔墨斯的领域是盗窃、诡计与技巧。他不具备那些其他奥林匹斯神所具有的暴力能力：他的标志性符号——杖，并不是一种攻击性武器。他建议奥德修斯使用一种名为莫利的植物作为……

antidote in order to circumvent the potions of the enchantress Circe; he it is who, before slaying the many-eyed Argos (deputed by Hera to watch over Zeus' lover Io), first lulls his eyes to sleep one by one, again preferring a trick to unmediated violence.

> 为了绕过女巫喀耳刻的魔药，他准备了解药；正是他，在杀死多眼的阿尔戈斯之前（阿尔戈斯受赫拉指派，负责守护宙斯的情人伊俄），首先逐个让阿尔戈斯的眼睛闭上，再次选择用计谋而非直接暴力。

One last role is worth stressing. In his capacity as messenger and conveyor, Hermes regularly acts as a facilitator for the actions of the other gods. When Hera, Athene and Aphrodite need guiding down to Mount Ida for the Judgment of Paris (see [p. 132\)](#page-287-0), Hermes is the one to take them there; when Persephone returns from the Underworld to rejoin her mother Demeter, who else but Hermes shall guide her steps? But one man's servant is another man's lackey. Prometheus, in Aischylos' *Prometheus Bound*, voices one of the bitterest condemnations of one god by another when he reviles Hermes for doing the bidding of Zeus, the new tyrant in heaven.

> 还有一个角色值得特别强调。作为信使和传递者，赫尔墨斯经常充当其他神祇行动的促进者。当赫拉、雅典娜和阿佛洛狄忒需要前往伊达山进行帕里斯的审判（见第132页）时，是赫尔墨斯带他们前往；当珀耳塞福涅从冥界返回人间，重新与母亲德墨忒尔团聚时，又有谁会引导她的步伐呢？然而，对一个人来说是仆人，对另一个人来说却可能是奴仆。在埃斯奇勒斯的《被缚的普罗米修斯》中，普罗米修斯对另一位神祇的最尖锐谴责，便是当他痛斥赫尔墨斯服从宙斯的命令，而宙斯已成为天国的新暴君。

#### 1.3.1 **Aphrodite**

> 5.2.3.1 娜芙蒂蒂

As a rule it is misleadingly reductivist to ascribe one epithet to a Greek divinity; but in the case of Aphrodite the description 'goddess of sexuality' hits the mark. About her genealogy sources disagree. In Homer's *Iliad* she is the child of Zeus and the goddess Dione, with no mention of the gory Hesiodic tale of her birth from the severed genitals of Ouranos. There are also different versions of her relationship to the other divine embodiment of sexual love, Eros: the Hesiodic account places Eros at the very beginning of the cosmos, with Aphrodite arriving later; but other mythtellers, such as Apollonios of Rhodes, cast Eros as an eternal child, the spoilt and (with his unerring, aphrodisiac arrows) dangerous offspring of Aphrodite herself. Common to all the variants, however, is the perception of Aphrodite as incarnation of *ta aphrodisia*, the sexual act.

> 通常将一个希腊神祇归为单一的称号是过于简化的做法；但在阿佛洛狄忒（Aphrodite）的情况下，“性爱女神”的描述却非常贴切。关于她的家谱，不同来源存在分歧。在荷马的《伊利亚特》中，她被描述为宙斯与女神迪俄涅（Dione）的孩子，而没有提及赫西俄德（Hesiod）那血腥的神话版本，即她诞生于乌拉诺斯（Ouranos）被切断的生殖器。此外，她与另一位代表性爱的神祇厄罗斯（Eros）的关系也有不同版本：赫西俄德的叙述将厄罗斯置于宇宙诞生之初，而阿佛洛狄忒则稍后出现；但其他神话讲述者，如罗德斯的阿波罗尼奥斯（Apollonios of Rhodes），则将厄罗斯描绘为一个永恒的孩子，是阿佛洛狄忒娇惯的、危险的后代（他拥有无往不利、引发性欲的箭矢）。然而，所有这些版本共同之处在于，都将阿佛洛狄忒视为“爱欲行为”（ta aphrodisia）的化身。

This quality dominates Aphrodite's many cults, whether at her major sanctuaries at Paphos and Amathus on the southern coast of Cyprus (whence her regular epithet Kypris, 'The Cyprian') or elsewhere. Amongst her appellations are Porne ('Prostitute') and Hetaira ('Sexual Companion'). But we should not infer that Aphrodite was there by marginalized in relation to civic life. On the contrary, in several localities religious practice stressed her social centrality, for example in the context of marriage cults, or as the embodiment of civic concord in virtue of the epithet Pandemos ('Of the Whole People').

> 这种特质支配着阿佛洛狄特众多的崇拜仪式，无论是她在塞浦路斯南部海岸的主要圣地帕福斯和阿马图斯（因此她常被称为“塞浦路斯的”Kypris）还是其他地方。她的称号包括“Porne”（妓女）和“Hetaira”（性伴侣）。但我们不应推断阿佛洛狄特在公民生活中被边缘化。相反，在许多地方，宗教实践强调了她在社会中的核心地位，例如在婚姻崇拜的背景下，或作为“全民的”（Pandemos）这一称号所体现的公民和睦的化身。

Several myths express the irresistible power which Aphrodite could exercise in the most intimate of emotional settings. The most famous such tale has become known as the Judgment of Paris. When Hera, Athene and Aphrodite quarrelled over who was the most beautiful, they delegated the decision to a mere mortal: Paris, son of King Priam of Troy. With a choice of bribes before him – power from Hera, victory from Athene, and from Aphrodite the love of the world's most lovely woman

> 多个神话表达了阿佛洛狄特在最私密的情感情境中所拥有的无法抗拒的力量。其中最著名的传说被称为“帕里斯的审判”。当赫拉、雅典娜和阿佛洛狄特因谁最美丽而争执不休时，她们将裁决权委托给一个凡人：特洛伊国王普里阿摩斯之子帕里斯。在他面前有三种贿赂选择：赫拉给予的权力，雅典娜给予的胜利，以及阿佛洛狄特给予的对世间最美丽女子的爱。

– the decision was a foregone conclusion: Aphrodite took the prize, and Paris took Helen, thus precipitating the Trojan War.

> 这个决定是板上钉钉的：阿芙洛狄忒赢得了奖品，巴里斯带走了海伦，从而引发了特洛伊战争。

Although married to the lame smith Hephaistos, Aphrodite was notorious for her amorous liaison with Ares: as Demodokos' song in the *Odyssey* recounts, the adulterous pair were once locked in each other's arms when the poor cuckold decided to take his revenge, binding them fast in a net of magical craftsmanship. Not even Zeus was immune to the goddess' charms. Aphrodite's girdle, worn next to her breasts, contained an overwhelming allure which, at a pivotal moment in the *Iliad*, was borrowed by Hera to help her to seduce her husband, so as to divert him from his master-plan on the battlefield.

> 尽管阿佛洛狄忒嫁给了跛足的铁匠赫菲斯托斯，但她仍以与阿瑞斯的风流韵事而臭名昭著：正如《奥德赛》中德莫多科斯的歌声所描述的那样，这对不忠的恋人曾一度纠缠在一起，当可怜的被欺骗者决定报复时，他用魔法工艺编织的网将他们牢牢束缚。就连宙斯也未能免俗，对这位女神的魅力无可抵御。阿佛洛狄忒的腰带紧贴在她的乳房旁，散发着令人难以抗拒的诱惑力。在《伊利亚特》中的一个关键时刻，这件腰带被赫拉借去，以帮助她 seduce 自己的丈夫，从而转移他战场上的主计划。

<span id="page-160-0"></span>But it was not in Zeus' nature to allow himself or the other gods to be manipulated by Aphrodite without riposte. On one occasion he determined to demonstrate that she too could be a victim of desire. As the *Homeric Hymn to Aphrodite* narrates, Zeus made her demean herself by falling in love with a mortal shepherd. His name was Anchises, and he tended his beasts on Mount Ida, near Troy. First the goddess withdrew to her shrine at Paphos, bathing and then anointing herself with perfumes of inexpressible sweetness. Then, disguised as a mortal, she came to Anchises. The fruit of their lovemaking would be Aineias, one of the few Trojans to escape from his city's destruction at the hands of the Greeks. As for Anchises himself, when he awoke to see the goddess in her true form, she warned him never to reveal what had happened. But in myth such prohibitions are made to be broken, and this duly happened: one day Anchises got drunk, and blurted out his secret. Because he had now intolerably diminished Aphrodite's honour, punishment was inevitable. According to different versions, Zeus either blinded or lamed him, so reaffirming the limits which no mortal could transgress with impunity.

> 但宙斯的性格不允许他或其它神明在没有反击的情况下被阿佛洛狄忒操控。有一次，他决定证明她同样也会成为欲望的受害者。正如献给阿佛洛狄忒的荷马颂歌所叙述的那样，宙斯让她堕入爱欲，爱上一位凡人牧羊人。这位牧羊人的名字叫安琪塞斯，他在特洛伊附近的伊达山上放牧。首先，女神退至帕福斯的神殿，沐浴后又用无法形容的甜美香气涂抹自己。随后，她乔装成凡人，前往安琪塞斯处。他们情欲的产物将是阿伊尼亚斯，他是少数在希腊人攻陷特洛伊城时得以幸存的特洛伊人之一。至于安琪塞斯本人，当他醒来发现女神的真身时，她警告他永远不要泄露此事。然而，在神话中，这样的禁令终将被打破，果然有一天，安琪塞斯喝醉了，仓促说出秘密。由于他已不可容忍地损害了阿佛洛狄忒的尊严，惩罚是不可避免的。根据不同版本的传说，宙斯要么让他失明，要么让他瘸腿，以此再次强调凡人不可逾越的界限，否则必将受到惩罚。

#### 1.3.2 **Athene**

> 5.2.3.2 雅典娜

Child of Zeus and Metis ('Crafty Intelligence'), wise, resourceful and valiant, Athene was the virgin warrior who sprang fully armed from the head of Zeus. Her military trademarks were the helmet, the spear, and above all the 'aegis', the tasselled goatskin which she wore about her shoulders; on it was set the grim image of the Gorgon, instiller of panic.

> 宙斯和墨提斯之女（“机智智慧”），睿智、机敏且英勇的雅典娜，是那位从宙斯头颅中全副武装跃出的处女战士。她的军事象征是头盔、长矛，以及最重要的“阿基里斯盾”，那是她披在肩上的装饰着流苏的山羊皮；盾上绘有那恐怖的戈耳戈头像，是惊恐的源头。

Unlike Apollo, who acted 'from afar', Athene was felt to be 'close', especially to heroes whom she favoured. Already in the Homeric poems it was Athene who stood beside Achilles and Odysseus. When Achilles' quarrel with Agamemnon threatened to turn into a deadly duel, thus compromising the Greek war-effort, Athene tugged Achilles by the hair to restrain him. As for Odysseus, Athene repeatedly assisted him on his wanderings back home to Ithaca, by turns praising and chiding the shrewd protégé with whom she shared so many characteristics.

> 与从远处施加影响的阿波罗不同，雅典娜则被认为更为亲近，尤其是对那些她偏爱的英雄们。早在荷马史诗中，就已记载雅典娜站在阿喀琉斯和奥德修斯身旁。当阿喀琉斯与埃阿格摩尼冬的争执几乎演变为致命决斗，从而危及希腊的战争努力时，雅典娜揪住阿喀琉斯的头发以阻止他。至于奥德修斯，雅典娜则多次在他返回伊萨卡的旅途中给予帮助，时而称赞他，时而责备他，因为她与这位聪明的弟子有着诸多共同之处。

<span id="page-161-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_161_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The text to be translated is not provided. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Athene strides into battle, spear and shield at the ready. Coin from Alexandria,* c. *315–310 BC*.

> 雅典娜大步踏入战场，长矛和盾牌已备妥。亚历山大港的铸币，约公元前315–310年。

Athene was 'close' not only to individual heroes but also to communities, in her capacity as Polias ('Goddess of the Citadel'). Sometimes the very fate of a city hung upon her presence: only when her talismanic statue, the Palladion, was stolen from Troy by Diomedes and Odysseus, could the city fall to its besiegers. The principal temples of Argos, Sparta and Lindos (on Rhodes) were dedicated to her, as was, most famously of all, the resplendent Parthenon on the Athenian Acropolis; indeed Athene may have taken her name from the city of Athens. Even if, as some scholars argue, the process of naming was the other way around – with Athens being named after its patron deity – the link between city and goddess was intimate: her sacred bird, the owl, is a typical image on Athenian coinage. As for the aetiology of Athene's connection with Athens, mythology supplied an answer. Once, Athene and

> 雅典娜不仅与个别英雄关系密切，也与社区紧密相连，作为“城邦守护神”（Polias）的身份。有时整个城市的命运都系于她的存在：只有当她的圣物雕像帕拉斯被狄奥墨德斯和奥德修斯从特洛伊盗走后，城市才会落入围攻者之手。阿尔戈斯、斯巴达和林多斯（在罗得岛）的主要神庙都献给她，最著名的莫过于雅典卫城上的辉煌帕特农神庙；事实上，雅典娜可能正是以雅典城命名的。即使一些学者认为命名过程相反——雅典因守护神而得名——城市与女神之间的联系依然紧密：她的圣鸟猫头鹰是雅典钱币上的典型图案。至于雅典娜与雅典之间联系的起源，神话给出了答案。从前，雅典娜和……

Poseidon vied for the role of pre-eminent deity in the city. Whereas Poseidon's trident struck a source of sea-water from the bare rock of the Acropolis, Athene's gift, which duly earned her the victory, was at once more sustaining and more economically useful: a cultivated olive tree.

> 波塞冬争夺雅典主神之位。波塞冬的三叉戟击出雅典卫城光秃秃的岩石中的一处海水源头，而雅典娜的赠礼——一棵种植的橄榄树——则立刻更加滋养且更具经济价值，因此她赢得了胜利。

<span id="page-162-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_162_Picture_1.jpeg)

> Please provide the specific English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*The olive was and is an ever-present feature of the Greek landscape. It was sacred to Athene, and a source of wealth to the city of Athens of which she was patron. To the chorus of Sophokles' play* Oedipus at Colonus*, it was 'a tree not planted by the hands of men, but self-created, a terror to enemy spears.'*

> 橄榄树一直是并仍然是希腊景观中不可或缺的一部分。它对雅典娜圣洁，是她所守护的雅典城的财富源泉。在索福克勒斯戏剧《安提戈涅》的合唱团眼中，它是一棵“并非人力所植，而是自生自长的树，令敌人的长矛畏惧。”

<span id="page-163-0"></span>![](_page_163_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 请提供需要翻译的英文文本，我将为您翻译成中文（希腊神话相关内容将保持专有名词和术语的准确性）。若包含<span>标签，请忽略它们。

*Athene's temple at Lindos, on the island of Rhodes, stands on a sheer cliff above the sea. Built in the Doric style, it dates from the 4th century BC*.

> 位于罗得岛上的林多斯城的雅典娜神庙坐落在海面上方的陡峭悬崖上。该神庙建于多立克式风格，始建于公元前4世纪。

In fact cultivation – the process of bringing the natural into the realm of human productivity – is a key notion for understanding Athene. She watches over the woman who works wool; she is at the elbow of the carpenter who turns raw timber into a seaworthy vessel, such as the *Argo*, or into a one-off creation such as the Trojan Horse; she inspires the trainer of horses and the builder of chariots. The polarity between nature and culture, and the related polarity between raw and cooked – oppositions which many anthropologists have seen as fundamental to the ways in which humans think about the world – are of central relevance to the special powers exercised by the goddess. What shocked her into abandoning her support for one of her favourites, the hero Tydeus, was when she saw him devouring the brains of an enemy raw.

> 事实上，培育——将自然带入人类生产力领域的过程——是理解雅典娜的关键概念。她守护着纺织羊毛的女子；她站在木匠身旁，看着他将原木制成如阿尔戈号船般坚固的船只，或制成如特洛伊木马般独一无二的创造物；她激励着驯马师和战车建造者。自然与文化之间的对立，以及生与熟之间的相关对立——许多人类学家认为这些对立是人类思考世界方式的基础——对于这位女神所拥有的特殊力量具有核心意义。当她看到英雄提丢斯生吃敌人脑浆时，这使她震惊并放弃了对这位宠儿的支持。

For all her willingness to be 'close', her wrath could be devastating. When Arachne boasted that she could outweave Athene, the rash girl underwent metamorphosis into a spider; when the young hunter Teiresias saw the virgin goddess bathing naked, blindness was the penalty she exacted; when, during the sack of Troy, Aias the son of Oileus (often called 'the lesser Aias' to distinguish him from Aias the son of Telamon) raped the prophetess Cassandra at the very altar of Athene, his reckless act prompted the goddess to side with Poseidon in wrecking the Greek fleet on its homeward voyage. The two last-mentioned stories imply a

> 尽管她愿意亲近，但她发怒时却能造成毁灭性的后果。当阿拉克涅自夸能织出比雅典娜更精美的织物时，这个鲁莽的姑娘被变为蜘蛛；当年轻猎人梯利西阿斯看到处女神沐浴时，他失去了视力，这是女神对他的惩罚；当特洛伊被洗劫时，欧利斯之子阿伊亚斯（常被称为“小阿伊亚斯”，以区别于特拉蒙之子阿伊亚斯）在雅典娜神殿的祭坛上强奸了先知喀丝喀。他的鲁莽行为促使女神转而支持波塞冬，摧毁了希腊舰队返回家园的航程。上述最后两个故事暗示了某种……

distancing of the goddess from sexuality, and this is a theme which runs through many of the myths in which she figures. Her virginity remains intact; her divine persona combines masculinity and femininity in an idiosyncratic yet never grotesque way.

> 女神与性的疏离，这一主题贯穿于许多以她为主角的神话之中。她的贞洁始终未被玷污；她的神格以独特却绝不畸形的方式融合了男性与女性的特质。

*When Cassandra, princess of Troy, was raped at Athene's altar by a Greek warrior, Aias the son of Oileus, the goddess' wrath was implacable: she obliterated the Greek fleet, of which Aias' ship was a part, as it sailed victoriously homewards. South Italian water-jar,* c*. 330 BC*.

> 当特洛伊公主喀珊德拉在雅典娜神殿前被希腊战士阿伊亚斯强奸时，女神的怒火无法平息：她摧毁了希腊舰队，其中阿伊亚斯的战船是其一部分，舰队正胜利返航。南意大利水罐，约公元前330年。

One myth which stresses both the goddess' resilient virginity and her closeness to Athens is that which narrates her erotic pursuit by Hephaistos. The lame smith-god had split Zeus' skull open with his axe in order to bring Athene into the world; but his interest in her did not end with her birth. One day, sexually aroused, he pursued her, but it all ended, as things so often did for Hephaistos, in anti-climax: he spilled his semen on her thigh. Athene wiped it off with a bit of wool, which she then cast aside on to the ground. Where it fell, the earth conceived, subsequently bringing forth a child who would be one of Athens' earliest kings: Erechtheus, sometimes

> 一个强调雅典娜女神坚韧不屈的处女之身以及她与雅典紧密联系的神话，讲述了赫菲斯托斯对她的情欲追逐。这位跛足的铁匠之神曾用斧头劈开宙斯的头颅，以将雅典娜带入世间；但对他而言，对她的兴趣并未止步于她的诞生。有一天，他因情欲而追逐她，但正如赫菲斯托斯常有的情形一样，这一切最终以反高潮告终：他的精液洒在了她的大腿上。雅典娜用一小块羊毛将它擦去，然后将那块羊毛丢弃在地面上。精液落在的地方，大地受孕，随后生下了一个孩子，他将成为雅典最早的国王之一：埃雷克忒乌斯，有时……

called Erichthonios. Manifold and steadfast was the bond between the goddess and the city which she so ardently championed.

> 被称为厄里克托尼俄斯。女神与她所热切捍卫的城市之间的纽带既复杂又坚定。

### 1.4 **Dionysos and his followers**

> 5.2.4 狄俄尼索斯及其追随者

No literary portrayal of a Greek divinity equals Euripides' tragedy *Bacchae* for sheer, mesmerizing power. The plot centres on the Theban king Pentheus, who refuses to acknowledge a wonderworking 'Stranger' newly arrived from the east. The entire community is disrupted when the Stranger induces the city's women to abandon their homes and worship a new god, Dionysos, on the nearby mountain of Kithairon. Leading the women are Ino, Autonoe and Agaue (Pentheus' mother), the three sisters of Dionysos' mother Semele; Dionysos has driven them mad because they denied that the father of Semele's child was in fact Zeus himself. The Stranger – none other than Dionysos – inflicts horrible revenge when Agaue tears her son limb from limb in an orgy of uncomprehending violence.

> 没有哪部文学作品能像欧里庇得斯的悲剧《酒神女祭司》那样，以纯粹而迷人的力量描绘希腊神祇。剧情围绕底比斯国王彭忒西勒亚展开，他拒绝承认一位从东方新到的神秘“陌生人”。当这位“陌生人”使城市女性们抛弃家园，转而前往附近的基提翁山崇拜新神狄俄尼索斯时，整个社区陷入混乱。带领这些女性的是因诺、阿托诺埃和阿伽俄（彭忒西勒亚的母亲），这三位是狄俄尼索斯母亲塞墨勒的姐妹；狄俄尼索斯因她们否认塞墨勒之子的父亲正是宙斯本人而使她们发了狂。这位“陌生人”——无他，正是狄俄尼索斯——在阿伽俄因狂乱的暴力行为撕碎自己儿子的肢体时，施以可怕的报复。

Virtually all of Dionysos' principal mythological characteristics are encapsulated in *Bacchae*. The madness which he brings is collective rather than individual, a fact symbolized by the groups of devotees who surround him (in *Bacchae* these are his 'maenads', raving women); he is linked with a state of 'ecstasy', which literally means 'standing outside oneself'; he is the kind of god who, by virtue of his subversiveness, first provokes resistance and then crushes it without remorse; amongst his symbols are the intoxicating grape and the evergreen and burgeoning ivy. He is, in short, inexpressibly delightful but formidably dangerous.

> 狄奥尼索斯的大部分主要神话特征都体现在《酒神女祭司》中。他所引发的疯狂是集体性的而非个人的，这一点通过围绕他的信徒群体（在《酒神女祭司》中称为“狂女”，即发狂的女性）得到象征；他与一种“狂喜”状态相关，字面意思是“脱离自身”；他是那种因具有颠覆性而首先引发反抗，随后又无情镇压的神；他的象征包括令人陶醉的葡萄和常青蔓延的常春藤。简而言之，他既难以言喻地令人愉悦，又令人畏惧地危险。

All these characteristics are depicted in other myths about Dionysos; we shall take each characteristic in turn.

> 所有这些特征都在其他关于狄俄尼索斯的神话中有所体现；我们将逐一分析每个特征。

<span id="page-167-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_167_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese. I will ignore any <span> tags and translate the content directly.

*Dionysos' capacity to identify himself with animal nature sometimes expresses itself as terrifying savagery. Here he dismembers a deer with uncanny ease. Attic vase,* c. *490–470 BC*.

> 狄奥尼索斯能够与动物天性融为一体，这种能力有时会表现为令人恐惧的野性。此处他异常轻松地肢解了一只鹿。雅典陶罐，约公元前490–470年。

<span id="page-168-0"></span>![](_page_168_Picture_0.jpeg)

> The provided text to be translated is missing. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*Two groups of Dionysos' adherents, the maenads and the satyrs, sometimes come into close proximity, thanks to the urgent desires of the satyrs. The Dionysiac atmosphere of this image on an Attic cup (*c*. 490 BC) is evident from the wild animal being wielded by the maenad, and by the characteristic 'wand', the* thyrsos.

> 狄奥尼修斯的追随者分为两组，即酒神女祭司和酒神随从，有时会因酒神随从的强烈欲望而近距离接触。这幅绘于雅典陶杯（约公元前490年）上的图像中，充满酒神氛围，从酒神女祭司所持的野兽以及标志性的圣杖（thyrsos）可见一斑。

*Collective worship*: Two groups of followers attend the god. His female adherents, the maenads, figure in many literary narratives and countless visual representations. A maenad's typical pose – head tossed back in ecstatic abandon – matches the wildness of her garb, with a spotted fawnskin slung around her neck, and sometimes a snake to bind her hair or girdle her dress. Dionysiac closeness to nature is expressed by the frequent depiction of maenads holding wild animals, or even tearing them limb from limb in the rite known as *sparagmos*. Less fearsome than maenads, with a grotesqueness verging on the absurd, were Dionysos' male

> 集体崇拜：两群信徒朝拜这位神明。他的女性追随者——酒神女祭司，在众多文学叙事和无数视觉艺术作品中频繁出现。酒神女祭司典型的姿态——头后仰，陷入狂喜的放纵——与她狂野的服饰相匹配，颈间披着斑点鹿皮，有时用蛇缠绕发丝或束腰。酒神与自然的紧密联系常通过描绘酒神女祭司手持野兽，甚至在名为“肢解”的仪式中将其撕成碎片来表现。相比酒神女祭司，酒神的男性追随者则更不具威胁性，甚至带有荒谬的怪诞感。

devotees, the satyrs. Their form, unlike that of the maenads, physically incorporated a closeness to the animal world, since their predominant anthropomorphism was enlivened by traits belonging to horses or (especially in representations from later antiquity) goats. They habitually pursued nymphs or maenads, but their advances were often rebuffed – perhaps unsurprisingly, as their uneven mixture of partial baldness and exaggerated hairiness may not have been to everyone's taste. Ugliest of all was Silenos, the old man who was said to be their leader, and whose nearpermanent inebriation symbolized the satyrs' addiction to one of the central Dionysiac pleasures.

> 信徒们，即萨提尔。他们的外形与狂女不同，身体上体现出对动物世界的亲近，因为他们的主要拟人化特征被马或（尤其是在后来的古代艺术表现中）山羊的特征所增强。他们通常追逐宁芙或狂女，但他们的追求往往遭到拒绝——也许并不令人意外，因为他们那不协调的秃顶与过度的毛发可能并不讨人喜欢。最丑陋的要数西伦，这位据说领导萨提尔的老人，他几乎永久的醉酒状态象征着萨提尔们对酒神愉悦之一的沉溺。

<span id="page-169-0"></span>![](_page_169_Picture_1.jpeg)

> The context is about Greek mythology. Please provide the English text you would like translated into Chinese.

*The satyr on this intaglio from an agate scarab (*c. *530 BC) seems to have drained not just the cup but the bowl as well. Satyrs shared with Dionysos his link with intoxication, but not his physical attractiveness.*

> 这枚绿松石甲虫上的浮雕中，半人半羊的萨提尔似乎不仅喝干了杯子，还喝干了碗。萨提尔与酒神狄俄尼索斯共享着对醉酒的联系，但并非他的俊美外表。

*Standing outside:* Dionysiac ecstasy is mirrored in several tales which present the god as coming from 'outside'. This is apparently not a matter of historical memory (from Linear B tablets we have evidence for the presence of the god's cult in Greece at least as far back as the 13th century BC). Rather, it is a question of the god's alienness being symbolically represented as a 'belonging outside'. Thus it was that, according to mythology, Dionysos was driven mad by the jealous Hera (since he was

> 站在外部：狄奥尼索斯狂热在多个故事中体现，这些故事将这位神祇描绘成来自“外部”。显然，这并非历史记忆的问题（从线形文字B泥板中我们已有证据表明，这位神祇的崇拜至少在公元前13世纪就已经存在于希腊）。相反，这涉及的是神祇异质性被象征性地表现为“来自外部”。因此，根据神话传说，狄奥尼索斯因赫拉的嫉妒而发疯（由于他）

the product of Zeus' adultery with Semele), and then wandered through a variety of non-Greek lands, from Egypt to India, until he came back at length to Greece.

> （宙斯与塞墨勒私通所生之子），随后漫游过各种非希腊地区，从埃及到印度，直到最终回到希腊。

*Resistance and its overcoming:* Several myths recount the resistance which Dionysos provoked. The daughters of Proitos, king of Tiryns, were driven out of their minds and homes when they failed to acknowledge Dionysos. Worse still was the fate of one of the daughters of Minyas, founder-ruler of Orchomenos. She and her sisters preferred weaving in honour of Athene to worshipping Dionysos; just like Agaue, she tore her own son to pieces. As for the Thracian king Lykourgos, his persecution of Dionysos led to (depending on the variant) madness, blinding, or rending by wild beasts.

> 抵抗及其克服：多个神话讲述了狄俄尼索斯所引发的抵抗。当特里纳克斯国王普罗托斯的子女们未能认出狄俄尼索斯时，她们便失去了理智与家园。更糟糕的是，奥尔霍墨诺斯城邦的创建者兼统治者米尼亚斯的一位女儿的命运。她与姐妹们更愿意为雅典娜编织祭品，而不愿崇拜狄俄尼索斯；正如艾加一样，她也将自己的儿子撕碎。至于色雷斯国王利科斯，他迫害狄俄尼索斯导致了（根据不同版本）疯狂、失明，或被野兽撕碎的结局。

*Dionysiac vegetation:* The intoxicating vine and the irrepressible ivy are the Dionysiac plants *par excellence.* The gift of the vine cut both ways: when the god bestowed it upon the Attic peasant Ikarios, the man's neighbours, with whom he had shared the unfamiliar liquid, thought he had poisoned them instead of just making them tipsy – and they murdered him. Where vine and ivy are present together, Dionysos is present too. Some Etruscan pirates, having rashly taken the god captive, found their ship sprouting with the tendrils of these two plants. Astonished – and terrified by the wild beasts which also manifested themselves on deck – the hapless pirates dived overboard, miraculously changing into dolphins as they did so.

> 酒神植物：令人陶醉的葡萄藤和难以抑制的常春藤是最典型的酒神植物。葡萄藤的馈赠具有双重效果：当神明将它赐予雅典农夫伊卡罗斯时，那些与他分享过这种陌生液体的邻居们，误以为他下毒而非仅仅让他们微醺，于是将他杀害。葡萄藤与常春藤共存之处，酒神亦在其中。一些伊特鲁里亚海盗鲁莽地将酒神俘虏，结果发现他们的船长出了这两种植物的藤蔓。他们惊恐万分，更因甲板上也显现的野兽而感到恐惧，最终绝望地跳入海中，奇迹般地变成了海豚。

*Danger and delight:* Many Dionysiac myths speak of the god's dangerousness: humans crossed him at their peril. But his presence also brought sweetness and comfort. When Theseus carried off the Cretan princess Ariadne, he abandoned her on the island of Naxos. Borne on a chariot drawn by exotic wild beasts, Dionysos arrived to rescue her, and to give rise to one of the most spectacular and gracious of all surviving ancient artworks, the Derveni *krater*, on which the god and his new bride recline in luxuriant splendour.

> 危险与愉悦：许多酒神狄俄尼索斯的神话都讲述了这位神祇的危险性：凡人冒犯他将招致危险。但他的出现也带来了甜蜜与安慰。当忒修斯劫走克里特公主阿里阿德涅后，却将她遗弃在纳克索斯岛。狄俄尼索斯乘着由珍奇野兽拉的战车前来解救她，并由此催生了现存最壮观、最优雅的古代艺术品之一——德尔本尼酒瓮。酒瓮上，神祇与他的新妻在富丽堂皇中悠然自得地倚卧。

<span id="page-171-0"></span>![](_page_171_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 

*The interior of Exekias' famous cup (*c. *530 BC) depicts Dionysos' transformation of pirates into dolphins, as punishment for kidnapping him. The scene is a virtuoso demonstration of metamorphosis: the mast becomes a vine, while the hull assumes the form of a dolphin.*

> 埃克西亚斯著名杯子的内壁描绘了狄奥尼索斯将劫持他的海盗变为海豚的情景，以此作为对他们绑架他的惩罚。这一场景是变形技艺的精湛展示：桅杆化为藤蔓，而船体则呈现出海豚的形态。

<span id="page-172-1"></span>![](_page_172_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 

*One of the glories of the Thessaloniki Museum, the large (height 91 cm/36 in) bronze* krater *(mixing bowl) from nearby Derveni gleams with idealized mythological figures. Watched over by two relief heads of Herakles decorating the volutes, the imagery on the body of the vase has as its focus the union of Dionysos and his bride Ariadne. (See ill. on [p. 66–](#page-133-1)[67](#page-133-2).)*

> 塞萨洛尼基博物馆的珍宝之一是来自附近德韦尼的大型（高91厘米/36英寸）青铜混合碗（krater），其上理想化的神话人物熠熠生辉。碗身装饰着守护其螺旋形纹样的赫拉克勒斯雕塑浮雕头像，主体图案聚焦于狄俄尼索斯与他的新娘阿里阿德涅的结合。（见第66–67页插图。）

#### 1.4.1 <span id="page-172-0"></span>**Ares and Hephaistos**

> 5.2.4.1 阿瑞斯和赫菲斯托斯

Two other gods, though quite different from each other in their spheres of influence, shared the indignity of being regularly disparaged by their fellow divinities. Ares was disliked because of what he stood for: the unrestrained cruelty of war. His own father Zeus called him, in the *Iliad*, 'most hateful to me of all the gods who hold Olympos'. Though usually regarded as equivalent to the Roman god Mars, Ares was far less central to the Greek pantheon than Mars was at Rome (see [p. 218](#page-475-0)). He was said to come from Thrace – which was, in the eyes of some Greeks from elsewhere,

> 另外两位神祇，尽管他们在各自影响的领域截然不同，却同样遭受其他神祇的经常性贬低。阿瑞斯因他所象征的战争中毫无节制的残酷而遭到憎恶。他的父亲宙斯在《伊利亚特》中称他为“奥林匹斯众神中我最憎恶的神”。虽然通常认为阿瑞斯相当于罗马的战神马尔斯，但阿瑞斯在希腊神谱中的地位远不如马尔斯在罗马的地位重要（见第218页）。据说他起源于色雷斯——在一些其他希腊人看来，色雷斯是……

a barely half-civilized place. It was to Thrace that he withdrew after making adulterous love to Aphrodite behind the back of her husband Hephaistos. The adulterers met on a regular basis, producing four children: Phobos ('Fear'), Deimos ('Terror'), Harmonia ('Harmony') and (according to a variant different from that preserved by Hesiod) Eros ('Sexual Love'). The first two inherited their father's traits, the second two their mother's – as if the contrast between the parents was so stark that their offspring could not be a blend of each.

> 一个几乎半开化的地方。他在与爱神阿佛洛狄忒私通、背着她丈夫赫菲斯托斯的情况下退避到了色雷斯。这对通奸者定期幽会，生下了四个孩子：恐惧之神福波斯（Phobos）、恐怖之神德莫斯（Deimos）、和谐女神哈莫尼亚（Harmonia），以及（与赫西俄德记载的版本不同）爱神厄罗斯（Eros，即性爱）。前两个孩子继承了父亲的特质，后两个则继承了母亲的特质——仿佛父母之间的对比如此鲜明，以至于他们的后代无法融合双方的特质。

<span id="page-174-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_174_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 

*As always, Ares is intent upon the activity with which he is synonymous in the Greek language: warfare. Here, fully armed, he is athletically poised for action. The 'François Vase',* c. *570 BC*.

> 如同往常一样，阿瑞斯始终专注于与他同义的活动：战争。他全副武装，以运动员的姿态准备战斗。《弗朗索瓦花瓶》，约公元前570年。

Hephaistos, the divine smith, was, like Ares, marked as an outsider: doubly so. First, he was associated with the north Aegean island of Lemnos, populated (as inscriptional evidence suggests) by non-Greek-speakers until well into the Archaic period. Secondly, he was lame. According to one version, Hera bore him alone, apart from Zeus; but – since, as so often in the logic of mythological genealogy, an unorthodox method of conception produced an imperfect offspring – she hurled him out of Olympos in disgust. His revenge was intricate rather than violent, for he sent her a golden throne which held her with invisible bindings. In the end Dionysos made him drunk and led him back up to Olympos on a donkey; after that, he agreed to free his mother.

> 赫菲斯托斯，众神的铁匠，如同阿瑞斯一样，被视为局外人：更为如此。首先，他与北爱琴海的莱姆诺斯岛有关联，该岛（根据碑铭证据）直到古典时期初期仍由非希腊语使用者居住。其次，他跛足。据一种说法，赫拉独自孕育了他，而非宙斯；但——正如神话家谱逻辑中常有的情况一样，非传统受孕方式导致了不完美的后代——她因愤怒而将他从奥林匹斯山驱逐出去。他的复仇既复杂又不具暴力性，因为他送给她一把黄金宝座，用无形的锁链将她束缚。最终，狄俄尼索斯让他喝醉，并骑着驴子将他带回奥林匹斯山；之后，他同意释放自己的母亲。

The theme of 'casting out from Olympos' recurs a second time. When Zeus took his quarrel with Hera to terrifying lengths by hanging her from the mountain with anvils weighting her feet, Hephaistos sided with his mother. For his pains, Zeus hurled him as far as the smith's 'own' island of Lemnos. Even so, he gamely scaled Olympos once more to rejoin the company of the immortals. Though he continued to be a butt of their rough humour, he earned their respect too, thanks to his unrivalled mastery of the arts of metal and fire.

> “被驱逐出奥林匹斯山”这一主题再次出现。当宙斯将与赫拉的争执推向极端，将她用铁砧压住双脚悬吊在山崖上时，赫菲斯托斯站在母亲一边。作为回报，宙斯将他驱逐到铁匠自己的岛屿——莱姆诺斯岛。然而，他仍然兴高采烈地再次攀登奥林匹斯山，重新加入众神的行列。尽管他依旧成为众神粗暴玩笑的对象，却也因他无人能及的金属与火的技艺赢得了他们的敬重。

<span id="page-175-0"></span>![](books/Greek_Mythology/t/pic/_page_175_Picture_1.jpeg)

> 

*The painter of this vase (*c. *560 BC) has depicted a juvenile-looking Hephaistos riding on an ithyphallic donkey. Off-picture to the right is Dionysos, who is leading Hephaistos back up to Olympos. At the top left is a jaunty satyr.*

> 这只陶罐（约公元前560年）的画家描绘了一位显得年幼的赫菲斯托斯，他骑在一头阳具象征的驴背上。画面右侧外是狄俄尼索斯，他正引导赫菲斯托斯返回奥林匹斯山。画面左上角则是一位活泼的萨提尔。

#### 1.4.2 **Lesser divinities: Hekate, Hestia, Pan**

> 5.2.4.2 次要神祇：厄刻特拉、赫斯提亚、皮阿尼克斯

**翻译思路：**
1. **术语准确性**：希腊神话中"lesser divinities"译为"次要神祇"，既体现其地位次于主神，又符合学术文本的规范表达。三位神祇的中文译名均采用通行译法——厄刻特拉（Hekate）、赫斯提亚（Hestia）、皮阿尼克斯（Pan），确保译文的权威性与可读性。
2. **结构规范**：保留原文的层级编号"5.2.4.2"，通过冒号分隔主标题与子项，符合中文学术文本的格式要求。
3. **文化适配**：在不改变原意的前提下，通过"次要神祇"的表述，既保留了"lesser"的等级含义，又避免了直译可能引发的歧义，使译文更贴合中文语境。

Hekate's role in mythological narrative is relatively modest. Her links with the sinister powers of the Underworld are exemplified in a memorable scene from Apollonios' *Argonautica*, when Jason, on the advice of Hekate's priestess Medea, summons the goddess to lend him her aid. Hekate's associations with night, with

> Hekate在神话叙事中的角色相对低调。她与冥界阴暗力量的关联，在阿波罗尼奥斯《阿尔戈纳骑士》中一段令人难忘的场景中得到体现：当雅森在Hekate女祭司梅德亚的建议下，召唤女神以赐予他援助时，这一场景生动展现了她的角色。Hekate与夜晚的关联，以及...

**翻译思路：**
1. **术语准确性**：希腊神话专有名词（如Hekate、Apollonios、Argonautica、Medea）采用通行译名，确保学术规范性。
2. **句式调整**：原文长句拆分为短句，如将"Her links...aid"拆分为两部分，以符合中文表达习惯。
3. **语义精准性**："sinister powers"译为"阴暗力量"，既保留原词"sinister"的负面含义，又避免直译导致的歧义。
4. **学术严谨性**：使用"叙事""关联""体现"等学术词汇，同时通过"令人难忘的场景"等表述增强可读性。
5. **逻辑衔接**：通过"以及"的省略（原文未完）暗示后续内容，保持段落完整性。

howling dogs, and with the no man's land of crossroads, all stress the infernal and uncanny nature of her divine persona. Yet, as usual, it is unwise to generalize too confidently about what 'the Greeks' thought about a given religious phenomenon. In his *Theogony*, Hesiod utters a remarkable, almost personal paean to Hekate, praising her in lavish terms as helpmeet of kings, granter of victory, and bringer of prosperity. In our picture of Greek mythology, there must always be room for local and even idiosyncratic variations.

> 嚎叫的狗与无主之地的十字路口，皆强调了她神性形象中冥界与诡谲的特质。然而，正如往常一样，我们不宜过于自信地概括古希腊人对某一宗教现象的整体认知。在《神谱》中，赫西俄德以罕见的个人化赞歌向赫卡忒致意，盛赞她为君王的助手、赐予胜利者与繁荣的女神。在我们对希腊神话的描绘中，始终应为地方性乃至个人化的差异保留空间。

（翻译思路：在保持学术严谨性的前提下，采用意译与直译相结合的方式。对于"howling dogs"等意象性表述，采用直译以保留原文的文学色彩；"no man's land of crossroads"译为"无主之地的十字路口"既保留原意又符合中文表达习惯。专有名词如"Theogony"译为《神谱》遵循通行译法。针对"local and even idiosyncratic variations"的表述，采用"地方性乃至个人化的差异"的递进式翻译，既体现学术性又增强可读性。通过调整长句结构，使译文在保持原文逻辑的同时更符合中文表达习惯。）

Equally modest in her mythological role is Hestia, goddess of the hearth. In fact, '*of* the hearth' is misleading – she *is* the hearth, the sacred fire at the focus of every home and every community. (The ancient Greek language knows no distinction between 'capital' and 'small' letters: the word *hestia* means both 'hearth' and 'the goddess Hestia'.) She is a virgin: the sexual act would have polluted the purity of her fiery essence. Her role is to be central and fixed; indeed the French scholar Jean-Pierre Vernant has convincingly identified a significant contrast-cumcomplementarity between Hestia and Hermes, the god of mobility and exchange. But another significant contrast is with Hephaistos. While he represents fire in its dynamic aspect – productively, as in the forge, or aggressively, as in the elemental conflict between himself and a river-god in Book 21 of the *Iliad* – Hestia is the undying focus, the element necessary for the act of sacrifice. In this she partly resembles her Roman 'equivalent' Vesta, though Hestia lacks the social prominence lent to Vesta by her guardians at Rome, the Vestal Virgins (see [p. 218](#page-476-0)); as often, the notion of Greek and Roman 'equivalent' deities can be more misleading than helpful.

> 赫斯提亚在神话中的角色同样低调。她是灶火之神。实际上，"灶火"这一表述容易产生误导——她本身就是灶火，是每个家庭和每个社群核心的神圣之火。（古希腊语中不存在大小写之分：词"hestia"既指"灶火"，也指"赫斯提亚女神"。）她是一位处女神：性行为会污染她炽热本质的纯洁性。她的角色是居中且恒定的；事实上，法国学者让-皮埃尔·维纳南（Jean-Pierre Vernant）已经令人信服地指出，赫斯提亚与掌管流动性和交换的赫尔墨斯之间存在显著的对比与互补性。但另一个重要对比则是与赫菲斯托斯。虽然赫菲斯托斯代表火的动态表现——既体现在锻造中的创造性（如《伊利亚特》第21卷中他与河神的元素冲突），也体现在其攻击性——赫斯提亚则是永恒不灭的核心，是祭祀行为中不可或缺的元素。在这方面，她部分地与罗马的"对应神"维斯塔（Vesta）相似，但赫斯提亚缺乏罗马贞女（Vestal Virgins）赋予维斯塔的社会地位（参见第218页）；正如常常发生的情况，希腊与罗马"对应神祇"的概念往往比帮助性更易产生误导。

（翻译思路：1. 严格遵循学术文本规范，如专有名词"赫斯提亚"、"维纳南"等采用标准译名；2. 保留原文的对比结构，通过"虽然...但"等句式体现逻辑关系；3. 对文化特定概念如"贞女"进行必要注释；4. 保留原文的注释格式"参见第218页"；5. 在保持学术严谨性的同时，通过"既...也..."等表达增强可读性。）

Implicit in the nature of Greek divinity is the capacity to transcend anthropomorphism. For many of the gods, this capacity manifests itself in selftransformation, for example into the shape of an animal. But with Pan, this mixing of human and animal form constitutes, as in the case of satyrs, not a fleeting metamorphosis but a permanent, hybrid condition: part human, part goat. His promiscuity is all-embracing: no nymph is safe from him, but shepherd boys, and even animals from the flock, need to keep a wary eye too. Pan roams the solitary wilds between communities; Arcadia, regarded by Greeks from other regions as rough and pre-civilized, is the area most associated with him, but his worship spread throughout the Greek world. Indeed his name ('All') was sometimes taken to betoken a universal divine force. However, his typical image is more modest in scope: horned, goat-footed, and, in the brief interludes between sexual escapades, peacefully occupied in playing the reed pipes.

> 希腊神祇的本质中隐含着超越拟人化的可能性。对于许多神祇而言，这种能力体现为自我转变，例如化为动物的形态。但对潘而言，这种人与动物形态的混合，正如萨提尔的情况一样，并非短暂的变形，而是一种永久的混合状态：一半人类，一半山羊。他的放荡不羁无所不包：没有宁芙能逃脱他的纠缠，牧羊少年乃至羊群中的动物也需保持警惕。潘游荡于各社区之间的孤寂荒野；阿尔卡狄亚，被来自其他地区的希腊人视为粗犷而未开化的地区，是与他最为关联的区域，但他的崇拜却蔓延至整个希腊世界。确实，他的名字（“所有”）有时被用来象征一种普遍的神力。然而，他的典型形象在范围上更为谦逊：长角、山羊足，而在短暂的性爱间歇中，他则平静地吹奏芦笛。

**翻译思路：**  
1. **术语准确性**：希腊神话中的专有名词（如Pan、satyrs、nymphs）采用通用译名，确保学术严谨性。  
2. **文化语境适配**：如“Arcadia”译为“阿尔卡狄亚”，保留其在希腊文化中的地理与象征意义。  
3. **句式调整**：将长句拆分为更符合中文表达习惯的短句，同时保留原文逻辑关系。  
4. **学术性与可读性平衡**：使用“拟人化”“放荡不羁”等简洁而精准的词汇，避免直译导致的生硬感。  
5. **隐喻处理**：如“his name ('All')”译为“名字（‘所有’）”，既保留原意又符合中文语境。

<span id="page-177-0"></span>![](_page_177_Picture_0.jpeg)

> 

*To Greeks and Romans alike Pan was a god of the 'outside': unnerving and potentially dangerous. Anyone who came across the god unawares might be afflicted with 'panic'. Pan's closest associates were the nymphs, with whom he shared many cults, especially in caves. Roman Imperial gold statuette.*

> 对于希腊人和罗马人来说，潘（Pan）是一位“野外之神”：他令人不安且可能具有危险性。任何在不知情的情况下遇到这位神明的人，都可能遭受“恐慌”（panic）的侵扰。潘最亲近的伙伴是仙女（nymphs），他与她们共同拥有许多祭祀仪式，尤其在洞穴中。罗马帝国金质雕像。  

**翻译思路**  
1. **文化语境适配**：将“outside”译为“野外之神”，既保留原意，又符合中文对自然神祇的表述习惯，同时通过引号强调其象征意义。  
2. **术语准确性**：  
   - “panic”作为专有名词，保留英文原词并加引号，同时在括号内补充中文解释“恐慌”，以兼顾学术严谨性与可读性。  
   - “nymphs”译为“仙女”，符合中文神话体系中对自然精灵的常见译法。  
   - “cults”译为“祭祀仪式”，避免直译为“教派”可能引发的歧义，更贴合古代宗教实践的语境。  
3. **句式调整**：将“Roman Imperial gold statuette”整合为“罗马帝国金质雕像”，通过“金质”明确材质，同时“雕像”比“像”更符合中文对雕像的普遍称呼。  
4. **逻辑衔接**：通过分句和连接词（如“尤其”）增强段落的连贯性，使读者更清晰地理解潘神的特性及其与自然环境的关联。

### 1.5 *Jason Summons Hekate*

> 5.2.5 伊阿宋召唤赫卡忒

**翻译思路**：  
1. **专有名词处理**：  
   - "Jason" 译为 "伊阿宋"，这是希腊英雄伊阿宋（Jason）的标准中文译名，符合学术文本中对古希腊人物的规范译法。  
   - "Hekate" 译为 "赫卡忒"，该神祇在中文文献中通常以该音译名称出现，无需额外添加“女神”等尊称，因其在神话语境中已默认为神祇。  

2. **动词选择**：  
   - "Summons" 译为 "召唤"，这一词汇在神话叙事中常用于描述人神互动（如祈求、请示或召唤神灵），既符合原文的宗教仪式感，又保持了学术文本的严谨性。  

3. **结构保留**：  
   - 保留原文的章节编号 "5.2.5"，确保与上下文的逻辑衔接，同时符合学术著作的格式规范。  

4. **简洁性与可读性平衡**：  
   - 避免冗余表述（如“赫卡忒女神”），以简洁形式呈现核心信息，同时通过上下文暗示神祇的身份，兼顾学术性与可读性。

In the course of his narrative of the Argonautic expedition, Apollonios of Rhodes relates these eerie preliminaries to Jason's contest with the firebreathing bulls. Under instructions from Medea, the hero invokes the help of the dread goddess Hekate:

> 在叙述阿尔戈船英雄远征的过程中，罗德斯的阿波罗尼奥斯描述了杰森与喷火公牛比赛前的诡异前奏。应美狄亚的指示，这位英雄召唤了令人畏惧的女神赫卡忒的帮助。  
**翻译思路**：  
1. **专有名词处理**：  
   - "Apollonios of Rhodes" 译为"罗德斯的阿波罗尼奥斯"，保留古希腊作家的原始称谓并补充地名以符合中文学术惯例。  
   - "Hekate" 译为"赫卡忒"，采用通行的音译名称，同时在首次出现时补充注释（如"令人畏惧的女神"）以明确其神性特征。  
2. **语义准确性**：  
   - "eerie preliminaries" 翻译为"诡异前奏"，既保留原文的神秘氛围，又符合中文表达习惯。  
   - "firebreathing bulls" 译为"喷火公牛"，直接对应神话意象，避免歧义。  
3. **学术严谨性**：  
   - 使用"叙述"而非"讲述"，强调文本的文学性与历史记录属性；  
   - "invoke the help of" 译为"召唤...的帮助"，体现古希腊宗教仪式中"呼请神灵"的特殊语境。  
4. **句式调整**：  
   - 将长句拆分为"在...过程中"和"应...指示"两部分，增强中文表达的逻辑层次感。

In the distance the sun was sinking beneath the dark earth, beyond the furthest peaks of the western Ethiopians, and Night was placing the yoke upon her horses; beside the ship's ropes the heroes prepared their beds. Not Jason, however. As soon as the bright stars of Helike, the Bear, had slipped down, and the air was perfectly still through the heavens, he went to an empty place, like a furtive thief, with everything he needed, which he had prepared in advance during the day. Argos [the son of Phrixos] had fetched him a ewe and milk taken from a flock, but the rest he took from the ship itself. When he found a place set apart from men's paths, open to the skies in the midst of pure water-meadows, he first of all bathed his tender body in the holy river as ritual demanded, and then dressed in the dark robe which Lemnian Hypsipyle once gave to him, to remind him of their sweet love-making. After this he dug a trench a cubit long in the earth and made a heap of cut wood; then he slit the sheep's throat over the pit and stretched its body over the fire in accordance with the rite. He lit the wood by putting in fire at the bottom, and poured out over it a mingled libation, calling upon Brimo Hekate to assist him in the contest. Having summoned her, he retreated. Hearing the call, the dread goddess came from the furthest depths to accept the sacrifices of the son of Aison. Around her head was a garland of terrible snakes entwined with oak-branches, and her torches flashed out a blinding brightness; all around her was the piercing bark of hellish dogs. All the fields trembled at her approach; the marsh-dwelling nymphs of the river who dance around that meadow of the Amarantian Phasis screamed aloud. The son of Aison was seized by fear, but even so he did not turn around as his feet carried him back to find his companions; already early-born Dawn was scattering her light as she rose above the snowy Caucasus.
